observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the prior year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor:

Require the Inventory Committee to (i) prepare a Physical Inventory Plan, containing outline and strategies on how to conduct and complete the physical inventory within the prescribed period; (ii) conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of all PPE owned by the Municipality, identifying the proper classification, individual identification, status of serviceability, accountability and location thereof; (iii) prepare and submit a complete RPCPPE to the Municipal Accountant not later than January 31 of each year, copy furnish the Audit Team; and (iv) prepare and submit IIRUP together with its supporting documents for disposal of unserviceable properties; and

Instruct the Inventory Committee, Appraisal and Disposal Committee, and Municipal Accountant to (i) undertake the necessary actions for the immediate disposal of the identified unserviceable properties of the Municipality to avoid further deterioration and consequent depreciation in its value; and (ii) implement one-time cleansing of PPE accounts and strictly adhere with the provisions of COA Circular 2020-006 to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

prepare the corresponding reports for each of the completed projects and submit the same to the Accountant within one month from completion; and

report to the Municipal Mayor any project/program which cannot be immediately implemented for proper disposition or referral to the Source Agency/ies.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Chairman and members of the Bids and Award Committee strictly comply with the provisions of the 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184, specifically the following:

Undertake procurement only if it is included in the approved Annual Procurement Plan;

Check the submitted Omnibus Sworn Statement of the bidders and ensure that it is compliant with the latest form prescribe in Item 6 of GPPB Circular No. 04-2020; and

Require all winning bidders to submit their DOLE-approved CSHP before approval of their contracts.

Accountant require the suppliers to submit the acceptable form of warranty security for the procurement of goods before payment is made to protect the interest of the Municipality.

Maintain a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and their components, along with a Local Road Inventory and Road Map; and

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

Require the GSO to maintain property cards and update records regarding acquisition, description, custody, disposal, and other information about the PPE, based on the source documents of the transactions, and reconcile with the accounting records and RPCPPE.

Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain PPELCs and update records regarding acquisition, description, custody, estimated useful life, rate of depreciation, disposal, and other information about the PPE, based on the source documents of the transactions, and reconcile with the property records and RPCPPE.

Instruct the Inventory Committee, in coordination with the GSO and Municipal Accountant to:

Conduct the physical count of PPE, and implement the One-Time Cleansing of PPE in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006; 

Undertake the disposal of the unserviceable properties in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006; and

Cause the submission of a complete Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year.
observations.managementAction: We agree with the observations and recommendations presented. As initial procedure, the Municipal Accountant or any other qualified representative shall nominate his attendance to COA Course offering, re: One Time Cleansing of PPE scheduled this year. This shall be helpful in the possible implementation of COA Circular 2020-006. Hopefully after or together with the reconciliation of prior year variances in Financial Statements, the corrective measures in PPE shall also be taken into priority list. Once the guidelines in one time cleansing is observed, all other internal control measures will also be carried- out/ followed moving forward.
observations.reason: Already drafted a Physical Inventory Plan but yet to schedule and finalize the conduct of physical inventory count.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous audit recommendation that earnest effort to implement all the approved PPAs should principally come from the Municipal Mayor being the head of agency identified as the responsible person in the Joint Memorandum Circular from DBM-DOF-DILG.
Likewise, we recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the MPDC to:

Address the issues affecting the delay on the bidding-related activities and ensure that all approved PPAs are procurement and implementation-ready and warrant the implementation of all approved PPAs for the optimal utilization of the appropriation for development projects; and

Continuously adhere to the provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1, dated November 4, 2020 to ensure that maximum attainment of desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcome of the Municipality is achieved and for the realization of the intended purpose of the 20% Development Fund.
observations.managementAction: The office of the Municipal Mayor and MPDC agreed to implement and recommendation and commit to fast track implementation of unimplemented projects of both current and prior years.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

2. Instruct the Inventory Committee to:

a. Formulate a Physical Inventory Plan and schedule for the conduct of physical inventory that will complement with the time of the members of the committee in order not to prejudice the operations of the Municipality. Also, conduct the physical count pursuant to 6.1 and 6.2 of COA Circular No. 2020-006; and

b. Cause the submission of a complete Report of the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year.

3. Require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Trace the differences in the account balances and reconcile her records with the financial statements; 

b. Act on the preparation and maintenance of the PPE ledger cards based on the source documents of the transactions; and

c. Provide the necessary disclosures required in IPSAS 17 on PPE.
observations.managementAction: Agree with the above recommendation. In fact, during the LGU Department Head’s meeting with OIC-MLGOO held last April 4, the re-organization of Inventory Committee Membership was discussed. As agreed, the new Inventory Committee members will set a meeting by April 28 to take up key matters such as designation of Property Custodian, conduct of physical inventory count, among others. Please note that tracing of difference, reconciliation of records, and other subsequent procedures will be conducted once the physical count is completed.
observations.reason: Already drafted a Physical Inventory Plan but yet to schedule and finalize the conduct of physical inventory count.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to request for the write off of the aforementioned accounts in accordance with COA Circular No, 2016-005 supported with an aging of dormant receivables and fund transfer, indicating in the remarks column the existence of the applicable column the existence of the applicable conditions, as follows:

a. Absence of records or documents to validate/support the claim and/or unreconciled reciprocal ac counts
b. Death of the accountable officer/employee/debtor
c. Unknown whereabouts of the accountable officer/employee/debtor, and that he/she could not be located despite diligent efforts to find him/her
d. Exhaustion of all possible remedies by the Management to collect the receivables and to demand liquidation of cash advances and fund transfers
e. No pending case in court involving the subject dormant accounts.

The accuracy and existence of the PPE account with a total carrying amount of P258,152,650.32 is unreliable due to an unreconciled difference of P72,761,392.65 and P152,370,703.81 between General Ledgers and Property Cards, and Report on Physical Count of Property, Plant, and Equipment (RPCPPE) and accounting records, respectively. Moreover, unserviceable PPEs totaling P8,468,853.07 were not disposed of, contrary to Section 119, 120 and 124 of Chapter 7, and Annex 9 of Volume 2 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs), thereby affecting the reliability of the PPE accounts and fair presentation of the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor order the:

GSO-Designate to maintain and update records regarding acquisition, description, custody, disposal, and other information about the PPE, based on the source documents of the transactions, and reconcile with the Accounting Records and RPCPPE.

Municipal Accountant to update the record regarding acquisition, description, custody, estimated life, rate of depreciation, disposal, and other information of PPE in the PPELCs and ensure that the total balance of PPELCs tally with the balances of controlling PPE accounts in General Ledger and the Financial Statements.

Inventory Committee, in coordination with the GSO and Municipal Accountant:

To complete the physical inventory within the prescribed period for one-time cleansing of the PPE accounts.

Prepare a report on the result of the physical count through RPCPPE including list of unserviceable PPEs, missing PPEs, and items found at station.

Any discrepancy between the RPCPPE, PPELCs, and PCs shall be investigated, verified, and adjusted. The total reconciled balances for each PPE account shall be established as the correct balance of PPE at year-end; and;

To undertake the disposition of the non-existing and unserviceable PPEs in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006.

Temporary relief of the Inventory Committee from their daily task to devote their full time in the conduct of the physical inventory taking until the same is completed.

We further recommended the Municipal Mayor to cause the conduct of an investigation to determine person accountable, circumstances of the loss, and the persons responsible for the loss, among others, and demand shall be made from the accountable personnel to produce the item or pay the depreciated replacement cost

observations.recommendation: The uncollected Waterworks System Fees and Rental Fees as of CY 2023 totaling P2,574,542.00 and P253,400.00 respectively, were not recorded due to the delayed submission of monthly Consumer Account Reports for the Waterworks System and Lease Contracts to the Municipal Accountant, contrary to Item 8 of Section 4 of PD No. 1445 and Section 21, Chapter 3 of the New Government Accounting System Manual for Local Government Units, resulting to an understatement of receivables and equity accounts by P2,827,942.00, thereby affecting the fair presentation of the financial statements.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct:

The Tomas Oppus Municipal Waterworks System Administration (TOMWASA) to submit the Monthly Consumer Account Reports indicating therein the consumption and amount due per consumer to the Office of Municipal Accountant. 

The Municipal Treasurer to submit the duly notarized Lease Contract for every tenant of the Commercial Building and Public Market to the Municipal Accountant within 5 days from the notarization and a copy of the cancellation of the contract. 

The Municipal Accountant to record the uncollected Waterworks System Fees and Rental Fees after verifying the accounts of the consumers and tenants.  Set up monthly receivables and recognize income based on the Monthly Consumer Account Reports and Lease Contracts.
observations.reason: The Municipal Treasurer partially submitted the notarized contract to the office of the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: Variations on the approved plans and specifications of the contracted projects namely Completion of Commercial Building B Phase II and Expansion of Legislative Building with contract price of P1,247,357.98 and P499,996.08, respectively, were not covered by an approved Variation Orders; while disparities were noted in the program of work and implementation by Administration of the Repair & Rehabilitation of Commercial Building A, contrary to Section 7 and Annex E of 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184, thus violating the principle of transparency, accountability, and legal compliance to procurement practices to safeguard public resources.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Engineer to ensure that projects are meticulously and judiciously planned, and:

Monitor compliance of the contractor to the terms of the contract and accomplishment based on the approved plans and specifications, and any variation shall be authorized through an approved Variation Order, either a Change Order or Extra Work Order, whichever is applicable.  

Any changes of requirements for projects implemented by Administration shall be reflected in the Annual Procurement Plan to avoid the procurement of unnecessary materials. 

Return to GSO any unused materials for safekeeping and proper accounting. 

We further recommended the Municipal Mayor to establish Constructors Performance Evaluation System (CPES) Implementing Units (IUs) who shall be responsible for evaluating the performance of their contractors using the NEDA-Approved CPES Guidelines during construction and upon completion for the type of project being implemented, regardless of the contract amount and funding source to ensure that the contractor will faithfully perform its obligations under the contract.
observations.reason: Concerned LGU personnel had no knowledge how to establish CPES.

observations.recommendation: Six PPAs for the Peace and Order Programs with a total appropriation of P220,000.00 were not aligned with the priorities outlined in the Peace and Order and Public Safety (POPS) Plan of the LGU, while 15 PPAs in the Annual Investment Plan totaling P515,000.00 were not supported by the Peace and Order Plan, and ineligible expenses totaling P9,840.00 were charged under the fund, contrary to Items 3.22 & 3.23 and 5.1.3.1 of the COA-DBM-DILG-GCG for GOCCs-DND Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2015-01 and Part II and IV of the Budgets Operations Manual for Local Government Units, hence, it did not only impede the effective utilization of resources but also hindered the ability of the Municipality to achieve its peace and order objectives coherently and systematically.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) that in the preparation of the Annual Investment Plan, conduct a thorough review of the Peace and Order and Public Safety (POPS) Plan of the LGU and ensure that all proposed PPAs are aligned with the priorities outlined in the POPS Plan and duly supported by the Peace and Order Plan. 

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the Local Peace and Order Councils to include only those PPAs that are directly related to crime prevention and law enforcement activities, capability development for personnel of law enforcement agencies, volunteers/partners, or programs for anti-illegal drug, illegal gambling, counter-insurgency, and/or counter-terrorism efforts, among others that will directly contribute to the maintenance of the peace and order and public safety.
observations.reason: Other PPAs that are equally necessary like the provision of support to the POC Secretariat also need to be appropriated.  However, this was not included in the budget despite inclusion in the POPS Plan.

observations.recommendation: Liquidations of funds transfers totaling P20,963,820.00 were not made and unutilized balances of P230,200.00 were not returned to the source agencies thus, obligation of the Municipality was not extinguished despite fulfilment of the purpose of fund transfers.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to return all unexpended balances of funds received to the source agencies.  

Likewise, submit Liquidation Reports upon completion of the purpose of fund transfers, together with all supporting documents and official receipt of returned unexpended balance, if any, to the Auditor and furnish the same to the source agency.
observations.reason: The unexpended balances of prior years’ fund transfers were not yet returned to the source agencies.

observations.recommendation: The reported balance of the Construction in Progress (CIP) accounts totaling P109,051,261.85 is unreliable due to an unreconciled difference of P31,477,185.29 between the List of CIP projects and General Ledger, absence of Subsidiary Ledgers, and the inclusion of completed projects totaling P71,866,778.57 which were not reclassified to the appropriate asset accounts upon completion, consequently resulting in the non-recognition of depreciations for CY 2022, contrary to Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015- 009, Section 10 of Chapter 1 of the New Government Accounting System Manual for LGUs, Philippine Application Guidance 3 to IPSAS 17, and DPWH Department Order No. 176, series of 2015, thereby affecting the reliability and fair presentation of the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

X x x

prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to reclassify the completed projects from the CIP to the appropriate PPE account, and henceforth effect the reclassification of the completed projects upon completion as shown in the Certificate of Completion; and

recognize the corresponding depreciation of the PPE from the date of completion. Depreciation shall be for one month if the PPE is available for use on or before the 15th of the month, and on the succeeding month if the PPE is available for use after the 15th of the month.
observations.reason:
Completed projects totaling P2,372,093.55 were not transferred to appropriate PPE accounts. Details are discussed in Audit Observation No. 3, Part II of this report. 


No depreciation was recognized because of non-reclassification to appropriate PPE accounts.

observations.recommendation: Revenue for Waterworks System Fees and Rent/Lease amounting to P512,684.00 and P190,200.00, respectively, was not recognized on an accrual basis resulting in the understatement of the revenue, while the time of payment for rental fees as stipulated in the contract did not concur with the provision in the Municipal Revenue Code, and 25% surcharge was not imposed, contrary to Section 4 of the New Government Accounting System Manual for Local Government Units, and Section 108 and 109 of the Municipal Ordinance 2017-319, hence, affecting the reliability of the reported revenue in the Financial Statements, and loss of income to the Municipality.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor secure written agreement for every undertaking involving fiscal administration and direct the:

Waterwork supervisor to prepare a complete statement of water meter reading showing the quantity of water consumed and the corresponding amount due from each consumer, and submit the same to the MTO and the Municipal Accountant.

MTO to collect the amount due from the water consumers based on the statement made by the Waterwork Supervisor, and prepare a separate statement of collections on waterworks to be submitted to the Municipal Accountant:

MTO to revise the stipulations in the lease contract which must be consistent with the provisions of the Revised Revenue Code of the Municipality and provide the Municipal Accountant with a copy of the Lease Contract of all the tenants and enforce the collection of 25% surcharges for every late payment of the rental fees.

Municipal Accountant to record the income on an accrual basis based on the monthly statement of the water consumption for the waterworks and based on the Lease Contract. And, prepare a subsidiary ledger per tenant. Also, conduct monthly reconciliation with the MTO records to ensure check and balance and to allow immediate detection and correction of errors.
observations.reason: The stipulations in the contract were not revised to conform with the provisions of the Revised Revenue Code.

observations.recommendation: The DRRM response and rescue equipment and other equipage for floods, earthquakes, and landslides were insufficient compared to basic equipment prescribed under DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-73, contrary to Section 2 (a) of Republic Act No. 10121 and Item 5.5 of National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (NDRRMC), Department of Budget and Management (DBM) and Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013, hence, may cause incapability of the Municipality for an immediate response during calamities.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council to procure the prescribed DRRM tools and equipment under NDRRMC, DBM, and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 to equip the Municipality in responding to calamities and strengthen the capacity for disaster risk reduction and management
observations.reason: The majority of the DRRM Equipment prescribed under the NDRRMC, DBM, and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 were not yet procured.

, and	related

observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Compute the depreciation of all undepreciated assets and draw a journal entry to set the accounts in order. 
X x x
Record accounting Entries for the recognition of PPEs found at station, recognition of loss of PPEs, writing- off/dropping from the books of accounts the remaining balances amounts lumped under the unreconciled SLs of PPEs, and derecognition of non-existing/missing items.
observations.reason:

Lost PPE items and unserviceable were still recorded in the books .

observations.recommendation:
The accuracy of the
year-end balance of
Cash  in  Bank  –

Moreover the collections were not deposited regularly and intact with the Authorized Government Depository Bank (AFDB) by the Municipal Treasurer incurring delays from 1 to 10 banking days for collections totalling P270,477.37, inconsistent with Section 69 PD No. 1445, and COA Circular No. 92-382, thereby exposing the government funds to risk of possible loss and / or misuse.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant; 

Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the collectors to    remit the collections daily before 5:00 PM or before  leaving  the office in case of emergency leave, and direct the Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact all her collections, as well as collections turned over by the Revenue Collecting Clerk, daily or not later than the next banking day.
observations.reason: Collections were not all deposited intact due to late remittance by the collectors to the Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation:
The	Design	and
Build	of	Tomas
Oppus	Public
Market,with
contract	cost

P49,949,638.60,
was	only	65.27%
accomplished as of

contrary	to	the

Agreement	which
sets	January	22,

completion	and
Annex  G  of  the

of RA No. 9184,
resulting in a delay
of up to 422 days in
the completion of
projects, thus, the

observations.managementAction:
Henceforth	we recommended that the Municipal Mayor 

Require		the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to	see	to	it that liquidated		damages will be effected on every day of delay until the project is completed	 and accepted by the officials concerned.
observations.reason: Liquidated damages not yet imposed since final billing has not been submitted by the contractor.

observations.recommendation:
Various Properties with a total cost of P61,097,717.07
were not covered with appropriate property insurance with the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS), contrary to Sections 2 and 5 of Republic Act (RA) No. 656
and Section 3.1 of Commission on Audit	(COA) Circular  No.  92-
390, thus, depriving the Municipal Government with adequate	 and reliable protection against	any damages or loss of	these assets.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor  through  the
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer submit list of insurable properties with their correct total cost to the Audit Team.; and 

 Municipal General Services Officer to file insurance for the uninsured properties with the GSIS consistent with the provisions		of Sections 2 and 5 of RA. 656 and Section 3.1 of COA Circular No. 92-390
dated November 17,
1992.
observations.reason: No list of insurable properties was submitted to the Audit Team. Details are discussed in Audit Observation No. 8, part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
The accuracy, existence	and valuation of the Road Networks account amounting to P4,495,296.00 as
of  December  31,
2019 is doubtful due to: a) non- reconciliation of balances of the Report of Physical Count of Property, Plant	and
Equipment (RPCPPE) for road networks	against the books resulting to a discrepancy of P88,450.00; b) non-
recognition of cost of the road lot component; c) non preparation   and submission of the Report	on the Physical Count of Local	Road Network (RPCLRN); d) non- maintenance of a complete Local Road Network Property Card and separate subsidiary ledgers for the road and its components and; e) non- disclosure in  the Notes to	 the Financial Statements the total road network system of the Municipality.
observations.reason: No road lots were recorded and components of the road networks were not segregated.

observations.recommendation: Various accounts receivable aggregating P989,061.44 remained dormant for several years contrary to COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016, due to the Municipality’s failure to institute action to collect them or write off dormant receivables, absence of records or documents  to validate the claim, unknown whereabouts of some debtors.
observations.managementAction: We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to send demand letters to the debtors for the immediate settlement of receivables with the signature of the Mayor, and request authority from COA for the write off of dormant Due from LGU and other Receivable accounts in accordance with COA circular		No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 if warranted, duly supported   by a quarterly  aging  of dormant receivable and other relevant documents
observations.reason: Request for write-off was not submitted to the audit team.

observations.recommendation: Due to PhilHealth, Due to GSIS, Due to GOCCs and Due to LGUs accounts totaling P1,295,660.69 were non-moving for three to nineteen years and were not supported with valid proof to warrant payment, contrary to the reportorial requirements set forth in Section 111 and 121 of PD No. 1445.
observations.reason:
Reversion has not been made since there was no request submitted to the audit team.



Reconciliation was not yet completed. 





Confirmation with source agencies and recipient of RPT was not made.

observations.recommendation:
Lands owned by the Municipality amounting	to P845,000.00  were
not registered under the Torrens Tile System, hence proof of ownership of these assets cannot be established.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Assessor to register all lands owned by the Municipality under the Torrens Title System pursuant to COA Circular No. 92-386.
observations.reason: The Municipal Assessor is still processing the titling of the lots.

observations.recommendation: Submission, rendition of accounts and related financial reports were very much delayed contrary to the provisions of Sec. 100 and 107 of PD 1445, thus, the importance and relevance	of  the financial information  for decision-making was defeated.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor should require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to strictly observe Sec. 100 and 107 of PD 1445 otherwise the penal provisions under Sec. 127 of PD 1445 shall be applied.
observations.reason: Submissions of Disbursement Vouchers and other financial reports were not made on time.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:
a.	City Accountant -
coordinate with the City Treasurer for the submission of relevant and sufficient documents relating to the unrecorded financial transactions; and
record adjustments based on relevant and sufficient documents; and
b.	City Treasurer-
institute sound measures to capture information and obtain documents on all electronic collections/deposits;
submit to the OCA all financial reports and documents promptly in accordance with existing regulations; and
coordinate with the City Accountant for the regular reconciliation of records.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the:
City Engineer and/or other Officials concerned to prepare turnover documents as basis for the derecognition of assets which were already transferred to the recipient agencies; and
City Accountant record adjustments corresponding to the noted accounting errors based on relevant and sufficient documents.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the City Planning and Development Office and the CEO to collectively formulate a holistic and realistic approach on the determination of which of the priority projects from the Local Development Investment Program are feasible for implementation during the year. With this in mind, preparatory activities such as detailed engineering could be thoroughly undertaken to optimize efficiency of project implementation.

We recommended that Management comply with Item 10, Annex “E” of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 in granting requests of contractors for work suspension of projects, and refrain from issuing Suspension Order without reasonable and valid basis.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GFPS to regularly monitor whether the GAD budget is being spent for what it was intended for and check on the actual cost of implementing the GAD activities to ensure the effective, efficient and judicious utilization of the said fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the City Accountant/Personnel-in-Charge to:
make reconciliation and verify the over-remittances and under-remittances of taxes and perform necessary action based on the result thereof;
regularly check that the amounts withheld match payroll records, contractor/supplier payments, and other taxable transactions to ensure accuracy before remittance; and
ensure that all taxes collected are fully remitted to the BIR within the period prescribed under the applicable tax laws and regulations. Late remittances should be avoided to prevent penalties and surcharges.

We recommended that Management establish a systematic payroll process and conduct regular compliance checks to ensure timely remittances to HDMF, so as to avoid incurring any interest, penalty or surcharges due to delayed remittances, and for the member-employees to fully and continuously enjoy the benefits of the programs and services of the HDMF.

We recommended that the City Accountant:
a) Review and recompute accumulated depreciation for Investment
Property-Buildings and all affected PPE accounts;
b) Prepare the Journal Entry Voucher to adjust the Accumulated
Depreciation of the affected accounts based on the recomputed
amounts, with reference to the computations suggested;
c) Follow-up on the status of its application for the installation of the e-
NGAS system so that the automation of the City Accountant’s Office
account record-keeping process will be expedited.

The Inventory Committee to conduct a semestral periodic physical
inventory count of its supplies and materials, and to prepare and
submit RPCI to the City Auditor not later than July 31 and January
31 of each year for the first and second semester, respectively,
furnishing a copy to the City Accountant.

The City Accountant to perform reconciliation of inventory balances
appearing in the books of accounts against the balance shown in the
physical count, upon his receipt of the RPCI from the Inventory
Committee, and to maintain Supplies Ledger Cards for each type of
supplies inventory to facilitate proper monitoring of balances.

The GSO to maintain Supplies Stock Cards for each type of supplies to
facilitate overall inventory management for the City government, and
ensure that the prepared SSMI reflect only those items that have actually
been physically issued or distributed to end-users.

1.3. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Withhold the correct amount of taxes from the employee’s monthly
salary as computed using the Withholding Tax Table;
b. Determine the tax due from each employee on taxable compensation
income for the entire taxable year on or before the end of the calendar
year but prior to the payment of the compensation for the last payroll
period;
c. Withhold from the employee’s December salary any difference between
the total tax due for the year and the sum of taxes withheld from
January to November;
d. Submit proof of settlement by the employees concerned of their tax
deficiencies for CY 2023 and CY 2024;
e. Prepare the appropriate adjusting entries to correct the balance of the
Due to BIR account;
f. Exert best efforts to recover the overpaid amount of ₱458,609.10 from
the BIR; and
g. Analyze and validate the taxes withheld in prior years and remit the
same to the BIR, if warranted.
1.4. We also recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer deduct the correct amount of withholding taxes in the monthly Payroll.

2.7. We recommended that the Human Resource and Management Officer
(HRMO):
a. Reconcile records with GSIS and promptly communicate any necessary
updates to employees' employment status to the Agency Authorized
Officer (AAO) using the prescribed GSIS format to ensure that GSIS
records are accurately updated in a timely manner; and
b. Ensure that all premium contributions and loan amortizations are
deducted from employees' salaries in the payroll for the month they are
due.
2.8. We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer or the AAO:
a. Promptly update the employee's employment status and salary
adjustments in the GSIS Web MPS as soon as the HRMO provides the
necessary information; and
b. Promptly inform the HRMO and Municipal Accountant on the
approval of employees' loan applications to ensure timely deductions.
2.9. Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Conduct monthly reconciliations of employee premium contributions
and loan amortizations by comparing the GSIS billing with actual
payroll deductions and remitted amounts. This ensures billing
accuracy, facilitates timely remittances to GSIS, and guarantees
correct salary deductions;
b. Conduct a thorough reconciliation of the GSIS billing for the unpaid
premiums of ₱521,377.69, and the premium contributions of
₱523,781.78 paid in CY2023, to verify whether the corresponding
amounts were actually deducted from the employees’ salaries and
determine the outstanding amount for remittance to GSIS; and
c. Maintain Subsidiary Ledgers for the Due to GSIS account.

3.5. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the accounting
records to verify the accuracy of the unaccounted/unsettled Due to PhilHealth
account with a balance of ₱28,872.97, as well as, the unsettled balance per
Statement of Premium Account (SPA) from PhilHealth totaling to
₱220,936.37. Any confirmed unsettled balances must be remitted to
PhilHealth immediately.
3.6. We also recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer
immediately communicate with the Municipal Accountant on the necessary
changes of the employees' employment status and its basic monthly salary, to
ensure that the PhilHealth records were updated accordingly.
3.7. Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure the full and
timely remittance of the amounts withheld from employees and the amount
for employer share, in accordance with the deadline set by PhilHealth,
including the immediate settlement of the interest charges on the delayed
payment made in October 2024 using its personal funds.

4.8. We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant:
a. double check or re-compute the amounts reported in the Reports of
Collections and Deposit;
b. accurately record the financial transactions in the cashbook and
accounting books; and
c. conduct periodic reconciliation of both records and make the necessary
adjustments to correct the errors identified in the said reconciliation.

5.13. We recommended that each Department prepare and submit to BAC the
PPMP for all PPAs, including the Infrastructure Projects based on the
approved AIP, taking into consideration the available resources, particularly
the budget ceilings, of the department or office concerned.
5.14. Further, we recommended that the BAC Chairman evaluate and consolidate
the PPMPs of both for Common Use Supplies and Equipment and for
Infrastructure Projects into the Annual Procurement Plan.

6.10. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the Municipal
Development Council, and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer,
identify and include in the AIP those priority projects which are
implementation-ready in the current budget year to ensure the optimum
utilization of the 20% DF.
6.11. We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer monitor the
implementation of priority projects identified in the AIP, especially those
financial assistance to be undertaken by the barangays.
6.12. We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal
Budget Officer submit a justification on:
a. the discontinuance of the development projects titled Construction of
Covered Drainage Canal -Poblacion (San Rafael Housing)- Kansanoc
and Concrete Overlay of Barangay Road (Buyo);
b. the change of the location and/or beneficiary of the project Construction
of Concrete Pathway (Buyo); and
c. the disbursements out of the 20% DF as Financial Assistance to
barangays for the construction of various development projects which
deviated from the PPAs identified in the AIP.
6.13. Lastly, we reiterated our recommendation that any changes in the Annual
Budget shall have a revised Annual Investment Plan and a Supplemental
budget.

7.10. We recommended that the Municipal Engineer attach “geotagged” pictures of
the Infrastructure Projects in every stages of the project, particularly before
the project implementation, upon mobilization, its monthly progress and upon
the completion of the project.
7.11. We reiterate the previous recommendation that payments of Honorarium of
the Resource Persons should be supported with a detailed computation.
7.12. We also recommended that the Municipal Social Welfare Development Officer
retain and submit a copy of the distribution list of beneficiaries whenever a
distribution of relief goods is conducted.
7.13. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough
review to ensure the completeness of the supporting documents attached to the
disbursement vouchers.

8.8. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the GAD Focal Person
strictly adhere to the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01, as amended, to ensure that all programs,
projects, and activities reported in the GAD Accomplishment Report are
aligned with the approved GAD Plan and Budget, thereby achieving the
intended objectives of the attributed funds.

9.13. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor obtain a corresponding
Sanggunian Bayan (SB) Resolution that authorizes the hiring of Job Order
(JO) personnel;
9.14. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Human Resource
Management Officer:
a. Develop and issue written policy guidelines outlining the standard
procedures for hiring Job Order (JO) personnel to ensure objective and
impartial selection process, specifying the required documents to be
submitted by applicants, the detailed job description in the Contract of
Service, the submission of accomplishment reports by JO personnel,
and other necessary internal control measures. This will help
streamline the hiring process and promote transparency and
accountability;
b. Conduct a comprehensive assessment and evaluation of the current
manpower complement to identify and remove any redundant or
unnecessary Job Order (JO) workers performing tasks already covered
by other staff or regular plantilla personnel; and
9.15. Limit the hiring of Job Order personnel strictly to essential roles, ensuring
that JOs are only employed to complement and support the existing workforce
of the LGU, thereby optimizing operational efficiency.

10.11. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Human Resource
Management Officer ensure that travel allowances are paid only to JO
personnel whose contracts explicitly authorize travel;
10.12. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue an Executive Order
establishing internal policies, guidelines, and limitations on travel claims for
JO personnel; and
10.13. Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant thoroughly
review the travel claims of JO personnel to ensure that they are properly
approved by the authorized official and supported by the required
certifications and other necessary documents before processing payment.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer to exert diligent efforts to take up the necessary adjustments and/or corrections regarding on the aforecited audit observations and other misstatements thereof, if any.
We recommended further to properly observe the policies laid down on the foregoing COA Circular in order to verify timely such account and to communicate immediately to the management any misstatements and/or deficiencies found thereof.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer to:
Review the Building, Construction in Progress, Accumulated Depreciation-Building accounts, and other affected accounts and effect the necessary adjustments and corrections, if warranted, to ensure the fair presentation of financial statements; and
Stop the practice of recording infrastructure project under Building and/or any PPE account when the same is not yet completed and/or available for use, in consonance with the foregoing COA Circular.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer to:
Review the Accounts Payable account and effect the necessary adjustments and corrections, if warranted, to ensure the fair presentation of financial statements;
Stop the practice of recording Accounts Payable for undelivered goods or unrendered services, in strict compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 and other prevailing accounting policies; and
Strictly require complete and proper supporting documents such as delivery receipts, inspection and acceptance reports, and other pertinent documents before recognizing and recording Accounts Payable to establish the validity and propriety of transactions.

We commended the Local Chief Executive, Municipal Engineer, MDRRMO, MPDC, and other concerned department heads for ensuring that the PPAs under the 5% LDRRMF were well-planned and feasible. Their efforts in formulating organized, realistic, and viable PPAs, along with their active involvement in the monitoring and implementation of these programs, are highly appreciated.
Regarding the noted deficiencies, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the MDRRMO to provide an explanation and/or justification for the absence of the pre-positioned goods from the above-mentioned purchase, which were not found in the storage area during the actual inspection.
Likewise, we further recommended the submission of the distribution list, if available, for the pre-positioned or relief goods to facilitate verification and/or examination. This is without prejudice to the issuance of the appropriate audit decision, if warranted.
Finally, we recommended the continuous adherence to the policies set forth in the applicable law and COA Circular to further strengthen the capabilities of the local government unit and its communities in disaster preparedness, mitigation, response, and recovery.

We commended the Local Chief Executive, GAD Focal Person, and other Department Heads concerned for ensuring that the GAD PPAs were effectively responsive to the gender issues within the community and aligned with the municipality’s mandate. Their efforts in developing organized, realistic, and viable GAD PPAs, as well as their active facilitation and monitoring of their implementation, are highly appreciated.
Moving forward, we recommended the continuous adherence to the policy outlined in the aforementioned Joint Memorandum Circular to further advance gender equality and the empowerment of women, ensuring the full realization of their legal rights.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Human Resource Officer, in coordination with the concerned Department Heads, to formulate and implement a standardized policy on the daily wage rates for Job Order (JO) personnel. The policy should be duly approved by the Local Chief Executive and must consider the personnel's qualifications, nature of work or assignment, and the prevailing minimum wage in the locality to ensure fair, consistent, and equitable compensation.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, to:
Diligently account for the beginning balance of the "Due to PhilHealth" account, ensuring that any necessary adjustments or corrections are properly recorded;
Coordinate with the PhilHealth Office to verify the status of unpaid contributions, if any, and promptly settle outstanding obligations; and
Henceforth, ensure the timely and accurate remittance of PhilHealth contributions to prevent interest charges, in compliance with existing applicable laws, rules, and regulations.

We commended the management for its compliance with the aforementioned laws, rules, and regulations related to Pag-IBIG.
For some deficiencies noted, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer to:
Exert diligent efforts to account the beginning balance of Due to Pag-IBIG account and make the necessary adjustments and/or correction if necessary;
Coordinate with the Pag-IBIG Office to verify whether the LGU has any unpaid contributions. If any are identified, settle the same accordingly; and
Ensure the timely and accurate remittance of Pag-IBIG contributions, in compliance with applicable laws and regulations, to avoid interest charges.

1.16. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
d. coordinate with the Inventory Committee, Municipal Treasurer and
Municipal Engineer in facilitating the conduct of inventory of all local roads
within the LGU’s jurisdiction;
e. maintain subsidiary records on the roads and road components for each road
network;
f. discontinue assigning residual values in computing depreciation, and make
appropriate adjustments thereon to correct the understatement of the
Accumulated Depreciation and Government Equity accounts.
1.17. We further recommended that the Municipal Engineer provide the Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer with information on the complete
description and segregated cost components of each road project.
1.18. Lastly, we recommended that the designated General Services Officer:
c. Independently maintain the Local Road Inventory and Road Map and the
Local Road Network Property Cards; and
d. Render the Report on Local Road Network at year-end.

2.10. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjusting
entries to correct recording errors in the affected accounts from the General Fund and
Trust Fund books, to properly account for the transfer of the unexpended
LDRRM Fund balances. Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant shall record and
make the transfer of any unexpended balance of LDRRMF (QRF & MOOE) and its
corresponding cash, from the General Fund to Trust Fund, at year-end.

3.11. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the members of the
Municipal Planning Development Council, through the MPD Coordinator, expedite
the implementation of prior and current years’ unimplemented development PPAs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor submit to the Audit Team the Executive Order establishing the Inventory Committee of LGU Mahaplag and require the Inventory Committee in coordination with the GSO to conduct the Physical Count of all PPEs of the LGU and submit an Official Report on Physical Count of PPE not later than June 30, 2025.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant-Designate establish and maintain complete and detailed PPE Ledger Cards, and that the LGSO-Designate do the same for Property Cards covering all LGU properties. This will strengthen record-keeping, ensure accountability, and support the accuracy of financial records.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and maintain the lapsing schedule of the PPE of the LGU to support the recorded accumulated depreciation and reconcile the same with the recorded amounts in the PPE Ledger Cards and Financial Statement.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjustments for the above-misstated accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and GSO reconcile their Road Network records.
We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer provide the Municipal Accountant and the GSO with the correct and complete description and cost segregation of road components of all road projects.
Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Assessor inspect and assess the cost of the road lot component of all road networks within the territorial jurisdiction of LGU-Mahaplag and provide data to the Municipal Accountant, GSO and Municipal Engineer.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the Road Lot component of the Municipal Road projects based on the report of the Municipal Assessor, maintain the LRNLC with complete description of each road component, and disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements in the ensuing years the breakdown of the Road Network Account.
Likewise, we recommended that the Inventory Committee, use the required format provided in COA Circular 2015-008 in reporting the Physical Count of Road Network System.
Finally, we recommended that the GSO prepare and maintain complete and detailed LRNPC for all roads and its components and in the ensuing years, prepare the Report on Local Road Network.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make a correcting entry to restore the amount of ₱31,712,558.95 to the Due to NGAs account and prepare and submit the Fund Utilization Reports of the same to the source agency in order to recognize its income.
Also, we reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that income should be recognized only upon liquidation.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant-Designate:
submit a written explanation to the Audit Team why the Bank Reconciliation Statement (BRS) was not prepared and submitted for the (1) General Fund-Main, for the CY 2024;
obtain the monthly bank statements from the depository bank promptly, prepare the monthly Bank Reconciliation regularly, and submit the same to the Audit Team within 10 days after the end of each month; and
draw the necessary adjusting entry/ies to restore the unreleased checks and cancelled checks to the Cash in bank account to reflect the actual Cash balance of the LGU at the end of the year. Said entries shall be reversed when checks are released to the payees in the ensuing year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant:
submit breakdown, if available, of the reported savings which was certified and used as basis for the approved supplemental budgets in CY 2024; and
submit justification as to the deficiencies noted by the Audit Team as to the amount of actual savings.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the Local Development Council, and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer:
identify and include in the AIP priority projects which are ready for implementation in the current budget year to ensure optimum utilization of the 20% Development Fund;
implement the remaining unimplemented projects; and
Re-appropriate the balances of completed projects to other PPAs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the MDRRM Council, and the MDRRM Officer:
ensure full implementation of PPAs identified in the LDRRMFIP in order to effectively increase the level of preparedness of the municipality during natural calamities;
Provide specific timelines and implement the PPAs under the LDRRMFIP within the said timelines; and
Re-appropriate the balances of completed and unnecessary projects to other PPAs.

We recommended that the GFPS Technical Working Group and GAD Focal Person provide the lacking breakdown of expenditures pursuant to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01, as amended.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue an Executive/Administrative Order to officially create and establish the GFPS TWG and GAD M&E Team, in accordance with the personnel requirements outlined in Sections 4.1 and 5.2 of JMC 2013-01, and clearly define their roles and responsibilities.

We recommended that the Local School Board and the Municipal Accountant diligently comply with the reportorial requirements in the allocation and utilization on Special Education Fund as outlined in the afore-cited regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant-Designate and GSO:
trace back the LGUs previous transactions on the acquisition of the listed unaccounted insured properties with no Accounting and GSO records;
ensure that the same are accurately recorded in the appropriate official records;
maintain an updated PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards for Motor Vehicles and DRR Equipment with detailed description of properties such as brand, color, plate numbers, etc.; and
submit to the Audit Team the necessary records.
We also recommended that the GSO-Designate submit to the Audit Team the PAR of the above unaccounted 7 insured properties with detailed description.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that all insurable LGU properties are covered under the General Insurance Fund (GIF) of the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS), in compliance with R.A. No. 656 and the guidelines outlined in COA Circular 2018-002.

We recommended that the Human Resource and Management Officer (HRMO):
Reconcile records with GSIS and promptly update employees on any necessary changes to their employment status. These updates should be communicated to the Agency Authorized Officer (AAO) using the prescribed GSIS format to ensure timely and accurate record updates; and
Ensure that all premium contributions and loan amortizations are deducted from employees' salaries in the payroll for the month they are due.
We also recommended that the AAO:
Promptly update employees' employment status and salary adjustments in the GSIS Web MPS upon receiving the necessary information from the HRMO; and
Monitor the approval of employees' loan applications and immediately inform the HRMO and Municipal Accountant to ensure timely deductions from payroll.
Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant-Designate:
provide the Audit Team with an explanation of the discrepancies identified in the GSIS billing, the amounts withheld from contributions and loan deductions, and the corresponding remittances as reflected in Tables 33 and 34; and
conduct monthly reconciliations of employee premium contributions and loan amortizations by comparing GSIS billings with actual payroll deductions and remitted amounts. This practice ensures billing accuracy, facilitates timely remittances to GSIS, and guarantees correct salary deductions.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the accounting records to verify the accuracy of the unaccounted/unsettled "Due to PhilHealth" account, which has a balance of ₱59,150.83. Additionally, the unsettled balance per the Statement of Premium Account (SPA) from PhilHealth, totaling ₱107,811.93, should be examined. Any confirmed outstanding amounts must be remitted to PhilHealth without delay.
We also recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer promptly coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to update employees' employment status and basic monthly salary. This will ensure that PhilHealth records reflect accurate and up-to-date information.
Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant in coordination with the Remitting Officer ensure the full and timely remittance of both employee contributions and the employer’s share in compliance with PhilHealth's deadlines. Additionally, any interest charges incurred due to delayed payments in May 2024 should be settled immediately using personal funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant-Designate in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer:
Exert diligent efforts to account the beginning balance of Due to Pag-IBIG account and make the necessary adjustments and/or correction if necessary;
Coordinate with the Pag-IBIG Office to verify whether the LGU has any unpaid contributions. If any are identified, settle the same accordingly; and
Ensure the timely and accurate remittance of Pag-IBIG contributions, in compliance with applicable laws and regulations, to avoid interest charges.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Immediately compute the correct tax liability for each affected employee and official, encompassing the previously excluded Service Recognition Incentive (SRI) and the excess clothing allowance. The corresponding under withheld amount should be reflected in a payroll adjustment and remitted to the BIR as soon as possible, along with any applicable penalties or interest; and
ensure accurate differentiation between taxable and non-taxable income in accordance with BIR regulations and regularly review payroll tax computation to avoid penalties and interest due to under withholding.
We also recommended that the management, thru the Municipal Accountant, allow regular training for relevant staff on current tax laws and BIR pronouncements to ensure ongoing compliance and prevent future under withholding.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and Head of the Human Resources Management Office:
Issue written guidelines on the standard procedures to be followed in the hiring of JOs to ensure professional and impartial selection process.
Limit the hiring of Job Order workers to indispensable functions only and pay the prescribed daily minimum wage as prescribed by the Regional Tripartite Wages and Productivity Boards for Region VIII;
Require the JOs to provide details of the actual duties/tasks accomplished in the monthly accomplishment report to facilitate proper evaluation; and
Prioritize the hiring for vacant Plantilla Positions, subject to the prescribed PS limitation.

1.7. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit to the
audit team the Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statement for the Market, PBG1,
and HPG accounts for verification.

1.8. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant take up the reconciling
items affecting the cash in bank balance per book to ensure the accuracy of the
CIB-LCCA balance.

2.10. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the concerned
officers and employees, immediately prepare and submit the necessary
documents to facilitate the appraisal and disposal of unserviceable properties,
and expedite the disposal process in strict compliance with the applicable guidelines.

2.11. We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Chairperson of the
Appraisal and Disposal Committee to attend a seminar on the Appraisal and
Disposal of Unserviceable Properties (ADUP), to be conducted by the Commission on Audit
Regional Office VIII – Training Department, to ensure proper understanding and
implementation of the prescribed procedures

1.12 We strongly recommended that the Municipal Treasurer continue to exert all
possible means of closing the dormant bank accounts, only if not use, otherwise
reactivate it, by complying all the bank requirements, as well as obtain regularly
the bank statements on all of the LGU’s bank accounts.
1.13 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant update regularly its
subsidiary ledgers for cash-in-bank accounts to facilitate preparation of its
respective monthly bank reconciliation statements.
1.14 Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that the Municipal
Treasurer and Municipal Accountant comply with the audit recommendations on
the matter.

2.15 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
d. coordinate with the Inventory Committee, General Services Officer (GSO)
in validating the report prepared by the Municipal Engineer with regards to
the conduct of inventory of all local roads within the LGU’s jurisdiction, and
to account its difference with the reported amount in the Financial
Statements;
e. maintain subsidiary records on the roads and road components for each road
network;
f. discontinue assigning residual values in computing depreciation, and make
appropriate adjustments thereon to correct the understatement of the
Accumulated Depreciation and Government Equity accounts.
2.16 We further recommended that the Municipal Engineer provide the
Municipal Accountant and the GSO with information on the complete
description and segregated cost components of each road project.
2.17 Lastly, we recommended that the GSO:
c. Independently maintain the Local Road Inventory and Road Map and the
Local Road Network Property Cards; and
d. Render the Report on Local Road Network at year-end.

3.10 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjusting
entries to correct recording errors in the affected accounts from the General Fund
and Trust Fund books, to properly account for the transfer of the unexpended
LDRRM Fund balances. Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant shall record and
make the transfer of any unexpended balance of LDRRMF (QRF & MOOE) and
its corresponding cash, from the General Fund to Trust Fund, at year-end.

4.9 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw a Journal Entry Voucher
(JEV) to record the completed project to its proper PPE Accounts. We also
recommended that all completed projects, accompanied by the necessary
supporting documents such as the approved Statement of Work Accomplished,
Certificate of Completion, and Certificate of Acceptance, be recorded as PPE in the
books of accounts of the Municipality at year-end.

1. Direct the GSO and Accounting Office to coordinate and conduct the
reconciliation of the results of the physical inventory and accounting
records, identify and address the nature and causes of the variances
and hold responsible the designated supply officers/custodians and
keep up reconciled reports at all times.

is granted by appropriate office to bring the reported balance of
the PPE account in the financial statements in order.

Council, through the Municipal Mayor as Chairman, review the
unimplemented projects in prior years funded from the Continuing
Appropriations of 20% DF during budget preparation to determine its viability and
necessity for inclusion in the AIP, otherwise, recommend reversion of the funds if
no longer needed or realign the appropriated fund to another development project needed by the municipality.

2.13 We also recommended for the reversion of the unexpended balance from the
completed projects to the General Fund for reappropriation.

3.9 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned
departments to expedite the implementation of the unimplemented PPAs
funded by various NGAs.

3.10 We also recommended that the Local Finance Committee and other concerned
municipal personnel update and submit the Fund Utilization Report/Report
of Disbursement on a monthly basis for all fund transfers from NGAs and
Financial Assistance funded projects and return to the Source Agency the
unexpended balances from the completed projects.

4.9 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and municipal officials concerned submit all the required documentary requirements in adherence with DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2022-105 to ensure compliance with applicable laws, rules, and regulations, and to prevent any potential issues or concerns in audit or legal reviews.

5.10 We recommended that the GAD Focal Person, through the Chairperson of the
Technical Working Group, applies the HGDG test on its PPAs and use the
results as basis in estimating the cost to be mainstreamed in the Annual GPB
of the Municipality and for the Accomplishment Reports for GAD.

Office (GSO) to prepare the Property Inventory Form (PIF) to serve as the
basis for assessing the general insurance coverage of all insurable assets,
properties, and interests of the municipality with the General Insurance Fund
(GIF) administered by the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS). The

30 of each year, in compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No.
2018-002.

6.9 We further recommended that the Municipality ensure that all its insurable
properties, assets, and interests are covered against insurable risks through
the General Insurance Fund, as administered by the GSIS, to safeguard public
resources from potential losses.

7.13 We recommended ensuring that the Local School Board (LSB) is provided with
complete, accurate, and timely reports, along with all essential information related
to the preparation of the Special Education Fund (SEF) budget, in strict compliance
with DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 1, s. 2017. This
will enable the LSB to effectively optimize the utilization of the SEF, ensuring that
it adequately supplements the annual education budget, particularly in supporting
the operation, maintenance, and priority programs of public schools.

7.14 We further recommended that the Local School Board and the Municipal
Accountant diligently comply with the reportorial requirements in the allocation
and utilization on Special Education Fund as outlined in the afore-cited regulations.

We recommended that the:
Municipal General Service Officer/Inventory Committee and the Municipal Engineer –
Verify the condition of each of the Road Networks listed in the Report on the Physical Count of the Local Road Network System; and
Reconcile the Report on the Physical Count of the Local Road Network System and Road Map to ensure that all road assets are properly documented and accounted for.
Municipal Engineer prepare a report showing the complete description and cost segregation of each individual road component of the municipality’ road network systems, upon completion of the reconciliation in a(ii) above, and provide the Municipal Accountant and Municipal General Service Officer with a copy of such report.
Municipal Accountant to –
prepare the adjusting entry to correct the understatement of the accumulated depreciation and related depreciation accounts noted in Item c of this observation, and henceforth compute the depreciation expense based on the useful life time-table prescribed by DPWH DO No. 176;
record the Road Network System per component based on the report submitted by the Municipal Engineer; and
maintain subsidiary ledgers for each individual component of the road network systems.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
communicate with the identified source agency (SA) to obtain the details/purpose of the funds received in order to evaluate LGU Palo’s status of accomplishment of its outstanding obligations, if any, under the grant, and facilitate the return of the balances to the SA when required;
review available financial records, reports and communication to gather missing information and verify account balances; and
revert to the General Fund the amounts, for which information could no longer be traced, and inform the Municipal Budget Officer of such reversion after confirming with the Municipal Treasurer the availability of the corresponding cash.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Issue written guidelines on the drafting of JO Contracts, taking into account their expected functions and responsibilities in relation to the specific needs of the particular office/department they will be assigned to;
Mandate the preparation of individual contracts for each JO personnel, adding express stipulations on the entitlement to overtime pay and other additional remuneration when appropriate and justified; and
Stop the grant of wage adjustments to national government employees detailed to the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Budget Officer to identify potential funding sources for the construction of a covered storage facility for disaster equipment.
Municipal Engineer, MDRRMO and GSO to prioritize the construction of a temporary covered storage facility or identify an existing sheltered area to protect the disaster-response vehicles and equipment from environmental damage, in order to prevent faster deterioration, breakdowns, and costly repairs or replacements.

We recommended that the BAC and Technical Working Group:
Submit the compliance of the winning bidder of the project requirements and technical specifications in the PBDs as indicated in paragraph 5.7; and
Henceforth, verify, validate, review and meticulously evaluate the bidding documents submitted by the proponent bidder during preliminary examination of bids, bid evaluation, and post-qualification.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Payroll Officer:
Adjust computations in the payroll to ensure that Pag-IBIG contributions are automatically capped at the limit prescribed by current guidelines, except when explicit consent is given by employees for their voluntary personal share;
Conduct a thorough reconciliation between government share already remitted in previous periods and that which the Municipality was legally obliged to remit under applicable guidelines, to determine the actual amount of over-remittance; and
Request representation with the Pag-IBIG Fund remittance branch, through the Office of the Municipal Mayor, for the adjustment in the amount of future government share obligations, taking into account the amount of over-remittance determined upon reconciliation in letter (b) above.

We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer revisit the LGU's budget to determine if the allocation for the government share of Pag-IBIG contributions reflect the correct amount that the municipality shall be liable for, in accordance with the guidelines.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor mandate the:
Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to align their tax remittance process with BIR RMC No. 47-2023 by ensuring that withholding taxes are remitted on the 10th day of the succeeding month, regardless of when the payee claims the check; and the
Treasurer’s Office to submit disbursement vouchers and supporting documents to the Accounting Office on a weekly basis, regardless of whether the checks have been claimed or not to ensure that the accounting records are updated promptly and the remittance of taxes can be processed on time.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer:
process the immediate remittance of the ₱55,918.50 tax withheld to the BIR;
properly record all tax withholdings in the accounting books, ensuring that the exact amount withheld is reflected accurately as a liability under the Due to BIR account; and
ensure that tax remittances are promptly processed and reported to the BIR in compliance with applicable tax regulations.

We recommended that the Chairperson of the GFPS-TWG:
seek the assistance of a gender analysis specialist/expert from the PCW or other relevant agencies to ensure proper implementation of the HGDG tool;
conduct gender analysis of the attributed PPAs through the administration of the HGDG tool during the project identification and preparation of GAD AR to determine whether the attributed GAD PPAs entirely address gender related issues, and submit the corresponding scores; and
establish, enhance, strengthen and maintain a gender responsive evaluation system to monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to obtain the Bank Statements of the five (5) depository accounts referred to in Table 2 and submit these to the Municipal Accountant for reconciliation.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to:
prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statement based on the Bank Statements as recommended above;
verify and reconcile the unaccounted discrepancies of net ₱1,683,997.30 and ₱389,153.46 across Cash in Bank accounts, and prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries;
exhaust all possible remedies to locate the original voucher/s and its supporting documents for the ₱2,123,433.45 withdrawal in the Bayanihan account. This is without prejudice to the issuance of Notice(s) of Suspension and/or Disallowance, if warranted; and
recognize the overbooked ₱2,140,355.89 from the written-off dormant accounts, as well as, effect the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to reflect the correct balance of the "Cash in Bank" account.

We recommended that management conduct an immediate investigation to determine the circumstances leading to the unauthorized write-off and take steps to corroborate the written-off dormant accounts. Henceforth, strictly adhere to COA Circular No. 2023-008 and relevant/applicable law, rules and regulation in future similar transactions to strengthen internal controls to prevent unauthorized transactions, and ensure observance to the principles of accountability and transparency.

We reiterated the prior year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor:
require the Municipal Accountant to (i) expedite substantiation of recorded PPE items and prepare detailed lapsing report to itemize lumped ₱751,002,397.84 PPE accounts, those labelled as “Beginning Balance” or the particulars were left blank/empty, based on the result of inventory count as recommended below;
instruct the Inventory Committee to (i) conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of PPE owned by the Municipality, including Land Improvements, Other Infrastructure Assets, School Buildings, Hospital/Health Centers and various Equipment accounts; and (ii) prepare and submit a complete RPCPPE to the Municipal Accountant not later than January 31 of each year, copy furnish the Audit Team, for reconciliation.

We also recommended that the Municipality exhaust possible remedy to reconcile the net ₱46,226,572.26 unresolved balances of the account and effect the necessary adjustments, as warranted, and avail the one-time cleansing of the PPE Account and strictly adhere with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for each road network to ensure proper accounting of the Road Network account for easy reconciliation of the Report on Physical Count of Local Road Networks and Disclose in the Notes to FS in the ensuing years the breakdown of the Road Network Account.
We also recommended that the Municipal GSO, who also serves as the Municipal Assessor to:
Maintain a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and their components, along with a Local Road Inventory and Road Map;
Prepare and submit a comprehensive Report of Physical Count of Local Road Networks to the Municipal Accountant. This report will facilitate reconciliation and resolution of any discrepancies; and
Inspect and assess the cost of the road lot component of all road networks within the territorial jurisdiction of LGU-Palompon and provide data to the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer.
We further recommended that the Municipal Engineer provide the Municipal Accountant and Municipal GSO with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for all road projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO), direct the concerned LGU personnel to apply for membership with PhilHealth to effect remittance of CY 2024 and prior year’s contributions and ensure that employees receive benefits due them, without prejudice to the imposition of penalties as provided for in Section 38 of R.A. No. 11223.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
review the accounting records to verify the accuracy of the unsettled prior years' Due to PhilHealth balance of ₱85,359.86 and the unsettled CY 2024 balance amounting to ₱184,836.93. Any confirmed unsettled balances must be remitted to PhilHealth immediately, based on the result of the recommendation above; and
coordinate with PhilHealth regarding the deficiencies noted in CY 2024 on the remittance of PhilHealth premiums of the aforementioned LGU Officials and employees, and remit the same, if warranted, together with the employer share.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with BIR regarding the tax compliance deficiencies noted in CY 2024 of the aforementioned LGU Personnel and file an amended return with the correct details to:
recognize the ₱175,781.05 over-remittance and facilitate refund to the entitled personnel;
immediately remit to the Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) the insufficient tax due of ₱240,527.54; and
effect the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to reflect the correct balance of the "Due to BIR" account.
We also recommended that Municipal Accountant and personnel in-charged with the monthly BIR remittances and filing of BIR Form 2316, implement periodic reviews on payroll tax computation and strictly adhere to the 1997 NIRC, as amended by Republic Act No. 10963 and relevant BIR pronouncements to ensure ongoing compliance and avoid penalties and/or interest.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal General Service Officer to determine the LGU properties, with their correct total cost including any improvement cost, not yet covered by insurance and facilitate the filing/application for the necessary insurance coverage of all identified properties with the GSIS under the Property Insurance Fund to ensure that any loss or damage to the same will be indemnified by the insurer pursuant to RA No. 656 and the guidelines outlined in COA Circular 2018-02; and
Local Legislative Body, in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer, prioritize and allocate sufficient funds in future budgets to ensure comprehensive property insurance coverage.
