In the future application of insurance with GSIS GIF, we also recommended that the Municipal GSO:
prepare and submit the PIF to GSIS not later than April 30 of each year; and
furnish copy of the PIF and proof of submission to GSIS and to the COA Audit Team for evaluation of the actions taken.

We recommended that the MSWDO/GAD Focal person, in coordination with
the Municipal Planning and Development Office, establish the LGU’s GAD Database and systematically gather updated data regarding various GAD information on gender statistics and age and sex-disaggregated data in the preparation of the LGU’s GAD plan to be able to come up with gender-responsive programs that effectively address current and relevant gender-related issues; and
the GFPS Technical Working Group provide the lacking breakdown of expenditures in accordance with Annex E of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2016-01;
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the GAD Focal Person, take the following actions:
provide a satisfactory explanation for the disbursement of funds exceeding the allocated budget and/or without proper appropriation in the approved Annual GPB; and
Ensure that appropriations are confirmed as available before any disbursements are made and that all funds are strictly disbursed within the approved budget limits.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, through the Municipal Engineer, stop the immediate issuance of the Certificate of Acceptance and Turnover on completed projects and issue only upon receipt of the Final Acceptance Inspection Report from the Inspectorate Team and Warranty Security from the contractor.
Moving forward, we recommended the Municipality’s Inspectorate Team conduct inspection of the projects after the one-year Defects Liability Period from the issuance of the Certificate of Completion and submit its Inspection Report within fifteen (15) days, indicating any construction defects/ deficiencies detected and the corresponding measures that must be taken by the contractor to correct them, the report is considered as the Final Acceptance Inspection Report if no defects/deficiencies are detected. If there are noted construction defects/deficiencies, the Municipal Mayor shall:
issue an order to the contractor to undertake repair works, at its own expense, for any damage to the infrastructure resulting from the use of substandard or inferior materials within ninety (90) days.  In case of failure or refusal to comply with this mandate, the Procuring Entity shall undertake such repair works and shall be entitled to full reimbursement of expenses incurred therein upon demand; and
instruct the Inspectorate Team, upon receipt of advice from the contractor that the defects/deficiencies in the said Order have been corrected, to conduct an inspection of the project and to submit its Acceptance Inspection Report within seven (7) days. If the report shows that the defects/deficiencies have been satisfactorily corrected, the report is considered as the Final Acceptance Inspection Report. Otherwise, the process is repeated until the noted defects/deficiencies have been satisfactorily corrected.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, thru the Municipal Engineer:
render a satisfactory justification on failing to notify the Audit Team in the demolition process of the Old ABC Building;
immediately submit the mandated documentation pertaining to the destruction/disposal prescribed by Item C of COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2024-1; and
ensure strict conformity to disposal regulations, including mandatory COA notification and involvement, outlined in COA Circular No. 89-296, COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2024-1 and any subsequent relevant COA and DBM issuances to ascertain adherence to the principles of accountability, transparency and prudent management of public resources.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, through the Municipal DRRM Officer and Municipal Accountant,
submit written explanation on recurring non-enforcement of liquidated damages in CY 2024 pertaining to the procurement of foregoing vehicles and/or equipment; and
impose Liquidation Damages, equal to one-tenth (1/10) of one-tenth (1/10) of one (1) percent (1%) of the delayed DRRM Rescue and Emergency vehicle/equipment costing ₱12,387,484.06, if warranted.
Also, to protect the interest of the government and to prevent further loss and wastage of government funds and resources, we reiterated the recommendation that the following measures for future projects of similar nature be undertaken:
Strictly monitor implementation of all projects, whether contracts for infrastructure, goods or consulting services, in accordance with the approved plans and specifications within the time frame specified in the contract agreement; and
Identify contracts which require appropriate management actions. If so, impose liquidated damages and/or administrative sanctions particularly termination of contracts and blacklisting of contractors for failure to comply with their obligations in accordance with the provisions of Sections 68 and 69, and Appendix 17 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 to ensure adherence to the principles of transparency, effectiveness and efficiency.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor,
through the Municipal DRRM Officer, render a written justification on the unaccounted food supplies and submit distribution documents such as the detailed Distribution Sheet and pictures of actual distribution of relief goods to substantiate the procurement and prove the veracity of the distribution/consumption; and
through the MSWD Officer:
assign a specific person to manage the Municipality’s warehouse for relief goods, ensuring proper storage and preservation to prevent deterioration. This person shall also be responsible for maintaining accurate records and accounting for both spoiled and unspoiled goods; and
cause the immediate distribution of unspoiled goods and disposal/ destruction of spoiled items to give space to newer stocks, if any, subject to proper documentation and recording procedures prescribed under existing regulations.
We also recommended that Management undertake immediate repair of the designated warehouse to ensure the safety and security of the stored prepositioned goods.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the designated Human Resource and Management Officer:
prioritize and consider filling-up the vacant positions by hiring new employees or expedite promotion of qualified employees to enhance its services and attain full implementation of the Municipality’s programs, projects and activities for the optimal benefits of the constituents, as well as to provide equal job opportunities to the community; and
intensify re-evaluation of the plantilla positions to address the current needs of the Municipality for the efficient and effective discharge of its functions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Human Resource and Management Officer and Bids and Awards Committee:
provide satisfactory explanation on the deficiencies noted in the hiring process of consultants whose functions are mere duplications of the functions performed by existing regular plantilla personnel;
assess the need to engage consultancy services and in case there is really a need for consultants. If so, engage only experts in their respective fields that will be selected thru proper procurement process; and
strictly adhere with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of Republic Act No. 9184, existing law, rules and regulations including COA Circulars on hiring of consultant to ascertain propriety, regularity and validity.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Human Resource and Management Officer and Municipal Treasurer:
adopt electronic payroll system through the Automated Teller Machine (ATM) of Government Banks for the payment of wages of Job Orders and Contract of Service personnel; and
ensure that wages of Job Orders and Contract of Service personnel conform with the mandated daily minimum wage rate pursuant to Wage Order No. RB VIII-24 of DOLE National Wages and Productivity Commission Regional Tripartite Wages and Productivity Board.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
ensure that the members of the newly formed Inventory Committee are comprised of appointed personnel who are accountable, competent, and properly oriented members from the Accounting, GSO, and user units;
support the eventual and successful conduct of the inventory activity by temporarily relieving the appointed members of the Inventory Committee of their regular duties upon the start of the inventory schedule until its completion, in order to allow full focus on the task;
issue an official memorandum or executive directive requiring full cooperation of all departments and user units during physical inventory and reconciliation activities;
schedule semi-annual or annual inventory checks as part of standard operating procedures and internal control measures; and
assign or hire a dedicated property clerk within the Accounting Office tasked solely with PPE monitoring, documentation, and coordination with the GSO.
We also recommended that the Inventory Committee:
conduct a complete physical count of all PPE, including older assets, within the prescribed timeline;
prepare and maintain an up-to-date Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) that accurately reflects the physical count and condition of all government assets; and
classify and address any obsolete or missing assets based on COA Circular No. 2020-006 procedures, including submission of Inventory and Inspection Reports of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and Disposal Reports as necessary.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
reconstruct a complete and detailed PPE subsidiary ledger using available documents (purchase orders, disbursement vouchers, inspection reports, etc.), and integrate data from the GSO; and
maintain and regularly update PPELCs for each PPE item.
Finally, we recommended that Municipal Accountant and GSO-Designate conduct regular reconciliation to match physical inventory with book balances, to timely correct errors and resolve discrepancies.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant to:
prepare adjusting entries related to the financial assistance by recording the transaction using the Due from LGUs account to properly reflect the nature of the fund transfer and facilitate liquidation monitoring; and
issue demand letters to recipient barangays on their liquidation responsibilities and monitor their compliance with the provisions in MOA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LDRRMO:
conduct a thorough review of all LDRRM funded programs and projects, including those under continuing appropriations, to assess their current status and progress;
strengthen procurement planning and coordination with the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to avoid delays and ensure timely project implementation; and
establish regular monitoring on the balances of LDRRMF and the PPAs assigned to it for the effective utilization of funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LDRRMO and the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (LDRRMC) to revisit the LDRRMFIP in order to incorporate revisions that include pre-identified PPAs under the thematic areas of Disaster Response and Rehabilitation, and Recovery.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, the Municipal Accountant, and the Municipal Budget Officer ensure that any additional remuneration granted to JO personnel is strictly supported by a clear and appropriate legal basis.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
OIC-Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to:
review payroll records and remittance report to identify specific personnel whose Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth contributions were withheld but not remitted; and
conduct regular and timely reconciliation of amounts withheld from employees' salaries and government contributions against the actual remittances made to Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth.
OIC-Municipal Treasurer to:
coordinate immediately with Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth to resolve the issues surrounding the remittance of the prior years' balances, including system limitations; and
ensure that all contributions are remitted in full and on time to prevent accumulation of unremitted balances.
Human Resource Management Officer to:
require all JO personnel to register for Pag-IBIG membership upon hiring; and
ensure that account numbers are obtained and recorded before any deductions are made.

We recommended that the Chairperson of the GFPS-TWG:
conduct gender analysis of the attributed PPAs through the administration of the HGDG tool during the project identification and preparation of GAD AR to determine whether the attributed GAD PPAs entirely address gender related issues, and submit the corresponding scores; and
establish, enhance, strengthen and maintain a gender responsive evaluation system to monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs.
We also recommended that the Chairperson of the GFPS-TWG and the Municipal Budget Officer separate non-GAD expenditures from the specific line-item allocations for GAD and clearly itemize each GAD program with its corresponding amount in the Annual Budget.

1.9 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
d. Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct the complete and timely
physical inventory of all Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE) of the
municipality. Thereafter, the Committee shall prepare the consolidated
RPCPPE, incorporating therein the cost/valuation provided by the
Municipal Assessor below, and submit a copy of such report to the Audit
Team, copy furnished the Municipal Accountant & GSO.
e. Mandate the Municipal Assessor to assist the Inventory Committee in
conducting an assessment of the current values of PPE assets
inventoried, or assign values if these are not available, and prepare a
report thereon for the information of the Inventory Committee.
f. Direct the GSO to prepare and maintain the property records for all PPE
inventoried.
1.10 We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant update and
reconcile the accounting and subsidiary records based on the RPCPPE, and
the report of assessed valuation/values submitted by the Municipal Assessor.
Adjustments to correct variances in the accounting records should be
justified and its reasons properly examined in order to ensure that the
accuracy of the valuation and balances of PPE accounts are duly supported.

2.9. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor mandate the Municipal
Accountant-Designate and the Municipal Budget Officer to coordinate their
efforts to prepare and submit the Statement of Comparison of Budget and
Actual Amounts (SCBAA), in accordance with the prescribed format under
COA Circular No. 2016-004. Henceforth, the two responsible officials should
ensure the necessary reconciliation between accounting and budget records
on a regular basis.
2.10. We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that concerned
personnel are provided with adequate training and technical support so that
responsibilities to report the LGUs fiscal operations are performed
appropriately and efficiently.

3.11. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the Municipal
Development Council, together with the Municipal Planning and
Development Officer and the Municipal Engineer, ensure the continued and
expedited implementation of LGU projects within the year. Further, they
should endeavor to earlier identify the underlying causes of project non-
implementation so that appropriate measures can be timely adopted to
facilitate the completion of all projects within the same fiscal year.
3.12. The management was amenable to the observation.

4.17. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor mandate the LGU personnel
assigned in the implementation of the AKAP to:
a. strictly comply with the documentary requirements embodied in DSWD
Memorandum Circular No. 30 series of 2024;
b. strengthen the verification process to confirm the legitimacy of the
beneficiaries and ensure that they are genuinely eligible;
c. ensure that the personnel handing the registration and distribution
process are adequately trained to assess and verify documents in order to
reduce the risk of errors and fraud in the beneficiary selection process;
and,
d. move towards digital listing of beneficiaries which would facilitate easy
verification of records, thereby reducing the risk of double listing of
beneficiaries.
4.18. We likewise recommended that the Municipal Accountant record in the
books the Utilization Reports on cash advances, only if these are supported
with pertinent necessary documents.

5.6. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor stop the grant of cash advances
to pay for items of expenditures enumerated in COA Circular No. 97-002
that shall instead be paid by checks or procured based on the procedures
prescribed in RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR.
5.7. The management agreed to implement the audit recommendation.

6.7. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the lacking
supporting documents on or before April 30, 2025 to avoid the issuance of a
notice of disallowance on the payments noted in Annex A of this observation.
6.8. We also recommended that the HRMO strengthen payroll control and
implement a strict verification process over the submission of required
documents before claims for salaries and wages can be submitted for
processing.

7.7. We recommended that management increase the amount of fidelity bond of
the Disbursing Officer to correspond to his accountability with the Fidelity
Bond Division of the Bureau of the Treasury, to properly safeguard against
loss due to theft, robbery, and other unforeseen events.
7.8. The management expressed their commitment to comply with the audit
recommendation.

8.6 We recommended that the Municipality collect the underpaid personal share
contributions from employees and immediately remit the amount of
₱54,000.00 for both personal and government shares to PagIBIG Fund, in
order to avoid the incurrence of penalties. Henceforth, the Municipal
Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, shall ensure that
the correct rate of Pag-IBIG personal and government share contributions
are computed, withheld, and remitted, so that employees can enjoy full
benefits and eligibility under the Fund.
8.7 The Management acknowledged the observation and expressed their agreement to
comply with the audit recommendation.

9.6 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor mandate the HRMO and the
Authorized Approving Officer to approve only those loan applications
wherein the net take home pay of the employee, after computation, does not
fall below ₱5,000.00, as required in the GAA.
9.7 We also recommended that Accountant strengthen internal control on the
issuance of certification on the net take home pay of employees for the
purpose of availing loans from any lending institutions.
9.8 The HRMO and the Municipal Accountant-Designate expressed their agreement
to comply with the audit recommendation.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for each road network to ensure proper accounting of the Road Network account and for easy reconciliation of the Report on Physical Count of Local Road Networks with accounting records.
We also recommended that the Municipal General Services Officer (GSO):
Maintain a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and their components, along with a Local Road Inventory and Road Map; and
Prepare and submit a comprehensive Report of Physical Count of Local Road Networks to the Municipal Accountant. This report will facilitate reconciliation and resolution of any discrepancies.
We further recommended that the Municipal Engineer provide the Municipal Accountant and the GSO with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for all road projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Head of the Agency, assign an adequate number of employees to the Inventory Committee to ensure the completion of the physical inventory and inventory report within three months or less.
We reiterated our audit recommendations in CY 2023 that the Municipal Accountant:
Maintain a complete and updated PPE and Real Property Ledger Cards to ensure that all PPEs are properly accounted for and to easily facilitate the reconciliation of the RPCPPE with accounting records;
Reconcile the PPELC with the RPCPPE, upon submission by the inventory committee, wherein differences noted should be verified and the corrections be made, where necessary
Identify the composition of the unidentified PPEs totaling ₱31,728,208.78 in order to provide details of the specific assets acquired by the municipality; and
Reclassify the incorrectly recorded PPEs (Table 1) to their appropriate asset accounts.
We also recommended that the GSO prepare the Report of Physical Count of Property and Equipment (RPCPPE) as soon as the physical count is concluded and submit the same to the Audit Team and the Municipal Accountant within 5 days from receipt of this memorandum.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the debit (negative) balance of the Due to NGA account; and
Review and reconcile the Subsidiary Ledgers for the Due to NGA account to ensure that accurate balances are reported in the Statement of Financial Position.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
correct the erroneous posting of accounts by transferring the unexpended LDRRMF balance from the Trust Liability account to the Trust Liability – DRRM account in the Trust Fund books;
conduct a thorough reconciliation of the Trust Liability – DRRM account to identify and record any unaccounted balances from prior years, in order to determine the cause of the account’s negative balance; and
regularly monitor and reconcile both the General Fund and Trust Fund accounts to prevent similar discrepancies in the future to ensure accurate presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant:
double check or re-compute the amounts reported in the Reports of Collections and Deposit before reflecting them in the cashbook and Cash in Local Treasury account;
accurately record the financial transactions in the cashbook and accounting books; and
conduct periodic reconciliation of both records and make the necessary adjustments to correct the identified errors.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Local Sangguniang Bayan to formulate and pass a local GAD code or ordinances, with the assistance of the LGU GFPS, to support the localization of the Magna Carta of Women and to ensure that GAD is mainstreamed in local policy-making and integrated in local development plans.
We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System, thru its Chairperson:
Institutionalize the GAD database, in coordination with the OIC Municipal Planning and Development Officer, with adequate and systematically gathered sex-disaggregated data which will provide the bases for gender analysis, planning, programming and policy formulation;
Undertake gender analysis to identify and confirm existing gender issues and ensure that the programs, projects and activities are responsive to the said issues; and
Revisit Annex B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01, which identifies the data requirements for particular gender issues.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the Head of the Human Resource Management Office and the Municipal Budget Officer, formulate and implement appropriate measures to address the delayed release of wages for Job Order (JO) personnel.  Proper planning and budgeting for JO personnel should be strictly observed. Any additional hiring should be supported by adequate appropriation to prevent funding shortfalls and delays in salary payments.
Furthermore, we recommended that Management periodically assess the necessity and cost-effectiveness of hiring JO personnel to ensure that staffing requirements are met without compromising budgetary constraints.  In cases where additional hiring is deemed necessary, management should proactively initiate the timely preparation and approval of a supplemental budget to cover the corresponding expenditures.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
ensure that essential LGU properties are covered under the General Insurance Fund (GIF) of the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS), in compliance with R.A. No. 656 and the guidelines outlined in COA Circular 2018-002; and
in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer, prioritize and allocate sufficient funds in future budgets to ensure comprehensive property insurance coverage.
In the future application of insurance with GSIS GIF, we also recommended that the General Service Officer prepare and submit the PIF to GSIS not later than April 30 of each year. Furnish copy of the PIF and proof of submission to GSIS to the COA Audit Team for evaluation of the actions taken.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a periodic review of taxes withheld on compensation to enable immediate changes where discrepancies or errors are identified, thereby ensuring adherence to tax regulations.
We also recommended that management, thru the Municipal Accountant, allow regular training to relevant staff on current tax laws and BIR pronouncements to ensure ongoing compliance and prevent future instances of over-withholding.

We recommended that the Accounting Unit:
Conduct a detailed review of the LGU employees’ deductions against the remittances made to GSIS, PhilHealth, and Pag-IBIG Fund for premium contributions and loan amortization to reconcile the identified over-remittances totaling ₱38,875.08; and
Regularly review the schedule of premium remittance and loan repayments as deducted from personnel payroll to prevent under/over remittance of premium contributions and loan amortizations.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant meticulously review the payroll deductions recorded in the Due to GSIS, Due to PhilHealth, and Due to Pag-IBIG subsidiary ledgers. These records should be reconciled with the actual amounts payable to each agency before processing of payment to ensure accurate and complete remittance of all contributions.

1.20 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue an Order mandating the -
a) Municipal Engineer to:
i. Conduct an inspection and inventory of the road networks within the
jurisdiction of the municipality, with the assistance of the Municipal
Assessor and Supply/Property Officer, making sure to segregate each
road asset component, including obtaining information as to its
location and actual condition.
ii. Prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network
System, adding therein the assessed values provided by the Municipal
Assessor, and copy furnish the COA, the Municipal Accountant and
the Supply/Property Unit for purposes of reconciliation of records.
b) Municipal Assessor to:
i. Assist the Municipal Engineer, Supply/Property Officer during the
conduct of inspection/inventory of the road lot components of all road
networks within the jurisdiction of the Municipality.ii. Assess the cost of the road network/lot components, assigning such
values to the individual items identified during the inventory.iii. Provide the Municipal Engineer with the assessed values on the road
network/lot components identified, for the preparation of the
inventory report.c) Supply/Property Officer to prepare and maintain the Local Road Network
Property Cards based on the details obtained from the inventory report
furnished by the Municipal Engineer.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain the Local Road
Network Ledger Cards and subsidiary ledgers with complete description of each
road asset components, and disclose such information in the Notes to FS in the
ensuing years, pursuant to the requirement under COA Circular 2015-008. Lastly,
we recommend for the Municipal Accountant to prepare an adjusting entry to
reclassify the bridge with re-blocking totaling ₱2,014,000.00 to the Road Networks
account.

2.13. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary
adjusting entries to recognize the correct depreciation expense for the
Thermal Decomposition Device and to reclassify the building assets into the
appropriate account, in order to ensure the accuracy and fair presentation of
the affected account balances in the financial statements.

3.13 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant classify the current portion of the
Loans Payable –Domestic account totaling ₱2,997,857.16, or the amount of loan
amortization due for 12 months from the reporting date, as Current Liability in the
year-end Statement of Financial Position.

3.14 We further recommended that Municipal Accountant disclose in the Notes to
Financial Statements the details and information of the Loans Payable –Domestic,
to provide additional information to users of financial information.

4.13. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor immediately direct the
contractor to explain the cause of the delay, and impose the corresponding
liquidated damages when warranted, as required under the IRR of RA 9184.

4.14. We further recommended that the Municipal Engineer improve the
supervision, monitoring, and documentation mechanisms for infrastructure
projects, using the calibrated steps provided under GPPB Circular No. 03-
2019, so that bottlenecks, delays, and other issues in project implementation
are resolved in a timely manner.

5.7. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue a directive to establish a
policy that authorized deductions shall in no case reduce the employee’s
monthly net take home pay to an amount lower than Five Thousand Pesos
(₱5,000.00) to comply with the Authorized Deductions provisions under the
General Appropriations Act.

5.8. We also recommended that Municipal Accountant stop the practice of signing
loan applications and of issuing Certifications requested by employees to be
submitted to lending institutions, if the net take home pay of the requesting
employee is below Five Thousand Pesos (₱5,000.00).

6.6 We recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development
Coordinator, with the assistance of the GAD Focal Person, gather information
on gender statistics, age and sex-disaggregated data and other information as
GAD database to be used for systematic and efficient gender-responsive
planning, programming, and policy formulation of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor immediately issue an executive
order to:
a. Create an Inventory Committee for the conduct of a physical count of
all the PPEs owned by the LGU. Thereafter, the Committee shall
prepare the Report on the Physical Count of PPEs, incorporating
therein the cost/valuation provided by the Municipal Assessor below,
and submit a copy of such report to the Audit Team, copy furnished
the Municipal Accountant & the General Services Officer.
b. Mandate the Municipal Engineer to assist the Municipal Accountant
in identifying and confirming the location, existence, and condition of
PPE assets, including any of its individual components as in the case
of road networks.
c. Direct the General Services Officer to maintain property cards on the
assets inventoried.
d. Direct the Municipal Assessor to conduct an assessment of the current
values of PPE assets inventoried, or assign values if these are not
available, and prepare a report thereon for the information of the Inventory
Committee.
e. Mandate the Municipal Accountant to update the accounting and
subsidiary records based on the RPCPPE, information provided by
the Municipal Engineer, and the report of assessed valuation/values
submitted by the Municipal Assessor.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, being the head of the
Development Council, together with the MPDC and all the members of the
Council, carefully, adequately and strategically plan and identify priority
projects under the 20% DF such that these are readily implementable during
the year, thereby avoiding the constant revisions of initially identified PPAs
contained in the duly approved AIP.

We recommended that the MDRRM Officer incorporate the existing balance
of the Special Trust Fund into the development of the LDRRMFIP in
ensuing years.

We also recommended that the MDRRMO, in coordination with the MPDC
Office, strengthen the selection process and prioritization of PPAs charged to
the LDRRMF through careful analysis and consideration of potential
disaster events or emerging hazardous situations that might threaten the
LGU and its corresponding capability to handle the risks of disasters and
other hazards.

We recommended that the LGU stop the practice of using the DRRM Fund for
PPAs that does not fall under nature, design, and description of the disaster-related
thematic areas, so that the LDRRM Fund is optimally utilized.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain subsidiary records
for roads and road components for each road network to ensure proper
accounting of the Road Network account and for easy reconciliation of the
Report on Physical Count of Local Road Networks with accounting records.

We also recommended that the Municipal General Services Officer (GSO):

a. Maintain a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and
their components, along with a Local Road Inventory and Road Map; and
b. Prepare and submit a comprehensive Report of Physical Count of Local
Road Networks to the Municipal Accountant. This report will facilitate
reconciliation and resolution of any discrepancies.

We further recommended that the Municipal Engineer provide the Municipal
Accountant and the GSO with the complete description and cost segregation
of road components for all road projects.

To improve the accounting and control over the Property, Plant and
Equipment account, we reiterated our CY 2023 audit recommendations with
modification that the Municipal Accountant:

Maintain complete and updated PPE Ledger Cards for all PPE items to ensure
proper accountability and facilitate reconciliation with the RPCPPE; 

Reclassify the misclassified PPE items to their correct accounts as detailed in
Table 1; 

Record the omitted assets totaling ₱13,890,199.00 and compute their
corresponding depreciation expense; and

Reconcile the PPE Ledger Cards with the RPCPPE wherein any discrepancies
should be verified and corrected promptly.

We also reiterated our previous recommendations, that the Municipal General
Services Officer conduct a complete physical count of all LGU PPE, including
items under the Buidlings and Other Structures account. The completed
RPCPPE should be submitted to the Accounting Unit once the physical count
is concluded.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

a. prepare the corresponding adjustments in the Trust Fund book to
record the unrecorded Debit/Credit Memos and disbursements totaling
₱3,273.06 and ₱23,351.70, respectively;

b. prepare monthly Bank Reconciliation Statement for all Trust Fund
accounts and submit them to the Audit Team; and

c. verify and reconcile the unaccounted discrepancies of ₱587,976.30 and
₱2,035,881.83 in the Trust Fund and General Fund accounts,
respectively, and prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer
submit the lacking Disbursement Vouchers and their supporting documents
within 5 days from receipt of this memorandum. This is without prejudice to
the issuance of Notices of Suspension and/or Disallowance for non-compliance
thereof.

Henceforth, we also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that
all Disbursement Vouchers and their supporting documents are submitted to
the Office of the Auditor within the reglementary period.

We commended the efforts made by Management in the efficient and effective
accomplishment of the identified priority development projects in CY 2024.

Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all personnel
concerned continuously adhere to the provisions of the aforementioned DBM-
DOF-DILG JMC, to ensure the optimal achievement of the Municipality’s
socio-economic development and environmental management goals while
effectively utilizing the 20% Development Fund for its intended purposes.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee, thru the BAC Chairman:

Comply strictly with the legal guidelines for Early Procurement Activities; and

Discontinue the practice of awarding contracts before the approval of the Annual
Budget to avoid financial and legal implications.

We recommended that the Mayor, as Chairman of the Municipal
Development Council, and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer:

a. Submit a written justification why the PPAs in Table 10 were
implemented despite their non-inclusion in the AIP;

b. Conduct detailed engineering of all projects, with the assistance of the
Municipal Engineer, prior to its inclusion in the AIP which includes the
preparation of the Program of Work with the Detailed Estimates and
plans/specifications to facilitate thorough planning and monitoring of
the projects’ implementation; and

c. Ensure that all PPAs implemented are included in the approved
Annual Investment Plan of the municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer:

a.) Strictly enforce the project durations stipulated in the contracts;

b.) Diligently inspect completed projects immediately after the reported
date of completion to verify timeliness, taking considerations on
Variation Orders or Time Extension, if any; and 

c.) Ensure that all materials and procedures comply with the Program of
Work, Detailed Estimates and Plans.

We also recommended the Municipal Accountant impose Liquidated
Damages, equal to one-tenth (1/10) of one percent (1%) of the cost of the
unfinished portion of the project for each day of delay.

Also, to safeguard government interests and prevent further losses, we
recommend implementing the following measures for future projects:

a.) Strictly monitor all project implementation (infrastructure, goods or
consulting services) to ensure adherence to approved plans and
specifications within the time frame specified in the contract
agreement; and

b.) Identify contracts requiring management actions. If so, immediately
impose appropriate remedies, including liquidated damages, contract
termination and blacklisting of contractors for failure to comply with
their obligations in accordance with the provisions of Sections 68 and
69, and Appendix 17 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184.

We recommended that the MDRRM Officer and Municipal Budget Officer
refrain from using the LDRRMF for expenses that are not related to disaster
risk reduction and management.

To ensure effective functioning of the MDRRM Office, we also recommended
that the MDRRM Officer submit a budget proposal Management detailing the
necessary funds for the maintenance and operation of the MDRRM Office, as
well as any required capital outlay.

We recommended that the municipality secure the applicable NTC permits to
purchase, possess and operate the radio communication equipment and
purchase only from licensed suppliers/dealers who have the necessary permits
from NTC, in compliance with Republic Act No. 584 and NTC Memorandum
Circulars.

We recommended that the HRMO and Accounting Unit:

Conduct a detailed review of the LGU employees’ deductions against GSIS billing to
reconcile the under remittances of GSIS premium contributions and loan
amortization totaling ₱177,133.67;


Henceforth, regularly update personnel records in the GSIS system to prevent
under/over remittance of premium contributions and loan amortizations; and 

Facilitate the remittance of noted unremitted contributions totaling ₱347,360.02 to
GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant carefully review the payroll
deductions recorded in the Due to GSIS, Due to PhilHealth, and Due to
Pag-IBIG subsidiary ledgers. These should be compared with the actual
amounts due to each agency before processing payments to ensure accurate and
complete remittance of all contributions.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

a. Immediately compute the correct tax liability for each affected employee and
official, encompassing the previously excluded Service Recognition Incentive
(SRI) and Productivity Enhancement Incentive (PEI). The corresponding
under withheld amount should be reflected in a payroll adjustment and
remitted to the BIR as soon as possible, along with any applicable penalties or
interest; and 

b. ensure accurate differentiation between taxable and non-taxable income in
accordance with BIR regulations and regularly review payroll tax computation
to avoid penalties and interest due to under withholding.

We also recommended that the management, thru the Municipal Accountant,
allow regular training for relevant staff on current tax laws and BIR
pronouncements to ensure ongoing compliance and prevent future under
withholding.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that all insurable LGU
properties are covered under the General Insurance Fund (GIF) of the
Government Service Insurance System (GSIS), in compliance with R.A. No. 656
and the guidelines outlined in COA Circular 2018-002.


In the future application of insurance with the GSIS GIF, we also
recommended that the Municipal General Services Officer prepare the PIF
and submit the same to GSIS not later than April 30 of each year to ensure
sufficiency of insurance coverage and protection in case of unforeseen natural
or man-made hazards. Furnish copy of the PIF and proof of submission to
GSIS to the COA Audit Team for evaluation of the actions taken.

We recommended that the Chairperson of the GAD Focal Point System, in
coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Office, establish
the LGU’s GAD Database for gender analysis in the preparation of the LGU’s
GAD plan to be able to come up with gender-responsive programs that can
address current and relevant gender-related issues.

We recommended that the Municipal Social Worker Development Officer
(MSWDO) and other personnel listed in Table 18 submit the required
documentation to support their hazard pay claims. This documentation should
include a travel or service report detailing duties involving exposure to
significant dangers, occupational risks, or physical hardships, or any other
applicable documents. This recommendation is without prejudice to the
issuance of a notice of suspension or disallowance if warranted.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough
review of all hazard pay claims to ensure they are fully supported by the
necessary documentation before processing any payments.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the misclassified PPE item in the books of accounts; and
Property Custodian to prepare the PCs with complete details, such as providing the date of acquisition/ issue/ transfer/ disposal of PPE; description of PPE; the balance of the PPE at year end; and amount of PPE.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Inventory Committee and the Municipal Accountant undertake collaborative procedures to ensure that all PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective records and that the PCs maintained by the Property Custodian and the PELCs maintained by the Accounting Office are reconciled.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the:
Municipal Engineer to regularly coordinate and submit the Final Inspection Report, Statement of Work Accomplished, As- Built Plans, Certificate of Turnover and pictures of the completed projects together with the Certificate of Completion to the Municipal Accounting Office, to ensure that completed projects are recorded in the books of accounts.
The Municipal Accountant to:
prepare the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the cost of the eight (8) completed projects in the books of accounts of the Municipality based on the Certificate of Completion and other aforementioned documents submitted by the Municipal Engineering Office;
ensure timely payment and recording of completed projects; and
exert effort to trace the unidentified balances in the “CIP” accounts presented in the books of accounts of the Municipality.
We also recommended that Municipal Mayor, thru the Municipal Engineer, to write a letter addressed to the contractors notifying them of their unbilled completed projects.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee regularly conduct the physical count of inventories and prepare the required RPCI. Submit to the Audit Team copy of the report within the prescribed period.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant record initially the procured inventory items in the appropriate Inventory account and recognize the Expense account upon issuance to the end-users supported with the SSMI and RIS.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor thru the Municipal PESO-Designate and Municipal Agricultural Officer hasten the implementation of the Livelihood Project funded by the DOLE and GPBP projects funded by the DA for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
make adjusting journal entries to correct the mathematical formula error in the ‘Due to Bureau of Treasury- Cattle Registration Fee/ Building Permit Fee’ account in the Due to NGAs- General Fund;
draw journal entries to transfer the Municipality’s 20% share from Fire Code Fees to the Trust Liabilities account;
draw the necessary journal entries to reclassify to the ‘Subsidy from National Government’ account the remaining National ADAC Performance Award received by the Municipality from the DILG; and
initiate the confirmation of existence of the unutilized and unreturned funds reflected in the ‘Due to NGAs’ accounts with identified Source Agencies through sending of confirmation letters. For funds with unidentified Source Agencies, effort should be exerted to identify whether these were non-moving for ten (10) years or more from the last transaction recorded in the books to consider its dormancy. Initiate request for write-off if these balances are warranted as dormant in compliance with the procedures set by the Commission on Audit.
We reiterated our CY 2023 recommendation that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer expedite the preparation of the fund utilization reports of all implemented and completed programs/ projects/ activities (PPA) funded by National Government Agencies. It is also recommended to promptly return the unexpended balances to extinguish the Municipality’s liability in the books of accounts, so that the funds can be put to use for other equally important projects of the Source Agencies.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the HRM Officer to:
Inform the concerned municipal officials/ personnel on the loan payments totaling P23,663.84 irregularly paid by the Municipality and consequently require them to refund the payments made; and
Conduct reconciliation with the records of the GSIS to determine of already paid and unpaid loans of the employees and make the necessary actions on the already paid loan but with corresponding deduction from the employee’s salary, and
Promptly coordinate with the GSIS to update the municipal personnel’s employment status and/ or salary adjustments. This will ensure that GSIS records reflect accurate and up-to-date information.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Verify   the accuracy of the “Due to GSIS” balance amounting to P532,693.51 to determine the employee and amount the unremitted contribution it is attributed to and remit immediately to GSIS;
Remit immediately to GSIS the unremitted CY 2024 contributions of municipal employees amounting to P21,528.84.
Submit proof of refund made by the concerned municipal officials/ personnel for loans irregularly paid by the Municipality totaling P23,663.84.
Lastly, we also recommended that the Payroll-in-Charge to prepare the monthly payroll and ensure that salaries of municipal officials and personnel are deducted with the correct amounts due for remittance to the GSIS.  She   should also ensure that these deductions tally with the monthly GSIS billing.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the accounting records to verify the accuracy of the unsettled balance of P638,681.19 in the “Due to PhilHealth” account for prior years. Any confirmed outstanding balance must be remitted to PhilHealth immediately.
We also recommended that the HRM Officer:
ensure the full and timely remittance of the amounts withheld from officials and personnel as well as the share provided by the Municipality in accordance with the deadline set by PhilHealth;
immediately remit to the PhilHealth the total withheld contributions of Mr. Teraza and of the other municipal employees which remained unremitted as of this writing; and
inquire with the PhilHealth regarding the unremitted premiums indicated in the SPA to immediately resolve the deficiencies noted.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
To review the accounting records to verify the accuracy of the prior years’ unremitted balance of P103,768.50 under the “Due to Pag-IBIG” account. Any confirmed outstanding obligations should be remitted to Pag-IBIG immediately, and necessary adjustments should be made in the books, if applicable.
To ensure the proper and timely recording in the books of the Pag-IBIG contributions withheld from employees, including the government share, and the corresponding remittances thereof.
We also recommended that the HRM Officer:
ensure the full and timely remittance of the amounts withheld from officials and personnel as well as the share provided by the Municipality in accordance with the deadline set by Pag-IBIG;
immediately remit to the Pag-IBIG the total withheld contributions of Mr. Teraza and of the other municipal employees that remained unremitted; and
Conduct reconciliation with the records of Pag-ibig to determine of unfulfilled obligations both of the employee and the LGU.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the Municipal Engineer and MPDC ensure that the PPAs under the   20% DF are well-planned, as well as procurement-and-implementation-ready such that the factors that may impede implementation, such as lot ownership concerns shall be considered.
For the abandoned road concreting projects, we also recommended that the:
Municipal Engineer verify the acts or causes which may constitute the ground(s) for termination and cause the execution of a Verified Report, with all relevant evidence attached.
Municipal Mayor:
upon recommendation by the Municipal Engineer, terminate the concerned contracts by a written notice to the Contractor conveying the termination of the contract; and
ensure completion of the abandoned projects through take-over of contracts or through implementation by administration.
Lastly, we recommended that the members of the Sangguniang Bayan, headed by the Municipal Vice Mayor, and the members of the executive body of the Municipality, headed by the Municipal Mayor, undertake collaborative measures to ensure the prompt enactment of the municipal annual budget. This is to facilitate the timely implementation of the budget and the realization of priority development projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Through the LDRRM Council ensure full utilization of LDRRMF thru implementation of all approved PPAs incorporated in the LDRRMF Investment Plan.
Through the LDRRM Council and Municipal Budget Officer:
ensure that expenditure items to be charged to the 5% LDRRMF are in the annual LDRRMFIP, otherwise prepare a revised AIP as approved by the local sanggunian and Local Chief Executive upon recommendation of the LDRRM Council before release and utilization of unexpended funds for new expenditure items in compliance with Item 6.5 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013; and
stop charging ineligible expenditure items to the 5% LDRRMF in compliance with Items 5.1 to 5.4 of the aforementioned JMC.
This is without prejudice to the issuance of the Notice of Disallowance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the members of the GFPS Technical Working Group (TWG) and GAD Focal Person to:
prepare the GAD Agenda in accordance with the mandate of 4.C.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01;
monitor actual expenditures to ensure the Municipality has sufficient funds for implementing all GAD activities and coordinate with the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant in preparing the annual GAD Utilization Report; and
refrain from charging non-GAD-related activities to the GAD budget thru coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that expenses excluded from the GPB and those not clearly addressing a specific gender issue are not charged to the GAD Budget.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
Examine the amounts of the tax withheld and tax due upon preparation of the report of Annual Tax on Compensation in order to identify errors that need to be adjusted at year-end; and
Inquire with the BIR on the appropriate actions to be taken for the erroneously withheld and remitted January 2024 tax of Mr. M. Buyser.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and Municipal Treasurer refund the total penalties paid to the BIR amounting to P33,301.56 due to non- remittance of CY 2022 withholding taxes within the prescribed period and submit to the COA Audit Team copy of the Official Receipt as proof of the refund made.

We recommended that the Local School Board, headed by the Municipal Mayor:
earnestly submit the quarterly and annual utilization reports of the SEF to the DepEd Central Office through its appropriate channel, copy furnished the local Sanggunian concerned and Regional Offices of the DBM and DILG within the prescribed period; and
post the utilization reports of the SEF for all quarters of the year in the Municipal website and/ or in at least three (3) conspicuous places within the indicated timelines.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the HRM Officer stipulate in the Job Order worker’s Contract of Service the allowable amount of traveling expenses based on the rates and terms agreed upon by the JO worker and the Municipality.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that travel claims of JO workers are supported with a certification from the Head of the Agency or any authorized approving official stating that official missions/ tasks could not be performed by/ or assigned to any other regular/permanent official and/ or employee; and that the tasks/ activities are necessary to fulfill the obligations as contained in their contract of service.
Lastly, we also recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that the wages of Job Order (JO) workers comply with the mandated daily minimum wage rate.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the Property Custodian to:
Prepare annually the consolidated Property Inventory Form listing all the insurable properties and other assets of the Municipality; and
Submit the consolidated PIF to the Audit Team Leader and to the GIF, GSIS, not later than April 30 of each year as required in COA Circular No. 2018-002 dated May 31, 2018 as basis for the assessment of general insurance coverage over all insurable assets, properties and interests of the Municipality with the GIF of the GSIS.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
reconcile the accounting records with the property unit and determine the causes of the variance for proper accounting thereof;
prepare and maintain PPELCs with complete significant information; and
prepare the necessary accounting entries to reclassify items costing below the P50,000.00 threshold for PPE and comply with the prescribed procedures relative to the increase in capitalization threshold as embodied in COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022.
Instruct the Supply Officer to prepare and maintain the property cards for each PPE account with complete significant information and reconcile the same with the ledger cards of the accounting unit; and
Require the Inventory Committee to conduct an annual physical count of the properties and prepare the RPCPPE with complete significant information in accordance with the prescribed format and reconcile the RPCPPE with the accounting records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:
Conduct a thorough review and validation of all long-outstanding accounts payable, particularly those aged more than one year, to determine their validity and whether the claims still exist and due for settlement;
Trace whether transactions have been erroneously recorded previously and prepare adjusting journal entries to correct the balance of the account; and
Henceforth, ensure that the Journal Entry Vouchers drawn for the recognition of payables should be consummated transactions supported with complete documents to prove that these are valid obligations.

We recommended that the BAC conduct a thorough post-qualification process to identify any non-disclosure of previously awarded municipal contracts and any omission of required information in the Statement of Ongoing Contracts, including those awarded but not yet started. Contractors found to be ineligible to undertake the project should be disqualified accordingly.

Also, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting journal entries to recognize the cost of completed projects, to its appropriate asset accounts and record the related depreciation expense in the books.

We recommended that the Human Resource and Management Officer communicate immediately to the GSIS any changes in the employees’ employment status to update the records of the GSIS and tally with the LGU records on premium contributions.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain subsidiary ledgers for the Due to GSIS account to properly monitor the unremitted amount of GSIS premium contributions and loan amortizations.

For future remittance, we recommended that the personnel in-charge with the preparation of the payroll accurately compute the amount of deduction of loan amortization in the payroll to match the amount billed by the GSIS.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile with PhilHealth records the unremitted premium contributions as of December 31, 2024 and the prior years amounting to P539,911.47 and P581,368.22, respectively.
We also recommended that the HRMO promptly submit updates on employees’ records to PhilHealth to ensure that the monthly billing statements align with the PhilHealth contributions deducted from the payroll.
Lastly, we also recommended that the personnel responsible for the preparation of the employees’ payroll ensure that the amount withheld from the employees is correct and is the same amount remitted.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Payroll Officer:
Reconcile their records for unremitted contribution in prior years, if any; and
Coordinate with Pag-IBIG to match the Agency’s records vis-à-vis the LGU to identify the source of the discrepancies.

Moreover, we recommend that the Payroll Officer:
Immediately settle any unpaid or under-remitted HDMF contributions, and
Henceforth, ensure that the total amount remitted matches with the actual premiums, loan amortizations, and MPL withheld from employees’ salaries, including the government’s share.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Accountant to:
reconcile the amounts of taxes withheld on government payments as against the actual remittances made to address the identified under or over-remittances and make adjustments, where necessary; and
ascertain the correct balance of unremitted taxes from previous years, if any, and maintain complete subsidiary records of all taxes.
Municipal Treasurer’s Office to strictly remit the taxes withheld in full and within the period prescribed under the Revenue Regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Human Resource Management Officer ensure that travel allowances are granted to JO personnel whose contracts explicitly authorize official travel.

Moreover, in the absence of stipulation in the contract as to the travel authority, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue an Executive Order establishing internal guidelines on travel claims for JO personnel or in the absence thereof, issue an Approved Travel Order stipulating the purpose of travel with the corresponding entitlements. Furthermore, the travel claims must be clearly specified in the contract and shall in no case exceed the rates and amounts prescribed under Executive Order No. 77, series of 2019.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant thoroughly review the travel claims of JO personnel to ensure that the same are supported with complete documentation, which includes a Certification from the Head of the Agency or any authorized approving official as required in Item 5 of the Specific Guidelines per COA Resolution No. 2021-044.

We recommended that the LGU comply with the required minimum 5% budget allocation for GAD.
We commended the adherence of the LGU on the submission of the GAD Plan and Budget to the DILG for review and endorsement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor take the initiative to ensure that all insurable LGU properties are covered under the General Insurance Fund (GIF) of the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS), in compliance with R.A. No. 656 and the guidelines outlined in COA Circular 2018-002 dated May 31, 2018 to mitigate the risk of losses resulting from natural and man-made calamities on its properties.
Likewise, we recommended that the Employee-in-Charge prepare and submit the Property Inventory Form (PIF) containing the list of all the insurable properties and other assets to the GSIS, showing their latest appraised values/valuation, appraisal date, location, and other information not later than April 30 of each year.

We recommended that the Local School Board comply with the timely submission of the SEF Approved Budget to the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Budget Officer which should not be later than seven days after the approval of the said Budget.

Also, we recommended that the Local School Board diligently comply with the reportorial requirements on the submission of the SEF Utilization Reports to the DepEd CO, through appropriate channels, copy furnished the local sanggunian concerned and Regional Offices of the DBM and DILG, in consonance with Item 6.1 of the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 dated January 19, 2017.

Due to the aforementioned observations which remained uncorrected, we reiterated our previous audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:
direct the Municipal Accountant to:
reconcile the accounting records with the property unit and determine the causes of the variance for proper accounting thereof;
compute and recognize depreciation on all of the depreciable assets of the Municipality;
prepare and maintain PPELCs with complete significant information; and
coordinate with the GSO-Designate/Supply Officer and undergo disposition procedures for non-existing/missing PPE.
require the Inventory Committee to:
complete the physical count of all properties as at year-end and henceforth, conduct an annual physical count of PPE; and
categorize the identified PPE in the RPCPPE based on its proper account classification and reconcile the RPCPPE with the accounting records.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Trace the composition of the unsubstantiated balances to determine if claims against the Municipality exist. If no claims exist, initiate the request for authority to write-off from COA.
Initiate the return of the unspent balances of completed PPAs to the source agencies.
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit funds from Other National Government Agencies for specific purpose in the Trust Fund account of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:
Stop the practice of recording accounts payable without complete supporting documents. Henceforth, the Journal Entry Vouchers drawn for the recognition of payables should be consummated transactions supported with complete documents to prove that these are valid obligations; and
Prepare adjusting journal entries to reclassify the amount of P38,745.80 as Leave Benefits Payable and ultimately correct the balance of the Accounts Payable account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Establish the correct balance of the Trust Liabilities – Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund account as of year-end through reconciliation and verification of prior records; and
Prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries, if required by the circumstances.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare prior period correcting entries to recognize the amount of Prepaid Insurance, Office Supplies, Other Supplies and Materials Inventory, Infrastructure Asset – Power Supply System and Buildings and Other Structures.

In addition, prepare journal entries to recognize accumulated depreciation in CY 2024 for the Infrastructure Asset – Power Supply System and Buildings and Other Structures.

Henceforth, for future transactions, strictly comply with International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) for the recording of financial transactions of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Establish the correct balance of the Biological Assets account through reconciliation of previous records, for its proper disposition in the books; and
Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to update the Biological Asset account, if warranted and duly supported with complete documentation.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare a journal entry to:
Reclassify the Construction in Progress – Infrastructure Asset amounting P5,997,999.65 to Infrastructure Asset – Road Networks.
Prepare prior period adjustment to recognize depreciation in prior years.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:
Trace the causes of the negative balance of the Due to BIR account under the General Fund for taxes withheld from suppliers in order to establish the accurate balance of the account as of December 31, 2024; and
Prepare adjusting journal entries to properly reflect the correct balances of the Due to BIR account per fund, if warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
Municipal Accountant to:
prepare the necessary accounting entries to adjust the balance of the Cash in Bank-Local Currency, Current Account due to incorrect charging of funds totaling P103,200.00;
make representation with the bank as to the credit memoranda amounting to P71,999.97 with unknown nature or purpose in order to determine its proper disposition in the books; and
prepare the appropriate adjusting journal entries to revert the stale checks to the Cash in Bank-Local Currency, Current Account and to recognize the appropriate payable/liability or equity accounts.
Municipal Treasurer to send written notices to the payees one month before the checks become stale. Stale checks shall be reported and attached to the Report of Checks Issued prepared at the period of cancellation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
ensure that appropriate journal entries are used to record payments for interest on loans; and
henceforth, see to it that the actual payments of interest reconcile with the recorded amounts in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
ascertain the correct balance of the receivable accounts by tracing the nature of the transaction and its pertinent supporting documents and prepare appropriate adjusting journal entries to arrive at the accurate balance of the account;
serve demand letters to the LGUs concerned after determination of the valid recipients/debtors, for immediate liquidation of fund transfers, particularly the long outstanding account; and
henceforth, conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables to ascertain the validity of the claims.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Supply Officer to:
maintain SCs on all inventories, reconcile the same with SLCs to validate the correctness and existence of inventories;
consolidate the RIS for which supplies and materials were issued using the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI).
Inventory Committee to:
conduct the physical count of inventories as of June 30 and
December 31, in accordance with regulations; and
prepare the RPCI in accordance with the prescribed format and copy furnish the Audit Team.
Municipal Accountant to:
record all procurement of supplies and materials as Inventories and its related expense upon issuance thereof; and
maintain SLCs on all Inventory accounts to substantiate the accuracy and correctness of recorded Inventories and reconcile the same with the supply records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MPDC and the Municipal Engineer to resolve the issues on the non-implementation of PPAs and fast track its implementation.

Henceforth, the PPAs that shall be approved and appropriated are well planned and implementation ready.

We recommended that the BAC Chairman and TWG to:
Submit the Statement of Work Accomplishment or any equivalent document to show the actual accomplishment of the said on-going projects as of December 17, 2024.
For future procurements, require the bidders to provide the percentage of completion and value of outstanding works in the Statement of All On-going Contracts pursuant to the prescribed format stated in the Philippine Bidding Documents; and
Conduct post-qualification more thoroughly for future procurements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to refrain from charging expenditures that are not within the four (4) thematic areas of disaster risk reduction and management such as disaster prevention and mitigation, disaster preparedness, disaster response, and disaster rehabilitation and recovery.
Municipal Accountant to:
establish the correct balance of the unspent DRRM Funds at the end of the year and ensure that all unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE shall be transferred to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability-DRRM” in the Trust Fund books; and
comply with the rules set forth under COA Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014 pertaining to the accounting guidelines for procured items out of the LDRRMF; and
trace the discrepancies noted in the balance per audit and per utilization report to ascertain the accuracy and reliability of financial information.
Municipal Treasurer to issue checks from the appropriate fund source in order to recompense the expenditures that were incorrectly charged from a different fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Payroll Officer:
Reconcile their records for the over-remittance of PhilHealth contribution in prior years amounting P87,671.16.
Reconcile their records for the unremitted premium contributions in CY 2024. If the result of the reconciliation proved that PhilHealth contributions deducted in the employees’ salaries are in excess of the amount billed by PhilHealth, return the over deductions to the employees.
Reconcile their records with PhilHealth for the unremitted premium contribution based on PhilHealth’s records.
For future remittance, we recommended that the Payroll Officer remit the PhilHealth contribution before due date to avoid incurrence of interest charge due to late remittance.

We recommended that the Human Resource and Management Officer communicate immediately to the GSIS any changes in the employees’ employment status to update the records of the GSIS and tally with the LGU records on premium contributions.

We recommended that the Payroll Officer:
Reconcile her records with the Municipal Accountant for the over-remittance of premium contributions in prior years and the unremitted premium contributions in CY 2024;
For the unremitted premium contribution in CY 2024, reconcile with the GSIS to come up with the accurate premium contribution due to the GSIS in CY 2024.
For the over remittance of premium contribution in prior years:
Withhold premium contributions from the employees’ salaries due from them in prior years.
Subsequently, if the result of reconciliation with the Municipal Accountant reveals that there are employees who are separated already with the LGU and were not deducted with their GSIS premiums or where deductions were insufficient in prior years, initiate claims for their GSIS premium contributions due from them in prior years. Otherwise, the personnel responsible for the payroll preparation and remittance to the GSIS will be held liable for the over-remittance.
Henceforth, always make sure that the amount of deduction in the employees’ payroll for their loan amortization is the same with the GSIS billing.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Reconcile his records with the Payroll Officer for the unremitted loan amortizations and CY 2024 and prior years
Subsequently, if the reconciliation proves that there are excess deductions of loan amortization on the employees’ salaries, initiate the return of excess deduction of loan amortization to the employees.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Payroll Officer to reconcile monthly the actual deductions in the payroll against the remittance and GSIS Billing to avoid discrepancies therein.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Payroll Officer:
Reconcile their records for unremitted contribution in prior years amounting  P159,041.51; and
Coordinate with Pag-IBIG to match the Agency’s billing records vis-à-vis the LGU to identify the source of the discrepancies.

Moreover, we recommended that the Payroll Officer:
Immediately remit any shortfall in HDMF contributions, and
Henceforth, ensure that the total amount remitted matches with the actual premiums, loan amortizations, and MPL withheld from employees’ salaries, including the government’s share.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the:
Municipal Accountant to:
Reconcile the beginning balance of the Due to BIR account for taxes withheld on government payments as against the actual remittances made to address the identified over-remittances and make adjustments, where necessary. Henceforth, conduct regular reconciliation of the balances; and
Ensure that the Revenue Official Receipts, Monthly Alpha List of Payees and other documents to support the remittance of withholding taxes to the BIR are attached in the Disbursement Vouchers.
Municipal Treasurer’s Office to strictly remit the taxes withheld in full and within the period prescribed under the Revenue Regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Require the Human Resource Management Officer to prepare contracts of agreement between the LGU and the job order workers, incorporating therein  the duration of the work to be undertaken by the JO personnel, the lump sum, piece of work or intermittent job to be performed as well as their authority to travel;
In the absence of stipulation in the contract as to the travel authority, issue an Executive Order establishing internal policies, guidelines, and limitations on travel claims for JO personnel or in the absence thereof, issue an Approved Travel Order stipulating the purpose of travel with the corresponding entitlements; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to thoroughly review the travel claims of JO personnel to ensure that the same are supported with complete documentation, which includes a Certification from the Head of the Agency or any authorized approving official as required in Item 5 of the Specific Guidelines per COA Resolution No. 2021-044.
We likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Budget Officer submit a written explanation/justification on the improper augmentation of funds without an Ordinance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor take the initiative to ensure that all insurable LGU properties are covered under the General Insurance Fund (GIF) of the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS), in compliance with R.A. No. 656 and the guidelines outlined in COA Circular 2018-002 dated May 31, 2018 to mitigate the risk of losses resulting from natural and man-made calamities.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer as the Employee-in-Charge:
Prepare and submit the Property Inventory Form (PIF) containing the list of all the insurable properties and other assets to the GSIS, showing their latest appraised values/valuation, appraisal date, location, and other information not later than April 30 of each year; and
Transfer insurance to the GSIS for the building insured with a private insurance company.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
GSO to initiate immediately the disposal of identified unserviceable PPEs following the disposal procedures set forth in COA-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2024-1 on the Revised Manual on the Disposal of Government Properties; and
Inventory Team to conduct physical count on the related PPE items totaling P119,320.00 and reconcile the result of inventory with the PPELC of the Municipal Accountant;
Municipal Accountant to:
Work with the GSO and Inventory Committee in reconciling the difference between the PPELC with the RPCPPE;
Follow the procedures in the derecognition of non-existing PPEs set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006; and
Take up the necessary accounting entries to derecognize PPEs once disposed based on original copies of the IIRUP and other supporting documents.
We further recommended that the concerned offices set a definite timeline or schedule of activities for the disposal procedures to ensure the success of the undertaking.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:
Require the Municipal Accountant to:
Verify the non-moving "Due to NGAs" account, reconcile the unsubstantiated balance, and implement necessary adjustments as warranted. Any confirmed unexpended balance must be immediately returned to the Source Agency.
Prepare the Fund Utilization Report on fully implemented fund transfers and return any unutilized balance.
Ensure that all disbursements from fund transfers are fully supported with complete documentation.
Direct all concerned Department Heads, particularly the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer, Municipal Agriculturist, Municipal Engineer, and Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, to expedite the implementation of projects, programs, or activities funded by the fund transfers.
Require the Municipal Agriculturist to make representation with the National Anti-Poverty Commission (NAPC) and secure confirmation on the approval of the revised line-item budget for the implementation of projects under the FY 2016 BuB-LGSF.
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to return the unexpended funds from unimplemented projects under FY 2016 BuB-LGSF to the Bureau of the Treasury if the approved revised line-item budget for the project implementation is not secured from NAPC.
