observations.recommendation: The Municipality implemented 20 out of 27 priority PPAs under the 20% Development Fund or an implementation rate of 66.67%, and utilized P22,397,006.66 out of P25,291,077.29 total appropriation or a utilization rate of 88.56%, while the unexpended balances of completed projects totaling P694,070.63 as of December 31, 2023, were  not reverted to the unappropriated surplus, contrary to DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020, and Section 322 of Republic Act No. 7160, thus funds have become idle and  not used to implement other development projects to  address the constantly evolving development needs of the respective constituents.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor, through the Local Development Council (LDC) and Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to prioritize in the planning and approval of PPAs that are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready.  Conduct due diligence before a purchase of a lot is funded to ensure that the lots are free from any claims to avoid interruptions in the implementation. 

We also recommended to the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Budget Officer and the MPDC to include in the annual budget preparation, the review of continuing appropriation unexpended balances and those PPAs that cannot be implemented, so that these could be reverted to the unappropriated surplus and re-appropriated to other priority PPAs embodied in the Annual Investment Program in order to prevent idle funds.

observations.recommendation: Deficiencies were noted in the conduct of the procurement through public bidding such as the failure: (1) to send an invitation to the observer 5 days prior the date of procurement activity, (2) to conduct a pre-bid conference for procurements with Approved Budget for the Contract above 1 million; (3) to require submission of latest Income Tax and Business Tax Returns for post-qualification; and (4) to disqualify the bidder despite the negative slippage in its previous contracts and misrepresentation of facts in the List of On-going Government Contracts, contrary pertinent provisions of Section 13, 22, and 34 of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184, that undermined the transparency and competitiveness of the bidding, thus, the procurement activities may be considered irregular.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the BAC Chairperson, the following:

Send a written invitation to observers at least 5 days before each procurement activity. In the event that a procurement activity has to be postponed, the observers shall be notified immediately of the change in schedule, either through email or by personal service. 

Submit justification on the failure to conduct a pre-bid conference on the two Public Bidding: Construction of Commercial Building B Phase II and Improvement/Rehabilitation of Felicity Dream Square. Henceforth, conduct pre-bid conference for contracts to be bid with an ABC of One Million Pesos (₱1,000,000.00) or more.

Require the submission of the latest Quarterly Income Tax Return and Business Tax Return within five (5) calendar days from receipt by the bidder of the notice from the BAC that the bidder has the Lowest Calculated Bid or Highest Rated Bid.

Carefully verify, validate, and ascertain all statements made in the documents submitted by the bidder so that any misrepresentations will be detected and to declare as eligible only those who strictly comply with the provisions of the RIRR of RA 9184.

Submit justification why the contractor was awarded with five (5) new contracts despite having negative slippage and misrepresenting itself by not declaring all its on-going contracts.

observations.recommendation: Deficiencies on the grant of Hazard Pay, Subsistence Allowance and Laundry Allowance to Public Health Workers such as: (a) complete documentary requirements for payment of Hazard Pay were not complied, (b) Hazard Pay amounting P10,532.00 was paid despite non-qualification of personnel, and (c) Subsistence and Laundry Allowances were paid at a monthly fixed amount rather than on actual workdays rendered contrary to DBM-DOH Joint Circular No. 1 s. 2012 dated November 29, 2012 and its amendment and Item 5.8 of COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 thus, appropriateness of the grant of hazard pay is doubtful and the payment of undue benefits.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Health Officer:

issue a Special Order covering the assignment of public health workers to hazardous/difficult areas, 

secure Certification by the Secretary of the DOH that the place of assignment/travel is a strife-torn/embattled/disease-infested/distress or isolated areas/stations, or areas declared under state of calamity or emergency, or with volcano activity or eruption, and

attach the complete supporting documents to the disbursement vouchers.

We likewise recommended the Municipal Accountant approve disbursement vouchers that are supported with complete documentation and strictly observe the guidelines on the grant of hazard pay, subsistence and laundry allowances.

This is without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.

observations.recommendation: Despite the absence of clear-cut hiring policies, the Municipality hired a total of 63 Job Order (JO) personnel which represents 38% of the total manpower, while wages paid to them were below the regional daily minimum wage rate, and payment of wages totaling P1,452,198.15 and P2,992,961.34 were not supported with DTRs and Accomplishment Reports, respectively, contrary to Section 76 of RA 7160, RTWPB Wage Order No. RB VIII-22, and COA Circular No. 2012-001 thus, risk of duplication of duties was not mitigated and cast doubt as to the validity and regularity of the payments made.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

X x x

X x x

Comply with the existing daily minimum wage rate for Region VIII, as prescribed by the RTWPB;

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

1. instruct the Inventory Committee to (i) conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of PPE owned by the Municipality, identifying the proper classification, individual identification, status of serviceability, accountability and location thereof; (ii) prepare and submit a complete RPCPPE to the Municipal Accountant not later than January 31 of each year, copy furnish the Audit Team; and (iii) prepare and submit IIRUP together with its supporting documents for disposal of unserviceable properties;

2. require the Municipal Accountant to (i) expedite substantiation of recorded unidentified PPE items based on the result of inventory count as recommended above; (ii) drop from the PPE account the identified items below the ₱15,000.00 threshold totalling ₱2,524,215.01; (iii) initiate the proper turn-over of the completed projects to the respective Barangay-Recipients supported with the properly accomplished turn-over documents, and effect adjustments in the books; and (iv) maintain and update PPE Ledger Cards for each category of assets and Real Property Ledger Cards for land; and

3. consider the creation of a General Services Officer or direct the Municipal Treasurer, in the absence of a duly appointed GSO, take charge in setting up and updating of Property Cards for proper monitoring, control and accountability of purchased/ acquired and issued PPEs.

4. Lastly, we recommended that the Municipality avail the one-time cleansing of the PPE Account and strictly adhere with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability

6. report to the Municipal Mayor any project/program which cannot be immediately implemented for proper disposition or referral to the Source Agency/ies.

observations.recommendation:

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

secure immediately an authorization from the Sanggunian Bayan, by way of ratification, for the transactions totaling ₱1,900,000.00 which were charged against lump-sum and generic appropriations of the 20% Development Fund;
specify in the Annual Investment Plan, together with the Local Development Council, the PPAs to be funded from the 20% Development Fund and avoid lump-sum and generic appropriations therein, otherwise, obtain the corresponding SB Resolution prior to the implementation of such PPAs; and
oblige the recipient barangays to submit liquidation documents to ensure that the funds released were only utilized for the development projects

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the grant of financial assistance out of the 20% Development Fund in the total amount of ₱1,900,000.00 in Due from LGUs and Prior Years’ Adjustment accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the heads of departments and operating units, most especially the HRMO, assess the agency-wide personnel needs and create a staffing pattern for the LGU, which shall be the basis in the hiring of Job Order personnel.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the HRMO issue written policies and guidelines on the hiring of JOs as a guide in drafting their contracts and in evaluating their accomplishments

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

impose other legal means to collect the disallowances from those who are no longer in the service of the LGU so as to return the funds to the bank account of the LGU for subsequent reappropriation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer provide the Municipal Accountant with a certified list of the taxpayers, as well as its total amount due and collectible for the year 2023, and thereafter, and provide such duly certified list at the beginning of every year.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant record at the beginning of every year the receivables from Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax based on the certified list of the taxpayers from the Municipal Treasurer.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to remit the 25% share of the barangays from the collections of basic real property taxes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the heads of departments and operating units, most especially the HRMO, assess the agency-wide personnel needs and create a staffing pattern for the LGU, which shall be the basis in the hiring of Job Order personnel.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the HRMO, issue written policies and guidelines on the hiring of JOs as a guide in drafting their contracts and in evaluating their accomplishments.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRMO to include, under a separate caption in the LDRRMFIP, a list of projects and activities to be funded from the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years, and to implement the planned priority projects in a timely manner, in order to enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

issue a directive to the newly created Inventory Committee to:

prepare, for his approval, a Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) containing the assignments of Committee members, schedules/location of inventory-taking and cut-off dates for the complete physical inventory of all properties of the Municipality over a three-month period in CY 2022;
start immediately, and complete during the year, the physical count of all PPEs of the Municipality, including road networks, in accordance with the procedures and schedule in the PIP;
prepare the corresponding RPCPPE and RPCLRN, and submit the same to the Accountant for reconciliation with the book balances and to the Audit Team for verification; and
issue a directive to the Municipal Engineer to assist the Property Officer in the preparation of the Municipal Road Map, with proper labels, and to assist the Inventory Team in the conduct of physical count of all municipal roads as basis in their preparation of the RPCLRN.

We also recommended that the designated Property Officer prepare and maintain Property Cards for each class of PPE, and LRNPC for all roads and its components and reconcile said Property Cards with the Accountant’s PPELC annually.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant start the cleansing of PPE accounts by following the procedures in COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended that the Management immediately submit the complete original or authenticated copies of contract and each all supporting document to the Office of the Auditor as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2009-01.

We recommend that:

Management submit the written request for write-off of dormant receivables and unliquidated cash advances duly supported with the requirements prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005.

The Municipal Accountant adjust the books of accounts to restore the beginning balances derecognized amounting to P4,853,667.65 and P2,435,277.68 for the accounts Advances to Officers of and Employees and Advances to Special Disbursing Officer, respectively.

The Municipal Accountant comply with all the requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that management return immediately to the Bureau of Treasury the unutilized DAP fund of P3 million in line with the Supreme Court en banc Decision promulgated on July 1, 2014, as modified in its Resolution promulgated on February 3, 2015.

We recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan:

Update immediately the Municipal Tax Ordinance to conform with the demands of time in strict compliance with the pertinent provisions of RA No. 7160 and its IRR.

Henceforth, update the Municipal Tax Ordinance every after five (5) years pursuant to Article 281 of the IRR of RA 7160.
We recommend that the deputized Collectors/Tellers remit their collection daily to the regular Revenue Collection Clerk who is accountable therefor.
We recommend that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer submit to the Auditor the Reconciliation Statements monthly.

We recommended that management:

Submit to the Auditor the contracts and their supporting documents within five (5) days from perfection.
We recommended that management create the Solid Waste Management Board and the latter to prepare, submit and implement the Municipal Solid Waste Management Plan, both in accordance with RA 9003.

We recommended strict compliance to Section 73 of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 93-404.  Otherwise, the accountable officer shall not be relieved from the accountability or be allowed credit for the loss during the settlement of his account.
observations.managementAction: The former Municipal Mayor in his letter dated February 27, 2015 submitted the Affidavits of Damage executed by the different heads of offices of LGU Mayorga, attesting to the loss and damage of records, properties and equipment during the onslaught of Typhoon Yolanda in the year 2013.  Said letter was received by the Audit Team on March 4, 2015.
observations.reason: Affidavit of Damage by the Municipal Health Nurse did not indicate the cost of PPEs damaged during the typhoon.  Although Affidavits of Damages were submitted, no request for relief of accountability was submitted for approval.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to adhere strictly with the provisions on remittance and deposits particularly Sec,69, PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 92-382.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the other officials concerned must settle all issued Notices of Suspensions before these turn into disallowances. Otherwise, the corresponding transactions shall be disallowed after ninety (90) days from receipt of the Notice. Henceforth, prescribed government laws, rules and regulations must be adhered to ensure economy, efficiency and effectiveness in operations. Require adequate documentation of financial transactions to avoid disallowances and suspensions in the audit of accounts.

1. We recommended that the Inventory Committee conduct the annual physical count of PPE to facilitate the preparation and submission of the RPCPPE not later than January 31 of each year, complete with all the required information which shall serve as the basis for the reconciliation and updating of accounting and property records.
2. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal General Services Officer prepare and maintain complete Property Ledger Cards and Property Cards, respectively, conduct periodic reconciliation of their records and update the same based on the results of the physical inventory.
3. Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the adjusting entries to reclassify as inventories the items with acquisition costs that are below the capitalization threshold of ₱15,000.00 together with the corresponding depreciation and prepare a JEV for the necessary adjustments; and drop from the LGU books the carrying amount of all unserviceable properties, as soon as the authority to derecognize is granted by appropriate office to bring the reported balance of the PPE account in the financial statements in order.

a. Conduct a quarterly monitoring on the implemented projects and activities with reference to the LDRRMFIP.

b. Re-examine and evaluate the PPAs of prior years, and determine the issues/concerns for the non-implementation of certain projects, and consider them in the preparation of the Annual LDRRMFIP.

c. Prepare the LDRRMFIP every year and include in the budgeting for the Programs, Projects and Activities, the unspent balance of the LDRRMF which should be transferred to the Special Trust Fund including all cash donations received.

d. Optimally utilize the LDRRM Fund to adequately equip the constituents in preparing to manage disaster risks towards a resilient community in times of calamities.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

1.Appoint a qualified employee to be the General Services Officer who: (a) will take custody of and be accountable for all properties, real or personal, owned by the local government unit and those granted to it in the form of donation, reparation, assistance and counterpart of joint projects; and (b) shall prepare and maintain the records on the acquisition, description, custody, disposal and other information about the property, plant and equipment based on the source documents of the transactions in the Property Cards;

2. Instruct the Inventory Committee to:

a. Formulate a Physical Inventory Plan and schedule for the conduct of physical inventory that will complement with the time of the members of the committee in order not to prejudice the operations of the Municipality. Also, conduct the physical count pursuant to 6.1 and 6.2 of COA Circular No. 2020-006; and 

b. Cause the submission of a complete Report of the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year.

3. Require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Trace the differences in the account balances and reconcile her records with the financial statements; 


b. Act on the preparation and maintenance of the PPE ledger cards based on the source documents of the transactions; and 

c. Provide the necessary disclosures required in IPSAS 17 on PPE.
observations.managementAction: Agree with the above recommendation. In fact, during the LGU Department Head’s meeting with OIC-MLGOO held last April 4, the re-organization of Inventory Committee Membership was discussed. As agreed, the new Inventory Committee members will set a meeting by April 28 to take up key matters such as designation of Property Custodian, conduct of physical inventory count, among others. Please note that tracing of difference, reconciliation of records, and other subsequent procedures will be conducted once the physical count is completed
observations.reason:
No GSO hired yet but designated the Municipal Treasurer as property custodian for the time being.

Already drafted a Physical Inventory Plan but yet to schedule and finalize the conduct of physical inventory count

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the differences among the details/schedule, General Ledger, Subsidiary Ledger, Statement of Financial Position, and Notes to Financial Statement of the Due to NGA account as required by Section 121 of Presidential Decree 1445 and Paragraph 27 of the International Public Sector Accounting Standards.
observations.managementAction: We agree with the recommendation. Our office has started utilizing/ maintaining a subsidiary ledger account for this “Due to NGA”, effective calendar year of 2021. This would be helpful in providing details and/ or breakdown of the total amount attributed to this account. In addition, with our team’s on-going and continuous effort of reconciling the difference/s in Financial Statement balances, we are optimistic that any existing issues may be corrected or lessened moving forward.

Municipal Treasurer to discuss this matter with the Municipal Mayor for appropriate disposition. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant agrees with the recommendation and will have to discuss this is in details with the former Municipal Accountant  Designate to come up with strategic approach for resolution.

Already in queue as part of year end closing

Already returned P10,000,000to the Office of the President in CY 2024

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing audit observations, we recommended that:

1. Earnest effort to implement all the approved PPAs should principally come from the Municipal Mayor being the head of agency identified as the responsible person in the Joint Memorandum Circular from DBM-DOF-DILG.

2.  Ensure the implementation of the approved PPAs for the optimal utilization of the appropriation for development projects.
observations.managementAction: Municipal Mayor is fully committed in the implementation of all the approved PPAs this calendar year. In fact, a meeting with the MPDO, ME, and the BAC was conducted on Feb. 6, 2023 to discuss the matter. One of the agreed was, that, the LGU would target four biddings every two weeks starting this month of February 2023. This is to fast track the implementation of the approved PPAs of our Municipality.
observations.reason: Previous years’ continuing appropriations were prioritized after current year implementation this year

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the:

2. Municipal Assessor to:

a. Update and reconcile records relative to real properties within the Municipality and its owners;



  3. Municipal Treasurer to:
 a. Reconcile the updated tax payer’s index card with the records of the Municipal Assessor;

b. Submit the Tax payer’s index card to the Municipal Accountant at the start of year as basis in recording RPT receivables; and

c. Develop collection strategies and intensify the collection of real property taxes and special education taxes to provide additional sources of funds of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction:
Municipal Accountant- Agree with the recommendation. Per discussion with the Municipal Treasurer, she will forward the RPT Tax account register/ tax payer’s index card, as basis/ reference in recording the full amount of RPT receivable.
Municipal Assessor- The team is currently conducting an on-going general revision of real property assessment that started last December 2022 and expected to complete until December 2023. Likewise, the office is planning to have physical survey per barangay of all real properties within the Municipality; target start date is on Q2 2023. In addition, updating and reconciling of real properties will be attended once the physical survey is completed. Lastly, since the Municipal Engineer is acting in concurrent capacity as Municipal Assessor- Designate, it would be difficult for her to devote full time in Assessor’s office, thus, immediate hiring of full time Municipal Assessor would be beneficial to the effective discharge of the relevant functions of the position.
Municipal Treasurer- Agree to reconcile the updated tax payer’s index card but will wait on the completion of general real property assessment revision conducted by the Assessor’s office. Also, to fast pace the process of reconciliation, MTO shall resume utilization of I-Tax System. On the other hand, we also agree to send the accountant tax payer’s index card every start of the year. Meanwhile, as regards collections strategy, the office is required to send monthly real property delinquent accounts to the provincial treasurer for sending of notice and follow-up to concerned accounts.
observations.reason:
Updating and reconciling records, 100% complete as of Q2 of 024

Ongoing updating and reconciling of records, 80% completed as of September 25, 2024

Sent notice of delinquencies to material accounts and posted tarpaulings and the like as information campaign and drive.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:
a. City Accountant -
1. record adjustments corresponding to the noted accounting errors
and omissions based on relevant and sufficient documents; and
2. coordinate with the City Treasurer for the timely submission of
reports and regular reconciliation of records.
b. City Treasurer-
1. submit to the OCA all financial reports and documents promptly
in accordance with existing regulations; and
2. coordinate with the City Accountant for the regular reconciliation
of records.

We recommended that the:
a. City Engineer and/or other Officials concerned prepare turnover
documents as basis for the derecognition of assets which are in
possession and control of the recipient agencies; and
b. City Accountant record adjustments corresponding to the noted
accounting errors based on relevant and sufficient documents.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. require the CPDO and CEO to -
• prepare design plans and program of works of PPAs as basis in
setting the appropriation of each PPAs to be indicated in the proposed AIP and
proposed Annual Budget;
• conduct feasibility or engineering study prior to implementation of PPAs;
• aptly conduct periodic monitoring and evaluation of the City’s PPA
implementation with appropriate reports submitted to the City Mayor for
perusal and appropriate action, if needed;
b. require the Local Development Council, CPD Coordinator and City
Budget Officer to –
• ensure that the proposed AIP and Annual Budget specifically identify or
itemize the PPAs to be funded under the 20% Development Fund;
• set definite and realistic timelines of PPAs’ implementation in the
proposed AIP; and
• require the Bids and Awards Committee to conduct early procurement
activities pertaining to development projects to ensure that the timeline set
for every project is strictly followed.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the City DRRM
Council, CDRRMO and City Budget Office to:
a. equitably allocate the 70% LDRRMF for disaster prevention and
mitigation, preparedness, response, and rehabilitation and recovery;
b. ensure that the proposed LDRRMFIP and Annual Budget specifically
identify or itemize the PPAs to be funded under the 70% LDRRMF;
c. set definite and realistic timelines of PPAs’ implementation in the
proposed LDRRMFIP; and
d. adhere to the provisions of NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 2014-01 dated April 4, 2014 and other
regulations on LDRRMF utilization.

We recommended that Management evaluate the feasibility of the project. If
implementation proves to be doubtful or no longer necessary, it may consider
preparing a different request or proposal. Otherwise, said funds shall be
returned to DOLE to relieve the City of obligations outlined in the MOA.

We recommended that Management:
a. fully utilize the automated teller machine payroll system for payment
of employee compensation and benefits; and
b. discontinue the practice of paying expenses out of the cash advances
when they could be practically paid through checks in compliance
with COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that Management adhere with COA Circular No. 98-002
and refrain from hiring private lawyers to render legal services which the
City Legal officer could provide.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
a. Inventory Committee to conduct regular physical count of PPEs and
prepare the annual RPCPPEs;
b. officials concerned to adhere to the guidelines and procedures on the
disposal of unserviceable properties pursuant to Sections 167 and 168
of the Supply and Property Management of LGUs prescribed in COA
Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992; and
c. Inspectorate Team to conduct inspection and monitoring of completed
projects within the one-year defects liability period to uncover defect/s
which needed to be corrected/rectified by the contractor pursuant to
Section 62.2.2.1 of the RIRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the City Mayor issue a written directive compelling the City
Engineer to submit monthly to the City Accountant and the Audit Team the
following:
a) Duly signed Statement of Work Accomplished and Certificate of
Completion on completed projects, regardless if claims are submitted by the
contractors or not;
b) Report of all projects being undertaken by the City, stating therein the
complete details of the projects including the updated percentage of
accomplishment.
We also recommended that the City Accountant:
a) Record all on-going and completed projects in the appropriate
accounts based on the status of the projects submitted by the CEO;
and
b) Discuss and reconcile records and reports with the City Engineer and
prepare the necessary adjustments on the affected accounts, where
necessary.

We recommended that the City Treasurer:
a) coordinate with LBP on the unrecorded deposits, and with DBP for the
credit adjustments and obtain the pertinent documents thereon;
b) forward the DVs and supporting documents on the unrecorded deposits and
disbursements to the City Accountant for proper recording;
c) assign a specific employee who will monitor daily the online
transactions in the City’s bank account and will forward to the City
Accountant the necessary documents for immediate reconciliation of
accounting and treasury records; and
d) take appropriate action on the stale checks and discuss the same with
the City Accountant as to its proper disposition.
We also recommended that the City Accountant:
a) obtain from the CTO the details and supporting documents of the
reconciling items in the BRS and prepare the Journal Entry Voucher
to record the same; and
b) coordinate with the City Treasurer on the appropriate actions to be
taken on the stale checks in order to restore the equivalent amount to
the Cash in Bank account of the City.

We recommended that the City Mayor, the City Budget Officer and the City DRRM
Officer:
a) Stop using the LDRRM Fund for operational and administrative
expenditures of the CDRRM Office, instead, provide sufficient budget in the General
Fund for its operations; and
b) Use the LDRRM Fund for procurement of relief goods only in instances where a
disaster or calamity is imminent or has been officially declared for a specific area,
encompassing the City, and only when duly supported with the appropriate and
necessary documents.

We recommended that the City Mayor as Chairman of the City Development
Council, through the Head of the City Planning and Development Office to
include in the City’s Annual Investment Plan and the City’s proposed budget
only those projects that can be implemented immediately during the budget year
and/or reprogram projects that can no longer be implemented.
We also recommended that the City Engineer’s Office complete the projects
which are already delayed and to implement all projects within the contract
period.

We recommended that the City Mayor, through the City Budget Officer and
the Head of the City Planning and Development Office, appropriate funds,
through realignment or supplemental budget, from any of the allowable
funding source for CBMS implementation in CY 2024 and conduct regular
data collection every three (3) years thereafter or at shorter intervals, if
necessary.

We recommended that the City Mayor, as Chairman of the City Peace and
Order Council (CPOC):
a) Require the CPOC Secretariat to ensure that the Minutes of the CPOC Meeting
and Resolution, which approved the Peace and Order Plan and the release of the
Confidential Fund, are signed by 2/3 of the CPOC members as evidence of
their concurrence; and
b) Require the Chief of the City PNP to prepare a certification which will emphasize
the peace and order situation in the City to justify the urgency to appropriate an
amount for confidential activities in the City’s budget, as well as the release of the
CF.
We also recommended that the City Accountant process the cash advance for
Confidential Fund only if the Disbursement Voucher is supported with
certified copies of the Minutes of the Meeting and CPOC Resolution bearing
the signatures of 2/3 of its members as evidence of their approval; and the PNP
Certification as basis for the release of Confidential Funds.

We recommended that the designated Special Disbursing Officer submit the
Quarterly Accomplishment Reports on the use of the CF to the Office of DILG
Secretary not later than the 15th day of the first month of the succeeding
quarter.

1.8 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal
Accountant to regularly coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer regarding
the existence of the stale checks. The latter should send written notices to the
concerned payees before checks become stale.
1.9 Also, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant take up the necessary
adjustment in its books of accounts for stale checks. Moreover, for checks that
become stale and need replacement, a new check may be issued upon
submission of the spoiled or stale check to the Municipal Treasurer. The
replacement check shall also be chronologically reported in the RCI prepared
at the time of cancellation.

2.16 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
to:
a. recompute the depreciation based on the changes in accounting
estimates due to the alteration of the residual value as prescribed by
Philippine Application Guidelines (PAG) 6 of IPSAS 17 and apply the
same in prospective application on the period following the audit year;
b. expedite substantiation of recorded unidentified PPE items and
exhaust possible remedy to itemize lumped PPE accounts, those

carrying amount; and

Accounting Standard (IPSAS) 17 and relevant/applicable COA
Circulars and other references.

projects totaling ₱11,919,993.91, based on the report of the Municipal

2.23 We recommended that the Chairman and members of the Bids and Award
Committee strictly comply with the provisions of the 2016 RIRR of RA No.
9184, specifically the following:
a) Undertake procurement only if it is included in the approved Annual
Procurement Plan;
b) Check the submitted Omnibus Sworn Statement of the bidders and ensure
that it is compliant with the latest form prescribe in Item 6 of GPPB Circular
No. 04-2020; and
c) Require all winning bidders to submit their DOLE-approved CSHP before
approval of their contracts.

2.24 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant require the suppliers to
submit the acceptable form of warranty security for the procurement of goods
before payment is made to protect the interest of the Municipality.

3.16 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the members of the
Municipal Development Council (MDC), through the Municipal Planning and
Development Coordinator, identify specific and detailed PPAs in the AIP to
avoid lump sum appropriations of the 20% Development Fund, otherwise,
secure first the authorization from the Sanggunian Bayan prior to any
disbursement therefrom.

4.11 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairperson of the MDRRM
Council, through the MDRRM Officer, identify disaster risk reduction and
management priority projects that will be funded from the CY 2018 and prior
years continuing appropriation amounting to ₱5,501,330.94; revise the CY
2024 LDRRMFIP to include the newly-identified projects; and implement the
same with the year.

5.9 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer transfer immediately the
amount of ₱9,007,680.00 from the STF to the General Fund and implement
stricter oversight procedures in her Office to ensure proper charging of funds,
including regular reconciliation of disbursements with budget allocations.

5.10 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary
accounting entries to setup the reciprocal accounts in the General and Trust
Fund books pending the transfer of cash

6.8 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant send a confirmation letter
to the Head of the Finance and Administration Department of the Office of
the President in order to verify if the LGU still have unliquidated fund
transfers in previous years. If none, record the amount as donation and use
it for disaster risk reduction and management activities of the Municipality.
Otherwise, prepare the Disbursement Voucher in favor of the Bureau of
Treasury to return the unutilized amount of ₱3,000,000.00.

7.13 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Budget
Officer, realign/supplement the needed amount for CBMS implementation
from any of the possible and allowable funding sources in the CY 2024 budget
and to conduct regular data collection every three (3) years thereafter or at
shorter intervals if necessary.

8.8 We recommended that the Supply Officer and the designated Inspection
Officer conduct inspection of delivered goods immediately upon receipt of the
items and prepare promptly the Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR),
regardless if claims are submitted by the suppliers or not, and indicate therein
the actual date of acceptance and inspection.

9.11 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
a) Require all collectors to remit DAILY the amount based on the
accountable forms issued as reported in the RCD;
b) Make a physical count of the unused accountable forms remaining in
the custody of the collector for every remittance made; and
c) Release new accountable forms ONLY when those issued are
completely reported.

1.15 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. expedite substantiation of recorded unidentified PPE items and
exhaust possible remedy to itemize lumped PPE accounts, those
labelled as “adjustments” or the particulars were left blank/empty in
the lapsing schedule;
b. effect the necessary adjustments in the books to correct erroneously
classified PPEs, including completed projects recorded under the
Construction in Progress accounts, to the appropriate asset accounts
and prepare the appropriate journal entries and other accounting
records to correct the depreciation in order to rectify the errors and
present the asset accounts to their proper carrying amount;
c. recompute the depreciation based on the changes in accounting
estimates due to the alteration of the residual value as prescribed by
Philippine Application Guidelines (PAG) 6 of IPSAS 17 and apply the
same in prospective application on the period following the audit
year; and
d. ensure adherence to the provisions of International Public Sector
Accounting Standard (IPSAS) 17 and relevant/applicable COA
Circulars and other references.
1.16 We also reiterated prior year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal
Mayor create an Inventory Committee, thru issuance of an Executive Order,
and require the same to (i) prepare a Physical Inventory Plan, containing
outline and strategies on how to conduct and complete the physical
inventory within the prescribed period; (ii) conduct a comprehensive physical
inventory of all PPE owned by the Municipality, identifying the proper
classification, individual identification, status of serviceability,
accountability and location thereof; (iii) prepare and submit a complete
RPCPPE to the Municipal Accountant not later than January 31 of each
year, copy furnish the Audit Team; (iv) prepare and submit IIRUP together
with its supporting documents for disposal of unserviceable properties; and
(v) strictly adhere with the provisions of COA Circular 2020-006 to establish
PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

2.13 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. issue a memorandum order for the Municipal GSO, as concurrent
Municipal Assessor, to (i) cause the registration and transfer of title
of the lands acquired by the Municipality; and (ii) make
representation with the Register of Deeds regarding the status of any
pending land titling application; and
b. require the Municipal Accountant, with the assistance of the
Municipal Assessor’s Office, to (i) conduct verification, analysis, and
validation of the balances of the land account; (ii) exhaust possible
remedy to reconcile their records and substantiation of the
unidentified properties; and (iii) consider the application of the
Revaluation Model as accounting policy on the Land account of the
PPE to come up with a fair presentation of the account balance; and
c. direct the Inventory Committee to identify land/real properties owned
by the Municipality and conduct a comprehensive physical
inventory to ascertain the existence, rights, and obligations of the
recorded land account items.

3.18 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately recognize the
₱9,538,536.24 unaccounted liquidations and the overbooked ₱1,141,763.12
financial assistance, as well as, effect the necessary adjustments in the books
of accounts to reflect the correct balance of the Due to NGAs account in the
financial statements.
3.19 We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure immediate
application for an increase in Fidelity Bond of the designated Disbursing
Officer equivalent to his maximum accountability. Otherwise, limit the grant
of cash advances to the extent of the maximum accountability of
₱5,000,000.00 only.

4.15 We reiterated the recommendation that the Municipal Mayor (a.) direct the
Municipal Accountant to obtain the lacking Bank Statements of the five (5)
depository accounts in Table 7; (b.) prepare the corresponding monthly
Bank Reconciliation Statements; and (c.) ensure submission of the same to
the Audit Team.
4.16 Moving forward, we also recommended that the Municipal Accountant
strictly adhere to Section 74 of PD 1445 and the provisions COA Circular
No. 96-004, and relevant/applicable law, rules and regulation to ensure
observance to the principles of accountability and transparency.
4.17 We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Treasurer reconcile their independent records for the Cash-in-Bank account
and prepare necessary adjusting entries, if warranted.

1.28 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a) prepare adjusting entries to reclassify the: (a) completed projects totaling
₱10,788,779.33 to its appropriate PPE account; (b) the semi-expendable
properties and supplies totaling ₱1,353,634.00, and; (c) the overstated
accumulated depreciation of ₱654,243.71;
b) record only as PPE those properties that meet the definition and
recognition criteria of PPE and the threshold amount of ₱50,000.00 and
below;
c) maintain complete and updated Property Ledger Cards and reconcile the
same with records of the Property Cards at the General Services Office; and
d) prepare a detailed Lapsing Schedule with the following details (a)
description of the asset; (b) date of acquisition; (c) cost of the asset; (d)
estimated salvage value; (e) estimated useful life; (f) depreciation expense
for each period; (g) accumulated depreciation to date; and (h) carrying
value at the end of each period (original cost minus accumulated
depreciation).

1.29 We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer inspect the five projects
totaling ₱24,015,763.92 to verify their existence and actual condition and
prepare a report on the results of such verification for submission to the
Municipal Accountant.
