the Accounting Office with copies of all documents related to properties and
inventories donated to the LGU from other agencies as basis of the Municipal
Accountant in recording said donations in the LGU books.

3.22 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the adjusting
entries to correct the deficiencies on the net misstatements of ₱8,124,170.58;

3.23 We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue a directive and
authorize the OIC – Municipal Treasurer to: (a) Enroll all bank accounts in the LBP weAccess; (b) Furnish the Municipal Accountant with the monthly bank statements of all bank accounts of the LGU for the preparation of the monthly bank reconciliation
statements; and (c) Close inactive/dormant bank accounts and remit to the Bureau
of Treasury the balances of the bank accounts in the Trust Fund.

3.24 We reiterated our previous recommendations, that the Municipal Mayor
compel the Municipal Accountant, through a written directive with timelines,
to:
a. Prepare and maintain Subsidiary Ledgers for each bank account to
facilitate monitoring of individual balances;
b. Prepare monthly the Bank Reconciliation Statements for all bank
accounts within the prescribed period to prove the correctness of the
bank accounts per books and per bank, then submit the BRS to the Audit
Team for verification; and
c. Prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to restore to the Cash in Bank account
the amount of ₱852,762.34 representing stale checks and the
corresponding liability account (if there are claimants) or Prior Period
Adjustment account (if none), and inform the Municipal Budget Officer
of said available funds.

4.12 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, the Municipal Planning and
Development Coordinator, the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal
Accountant discontinue the utilization of the 20% Development Fund for
personal services and administrative expenses; and utilize funds allocated for
KALAHI-CIDSS Program for socio-economic development projects only, to
avoid the incurrence of irregular expenses.

4.13 We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Budget
Officer consider replenishing the amount of administrative expenses
erroneously charged to the 20% DF.

5.8 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, the Municipal Engineer, and the
LDRRMO undertake all the necessary detailed engineering activities and
market research to avoid potential issues that could delay project
implementation and completion before submitting a request for financial
assistance from the National Government.

6.16 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, direct the Municipal
Accountant, Treasurer, Budget Officer and LDRRMO to:
a. review and evaluate the charges on the reverted and reprogrammed CY
2019 and prior years continuing appropriations;
b. determine whether the obligations charged to the CY 2019 and prior years
continuing appropriations have been fully paid;
c. assessed whether the ₱3,525,809.73 dormant funds are due for reversion to the
General Fund; and
d. implement a structured communication system within the LGU to ensure
that all key officials are kept informed about the management of funds
earmarked for disaster risk reduction and management.

7.7 We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal
DRRM Officer allocate the correct amount for the Quick Response Fund in
accordance with the prescribed percentage of 30% of the total current
appropriation for LDRRMF.

8.7 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the
Municipal DRRM Officer and the Municipal Budget Officer, prepare and
submit the Report on the Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to the COA Audit
Team every 15th day after the end of each month.

9.6 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, Budget Officer and the focal
person of the Peace and Order Council:
a. Prepare and approve the Physical and Financial Plan when allocating CF
in the annual budget, indicating therein the proposed amount allotted for
each program, activity and project, as basis for confidential
disbursements; and
b. Obtain a PNP Certification, containing the concrete details of the peace
and order situation in the Municipality, prior to allocating CF from the
budget for Peace and Order Programs to justify the need for such
allocation; and include the said PNP Certification as an integral part of the
Peace and Order and Public Safety Plan.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant-Designate:
c) Verify the causes of the difference between the Subsidiary Ledgers
and the Bank Statements totaling ₱11,131,564.56 as of December 31,
2023 and prepare the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts
to reflect the correct balances of the Cash in Bank accounts and
other affected accounts;
d) Prepare monthly the Bank Reconciliation Statements for all bank
accounts within the prescribed period to prove the correctness of the
cash balances per books and per bank, then submit the same to the
Audit Team for verification.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer submit to the designated
Municipal Accountant:
a) ALL lacking Disbursements Vouchers totaling ₱933,449.63 on or
before May 31, 2024 to avoid the issuance of the Notice of
Disallowance; and
b) The complete monthly financial transaction documents and reports
every 10th day of the month together with the related source
documents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue a directive to the Inventory
Committee to perform the following tasks, and monitor their compliance
thereof:
a.) conduct a comprehensive physical count of all properties owned by the
Municipality and investigate the PPEs which are not found/missing and
prepare a report thereon;
b.) prepare a complete RPCPPE, properly classified as to serviceable and
unserviceable, with proper identification of persons accountable, status
of serviceability and location for submission to the Municipal
Accountant not later than January 31 of each year, copy furnished the
Audit Team; and
c.) Submit to the COA Audit Team Leader (ATL) a request for authority
to derecognize non existing/missing PPEs for which accountability
could no longer be pinpointed, together with the required supporting
documents, particularly the certified copy of the report of investigation.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a.) Prepare adjusting entries to reclassify from the PPE accounts the
identified items with unit cost below the ₱50,000.00 threshold totaling
₱1,014,890.00 to the appropriate expense account and provide the list
to the Property Officer for the issuance of the Inventory Custodian
Slips;
b.) Adopt the procedures prescribed for one-time cleansing of the PPEs on
missing/unaccounted properties after the conduct of investigation;
c.) Maintain and update PPE Ledger Cards for each category of assets.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Tally the amounts and balances of LDRRM Fund presented in the
Notes to Financial Statements with the balances in the General Ledger
and ensure that it is consistent with the source documents/registries;
and
b. Verify the discrepancies between the GL Balance of Trust Liability-
DRRM account and the records maintained by the Budget Officer and
make the necessary corrections, where necessary.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue a directive to the Municipal
Accountant-Designate, the Municipal Treasurer and the MDRRM Officer, to
set a cut-off date every December for the processing of transactions to be
charged to LDRRMF in order to determine before yearend the unexpended
QRF and DRRMF- MOOE due for transfer to the Special Trust Fund.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant-Designate notify the
Municipal Treasurer in writing the amount of unexpended QRF and DRRMF-
MOOE to be deposited to the LDRRMF Trust Fund before the last working
day of the budget year and record the same in the Trust-Liability DRRM
account.

We further recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer and MDRRMO
request for the enactment of an Ordinance to make available the unexpended
appropriation of CY 2023 amounting to ₱3,792,658.05 which was reverted to
the unappropriated surplus in the General Fund, to initiate the transfer to the
LDRRMF Trust Fund and before using the same amount in CY 2024.

We recommended that the designated Municipal Accountant review and study
the relevant manuals and issuances on the preparation of the SCBAA and
confer monthly with the Municipal Budget Officer to obtain the needed data
on the original and final budget for comparison with accounting records on the
actual utilization. Thereafter, update the working papers on said comparison
so as to prepare the SCBAA at year-end together with the required
reconciliation.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer confirm the availability of the
cash in the bank equivalent to ₱131,525.09 and prepare a certification to that
effect for submission to the Municipal Mayor for disposition of its utilization, to the Municipal Budget Officer for information of the additional fund source and
to the Municipal Accountant for reclassification to the appropriate income
accounts instead of the Due to NGAs account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the Municipal
Development Council, through the Head of the Municipal Planning and
Development Office include in the LGU’s Annual Investment Plan and
proposed budget only those projects that can be implemented immediately
during the budget year and/or reprogram projects that can no longer be
implemented.

We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer conduct site inspection on
the proposed projects of the LGU and prepare the appropriate Program of
Works PRIOR to its inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan.

We reiterated our recommendation for the Municipal Accountant to prepare
the Disbursement Voucher/s, the Municipal Mayor to approve, and the
Municipal Treasurer to issue the corresponding check in favor of the Bureau of
Treasury for the amount of ₱2,684,778.91 which remained idle for several years.

We recommended that the Chairman and Members of the Bids and Awards
Committee and the Secretariat, require the winning bidders to submit the
Performance Security, duly dated after receipt of the Notice of Award but
PRIOR to contract date, as part of the pre-requisite documents to contract
execution.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as the authorized person
to enter into contract with the contractors, sign contracts only when the
required documents are complete as listed in the Notice of Award.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant verify the authenticity of the
Performance Security submitted by the contractors for each procurement contract
before payment is made to protect the interest of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor stop the grant of cash advances
to the Special Disbursing Officer, and issue checks in the name of the supplier,
for the purchase of various goods needed by the Municipality, including the
MDRRMO.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the BAC conduct
Public Bidding or other alternative modes of procurement for all procurement
with Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC) amounting to ₱100,000.00 and
below to avoid disallowance of the disbursements in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the focal person of the
Municipal Peace and Order Council, obtain the PNP Certification containing
the concrete details of the peace and order situation in the Municipality,
PRIOR to allocating Confidential Funds in the budget for Peace and Order
Programs to justify its urgency; and include the said PNP Certification as an
integral part of the Municipal Peace and Order Public Safety Plan.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Budget
Officer allocate funds for Confidential Funds at an amount not exceeding 30%
of the total annual amount allocated for the Municipality’s Peace and Order
Program (POP).

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a.) submit the Disbursement Vouchers of every cash advance for Confidential
Fund, with complete supporting documents, in a sealed envelope, to the
COA ATL within seven (7) days from release of check together with the
Transmittal Letter enumerating the supporting documents post audit; and
b.) record the liquidation of the cash advance for CF in the books of accounts
ONLY upon receipt of the Transmittal Letter duly received by ICFAU.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a.) Instruct the designated Disbursing Officer, or authorized representative,
to submit directly to ICFAU, COA Central Office, the Liquidation Report
on the utilization of the Confidential Fund in a sealed envelope with a
visible label "CONFIDENTIAL - For ICFAU Only", within 30 days after
every quarter or after the cash advance had been fully utilized whichever
comes first; and
b.) Stop the grant of additional cash advance for Confidential Fund unless
supported with the Transmittal Letter to ICFAU of the Liquidation Report
on the previous cash advance duly stamped "received" by the said Office.

We reiterated our audit recommendations in CY 2022 that the Municipal Accountant:
Maintain a complete and updated PPE and Real Property Ledger Cards to ensure that all PPEs are properly accounted for and to easily facilitate the reconciliation of the RPCPPE with accounting records;
Reconcile the PPELC with the RPCPPE, upon submission by the inventory committee, wherein differences noted should be verified and the corrections be made, where necessary
Identify the composition of the unidentified PPEs totaling ₱32,144,597.38 in order to provide details of the specific assets acquired by the municipality; and
record the transfer of PPE totaling ₱998,458.17 from Trust Fund books to its respective PPE classification in the General Fund books and compute and record its corresponding depreciation expense.
We also recommended that the GSO designate prepare the Report of Physical Count of Property and Equipment (RPCPPE) as soon as the physical count is concluded and submit the same to the Audit Team and the Municipal Accountant.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Promptly obtain the monthly bank statements from the depository banks and regularly prepare the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements and ensure submission thereof to the Audit Team for auditing purposes;
Verify and determine the composition of the Cash in Bank account especially those under the Trust Fund accounts and prepare the Subsidiary Ledgers for the said accounts; and
Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to restore the cash corresponding to the stale checks’ amounts and re-establish the payable account thereof to avoid understatements of asset and obligations in the books.

We recommended that the Municipal Agriculturist promptly furnish the Municipal Accountant with distribution lists and other pertinent reports concerning the delivery of agricultural produce and supplies to the identified farmers' associations.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
prepare the necessary adjusting entries to record the procurement of ready to lay chickens, grower mash and layer mash to the Agricultural Produce for Distribution and Agricultural and Marine Supplies for Distribution accounts instead of the Breeding Stocks account;
draw the accounting entries to recognize the expense account for the distribution of the agricultural produce and supplies to the identified farmers associations upon receipt of the distribution list and other reports from the Municipal Agriculturist; and
henceforth, record all the transactions of the municipality using the proper accounts provided in the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs so that transactions are faithfully and correctly presented in the financial statements to provide the users thereof of reliable data/information.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant stop the practice of recording immediately to expense accounts the inventories received by the municipality. Henceforth, record subsequent procurements of inventories as an asset under the appropriate inventory account and recognize the related expenses based on the monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued, supported by duly approved RIS, and Distribution List.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as head of the Municipal Peace and Order Council, require:

the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, in collaboration with the Philippine National Police as the implementing office, to prepare a Physical and Financial Plan for Confidential Funds indicating the proposed amount allocated for each program, projects and activities – consistent with Peace and Order and Public Safety (POPS) Plan; and
the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate in the Annual Appropriation Ordinance funds for Peace and Order, as part of the POPS Plan, as required in Section 4.1.3 of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01, taking into consideration the Physical and Financial Plan, recommended above.
Also, to protect the interest of the government and to prevent loss and wastage of government funds and resources, we recommended that:
Management strictly adhere to the provisions of the COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 to ensure proper budgeting, utilization, liquidation and reporting of the confidential funds;
The Municipal Mayor, as the Special Disbursing Officer (SDO) of the CF, oversee agency-wide use and disposition of the CF and institute and maintain sound/effective internal control measures to prevent irregular, unnecessary, excessive, extravagant and unconscionable expenditures – as well as promote prudence in the use of government resources by those involved in the confidential operations; and
The Municipal Accountant ensure that (i) the amount of CE will not exceed the authorized funds provided for the CF; (ii) submit to the Audit Team for post audit all CF cash advance vouchers and complete required supporting documents within seven (7) days after release of check, in a sealed envelope with a visible label “CONFIDENTIAL – for the COA ATL Only” and with a covering transmittal letter enumerating the submissions; (iii) Record the liquidation in the books within five days after the receipt of the copy of the transmittal of documents duly acknowledged by the ICFAU.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all personnel concerned continuously adhere to the provisions of the aforementioned DBM-DOF-DILG JMC, to ensure that maximum attainment of desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the Municipality are achieved and the intended purposes of the 20% Development Fund are realized.

We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer:
submit a written explanation or justification regarding the deficiencies above noted. This is without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Suspension/Disallowance, if warranted; and
henceforth, initiate the enactment of an Appropriation Ordinance to make available the unexpended appropriation which were reverted to the surplus of the general fund allowing for expenditures to be incurred with specific appropriation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other concerned personnel undertake measures to ensure the immediate utilization of the village type dyer and processing center project in Barangay Poblacion.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor and Municipal Engineer render satisfactory explanation on revising the contract period from 130 days to 200 days without any documents supporting the change thereof.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:

the Municipal Engineer to strictly adhere to the project duration stipulated per contract and exercise diligence in the inspection of completed projects immediately after the reported date of completion to determine whether projects were completed on time, taking considerations on Variation Orders or Time Extension, if any, and make sure that all materials and prescribed procedures conform with the Program of Work, Detailed Estimates and Plan;
the Municipal Accountant to impose Liquidated Damages, equal to one-tenth (1/10) of one (1%) of the cost of the unfinished portion of the project for each day of delay; and
the erring contractor to pay the Liquidated Damages incurred for the 61-day delay incurred in the establishment of the village type dyer and processing center project.
Also, to protect the interest of the government and to prevent further loss and wastage of government funds and resources, we recommended that the following measures be undertaken for future projects of similar in nature:
Strictly monitor implementation of all projects, whether contracts for infrastructure, goods or consulting services, in accordance with the approved plans and specifications within the time frame specified in the contract agreement; and
Identify contracts which require appropriate management actions. If so, impose liquidated damages and/or administrative sanctions particularly termination of contracts and recommend blacklisting of contractors for failure to comply with their obligations in accordance with the provisions of Sections 68 and 69, and Appendix 17 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184.

We recommended that the MDRRM Officer:
submit written justification why the LDRRM Fund in the Special Trust Fund was utilized for disbursements not included in the LDRRMF Investment Plan;
consider all unpaid valid obligations in the identification of PPAs to be charged in the LDRRMF in the Special Trust Fund in the ensuing years; and
henceforth, utilize the LDRRM Fund, especially those in the Special Trust Fund, for programs, projects, and activities in accordance with the approved LDRRMF Investment Plan to ensure that its utilization is well-planned and duly authorized by the MDRRM Council.
We also recommended the Municipal Accountant to record all valid claims chargeable against the appropriations in the year the expenses were incurred in the books of accounts of the Municipality and recognize payables for all unpaid expenditures as of yearend to avoid disbursements with no corresponding fund or budget.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the MDRRM Officer:
stop the practice of utilizing the LDRRM Fund for activities and programs which do not directly support the disaster risk management activities of the municipality; and
henceforth, ensure that the LDRRM Fund will be utilized strictly for disaster prevention and mitigation, disaster preparedness, disaster response, and disaster rehabilitation and recovery purposes only.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and government officials and employees concerned:
Submit the lacking supporting documents noted in Appendix D.3 to avoid suspension in audit; and
Refund the excess amount of Daily Travel Expense claimed for their respective travels totaling ₱238,115.60 (Appendix D.4); and
Henceforth, only approve travels to the most necessary and essential to the effective performance of an official or employee’s mandates and functions.
We also recommended management to stop granting travel allowances beyond what is allowed under Executive Order No. 77, to avoid incurrence of irregular expenditures and disallowances in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to:
Stop the practice of retaining collections, instead, deposit all collections to the AGDB at least once a week; and
Stop using collections for operating expenses of the Municipality, instead, charge petty non-recurring expenses to the Petty Cash Fund (PCF).

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare an adjusting entry on the understatement depreciation expense amounting to ₱187,033.88 of the PPEs acquired in CY 2022 to 2023 using the appropriate account codes.
Also, recompute the accumulated depreciation for all depreciable assets acquired prior to CY 2022 by providing the residual values and effect the necessary adjustments upon determination of the correct amounts, for the proper valuation of the PPE; and
Prepare an adjusting entry to reduce the PPE balance at ₱ 1,246,150.00  which represents inventory items issued to end-users supported with SSMI and RIS prepared by the Supply Officer; and, henceforth, record the semi-expendable items costing below P50,000.00  on an individual or per item basis as Inventories upon acquisition and as Expense upon issuance.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Record a reduction in the Due to NGAs account and the corresponding revenue account only when the  Report of Disbursements/Liquidation Reports on the utilization of funds transferred from national
government agencies are duly received by COA and the Source Agency; and
Record in the appropriate expense account in the Trust Fund the release of financial assistance to beneficiaries from funds received from NGAs.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant include in the Notes to Financial Statements disclosures on the details of the LDRRM Fund, specifically the composition of the appropriations, amounts utilized and the balances of the unexpended LDRRM Fund per year.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
submit the Disbursement Vouchers of every cash advance for Confidential Fund, with complete supporting documents, in a sealed envelope, to the COA ATL within seven (7) days from release of check together with the Transmittal Letter enumerating the supporting documents for post audit; and
record the liquidation of the cash advance for CF in the books of accounts only upon receipt of the Transmittal Letter duly marked received by ICFAU of COA.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Approve the cash advance for Confidential Fund only when the required supporting documents are complete and release the CF cash advance only to permanently appointed officials designated as disbursing officers; and
Stop the practice of transferring cash advance from one Accountable Officer to another.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require the Special Disbursing Officer to obtain a cash advance at an amount that is within the cash accountability of his existing fidelity bond; in case a cash advance of
higher amount is needed, the same should be entrusted to the Municipal Treasurer; and
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant process cash advances to the SDO only after the previous cash advances were liquidated, in order to prevent accumulation of unliquidated cash advances.

1.25. We reiterated the prior year’s audit recommendation for the Municipal
Mayor to compel the Inventory Committee, through a written directive with
timelines and sanctions, to:
(a) Accomplish and complete in CY 2024 the comprehensive physical
count of all properties owned by the Municipality, from the
preparation of the Physical Inventory Plan until the submission of
the Report of Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) and Report on the
Physical Count of the Local Road Network (RPCLRN) in the required
formats to the Municipal Accountant before the year-end closing of the
LGU books of accounts.
(b) Conduct an investigation on non-existing/missing PPEs and prepare
a separate report thereon for immediate submission to the Municipal
Accountant as a pre-requisite for the filing of the request for
derecognition;
(c) Conduct a reasonable verification of the LGU’s ownership of the PPE
items found during the physical counts but not accounted for in the
records of the Accounting Office and General Services Office.
1.26. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
(a) Derecognize from the PPE accounts those items costing less than
₱50,000.00, and reclassify them to the appropriate accounts and submit
a list of these items to the GSO for issuance of the corresponding
Inventory Custodian Slips;
(b) Prepare the necessary accounting entries to recognize PPEs found
during physical count but not yet recorded in the books after LGU’s
ownership has been established by the Inventory Committee; and
(c) Prepare and maintain the subsidiary ledgers for the cited sub-accounts
with no subsidiary records and provide copies to the Auditor to
facilitate the verification of the balances;
1.27. We further recommended that the Head of the General Services Office:
(a.) Prepare and maintain the Property Cards (PCs) for those PPE items
not yet covered PCs, including PPE items found during the physical
count but not yet accounted for in the GSO records, after LGU’s
ownership has been established by the Inventory Committee; and
(b.) Issue Inventory Custodian Slips to the end-users of the items costing
less than ₱50,000.00 which were reclassified by the Municipal
Accountant as Prior Period Adjustments.

2.8. We recommended the Municipal Budget Officer adjust immediately the
recorded balances of the Continuing Appropriations in the RAAO maintained
for the 20% Development Fund to include the omitted balances in the total
amount of ₱972,939.95.
2.9. The Budget Officer agreed to correct the balances of the 20% DF Continuing
Appropriations in the RAAO by including the unexpended CY 2022 balances
which were inadvertently excluded in the CY 2023 RAAO, SAAO and SCBAA.

3.8. We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
a) Cancel all the stale checks which remain unclaimed by the payees and
present these checks in the current Report of Checks Issued (RCI) after
the last issued check at the date of cancellation, and submit to the
Accountant the said report together with any original DVs and
supporting documents that can be recovered from the files of the
Municipal Treasurer’s Office; and
b) for stale checks issued within two years from December 31, 2023 which
remained unclaimed, send written notices to the payees of checks, and
give them a specific date within which to file their claims for
replacement.
3.9. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the adjusting
entries to restore the amounts previously recorded as disbursements on all the
stale checks.
3.10. The concerned officials committed to review those outstanding checks and the
pertinent transactions, and adhere to the audit recommendations.

5.11. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal
Engineer:
a) Initiate the calibrated measures to terminate the contract and report
the erring contractor for blacklisting;
b) Inspect and assess the status of the project, including an assessment on
whether the project could still be recovered and completed; and
c) If the existing project is no longer considered recoverable, prepare a
certification as to its status for submission to the Municipal Accountant
and Municipal Treasurer as basis for the return of the remaining funds
to the Bureau of Treasury.

6.13. We recommended that the MDRRM Officer undertake the following
processes in preparing the annual LDRRM Investment Plan to be submitted
to the Local Sanggunian, through the Municipal DRRM Council:
(a) Identify the specific trainings and seminars, supplies, equipment,
infrastructure facilities, activities, and programs needed by the LGU at
the planning phase;
(b) Coordinate with other departments as to the PPAs to be included in the
LDRRMFIP to avoid duplication; and
(c) Monitor the PPAs in the continuing appropriation for their
implementation and re-program any item if it is no longer viable.

7.10. We recommended that, henceforth, the Municipal Mayor, as Chairperson of
the Municipal Peace and Order Council, through its Technical Working
Group and Secretariat to:
(a.) secure, PRIOR to allocating CF, the required certification of the
Tolosa Chief of Police containing the concrete detail of the
circumstances that warrant the allocation of Confidential Fund (CF)
if the Council deems that the peace and order situation calls for the
use of CF; and
(b.) prepare the Physical and Financial Plan to support the proposed
allocation for CF to be submitted to the SB for funding.
7.11. We further recommended that, henceforth, the Municipal Budget Officer
review the proposed annual budget to make sure that the allocation for CF is
properly justified with the necessary documents.
7.12. The officials concerned understood the flaws in their process of allocating CF after
the Audit Team gave a brief explanation on the how to allocate CF, including the
need to justify the same with the mandatory documents. In light of this better
understanding, the officials committed to adhere with the audit recommendations
to correct the deficiencies in their process.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Continue resolving the discrepancies noted between the list and
balances of PPEs in the Subsidiary Ledgers and the Schedule of
Depreciation;
b. Prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to record the total accumulated
depreciation of the PPEs aggregating ₱1,567,256.00 to reflect its actual
carrying values; and
c. Adopt the procedures on the one-time cleansing of PPEs and on the
disposition of non-existing PPE items as prescribed in COA Circular No.
2020-006

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor mandate the Municipal
Engineer to assist the Municipal Accountant in identifying and confirming the
existence of infrastructure projects, conduct an assessment of its current values if
not found in the books and prepare a report thereon in order to lessen the discrepancies in the
accounting records.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a) set a cut-off date every December, in coordination with the Municipal
Treasurer and the MDRRM Officer, on the processing of transactions to
be charged against the LDRRMF to determine before the last working
day of the year the unexpended QRF and DRRMF- MOOE due for
transfer to the Special Trust Fund bank account;
b) record the unexpended QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE balance in the Trust
Liability-DRRM account in the Trust Fund books at year-end, even if
there is no actual cash transfer yet; and
c) notify the Municipal Treasurer, in writing, of the amount of unexpended
QRF and DRRMF-MOOE to be deposited to the DRRMF Trust Fund
bank account before the last working day of each year.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer transfer the unexpended
balance of the QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE from the General Fund to the bank
account of the Trust Fund for Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, the Municipal Budget Officer,
and the MDRRM Officer:
a. Stop using the DRRMF for operating expenses and utilize the Fund to
implement programs, projects, and activities related to disaster risk
reduction and management only;
b. Replenish the amount of LDRRMF improperly utilized in CY 2023
totaling ₱1,559,330.25 through a re-alignment from the CY 2024
appropriations in the General Fund to restore the cost of the ineligible
charges; and
c. Ensure that expenditures for projects and activities charged against the
LDRRMF are within the context envisioned by the provisions of JMC
2014-1 and JMC 2013-1.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the Disbursement
Voucher and the Municipal Treasurer issue the check, in favor of the Bureau
of Treasury for the return of the unutilized and long outstanding funds in the
Due to NGAs account totalling ₱3,442,207.36 in the Trust Fund and
₱1,946,754.05 in the General Fund to extinguish the liability of the
Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the Municipal
Peace and Order Council, require the Chief of the PNP of LGU Tunga, Leyte
to issue a certification containing the details of the peace and order situation
of the Municipality as basis of the urgency in allocating confidential funds in
the LGU’s budget.

We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer prepare the Physical
and Financial Plan as basis in determining the amount to be appropriated for
Confidential Fund which must not exceed 30% of the appropriation for Peace
and Order Program, and include said document as an integral part of the
budgeting process.

We commended the Municipal Mayor, the Municipal Planning and
Development Officer and the Municipal Engineer for the high implementation
rate of the PPAs under the 20% DF and recommended to sustain the speedy
implementation of LGU projects during the year they were budgeted.

1.12 We reiterated our audit recommendations in CY 2022 with modification that
the Municipal Accountant:

a. Maintain a complete and updated PPE and Real Property Ledger
Cards to ensure that all PPEs are properly accounted for and to easily
facilitate the reconciliation of the RPCPPE with accounting records;
b. Prepare adjusting entries to record the understatement or
overstatement of recorded depreciation expense (Table 1). Moving
forward take into consideration that recognition of depreciation
expense should start when PPE item is received and ready for use;
c. Recognize the depreciation expense of the PPEs referred to in
paragraph 1.9.3 and henceforth, ensure that all depreciable PPEs are
provided with depreciation expenses;
d. Reclassify as inventories the items with acquisition costs that are below
the capitalization threshold of ₱15,000.00 (Table 3);
e. Reclassify the incorrectly recorded PPEs to their proper account
(Table 4); and
f. Reconcile the PPELC with the RPCPPE wherein differences noted
should be verified and the corrections be made, where necessary.

1.13 We also reiterated our previous recommendations, that the Municipal General
Services Officer:

g. Issue Property Acknowledgment Receipts for donated equipment and
Inventory Custodian Slips for items below the capitalization threshold
in lieu of the previously issued Property Acknowledgment Receipt; and
h. Ensure that Report of Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) was prepared
and accomplished completely when the physical count is concluded.

2.7. We reiterated our audit recommendations in CY 2022 with modification that
the Municipal Accountant:

a. Regularly prepare the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements and
ensure submission of the same to the Audit Team;
b. Verify and determine the composition of the Cash in Bank account in
the Trust Fund and prepare and submit the updated Subsidiary
Ledgers for the said account, effecting the necessary correcting entries
for erroneous recordings made from prior years, if warranted; and
c. Prepare the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to restore the unreleased
checks based on the schedule prepared by the Municipal Treasurer at
the end of every year to fairly present the Cash in Bank balance of the
LGU in the Financial Statements, and to reverse the entries every
January of the ensuing year.

3.13 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal
Accountant:

a. Obligate transactions by preparing an accounting entry recognizing the payable
account for those transactions that have occurred and budgeted/ funded in the
same year to avoid disbursements with no corresponding fund or budget.
b. Prepare adjusting entry reclassifying the Motor Vehicle account to Disaster
Response and Rescue Equipment account and its corresponding Accumulated
Depreciation.
c. Henceforth, proper accounting treatment should be strictly observed in
recording the financial transactions under Trust Liabilities-DRRMF,
particularly in the expense and asset recognition.

3.14 We also recommended that the Municipal DRRM Officer together with the
Municipal Budget Officer strictly monitor and evaluate the use and disbursement
of LDRRMF so as not to exceed the budgeted appropriation.

4.6 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw an adjusting entry to
correct the misclassification of accounts and henceforth, record all the
transactions of the municipality using the appropriate accounts provided in
the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs, so that transactions are faithfully
and correctly presented in the financial statements, which will provide reliable
data to the users thereof.

5.12 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as head of the Municipal Peace
and Order Council:

a. submit written justification why the appropriation for confidential funds in CY
2023 was not made as required by existing regulations;
b. require the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate in the Annual Appropriation
Ordinance funds for Peace and Order (POP), as part of its POPS Plan, as
required in Section 4.1.3 of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No.
2015-01 taking into consideration that the total amount for Confidential Funds
(CF) shall not exceed thirty percent (30%) of the total annual amount allocated
for the LGU's POP;
c. assume the responsibility as Special Disbursing Officer (SDO) of the CF of the
agency, or designate a regular/permanent employee for that purpose;
d. Approve the cash advances for CF and oversee its agency-wide use and
disposition; and
e. Institute and maintain sound and effective internal control measures to
discourage and prevent irregular, unnecessary, excessive, extravagant and
unconscionable expenditures as well as promote prudence in the use of
government resources by those involved in the intelligence and/or confidential
operations.

5.13 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant, in the subsequent grant of
Confidential Fund cash advances:

a. Ensure that the amount of CE will not exceed the authorized funds provided for
the CF;
b. Record the cash advance in the books of accounts and maintain a separate
subsidiary record of cash advances solely for CF per SDO;
c. Submit all DVs for cash advance for intelligence and confidential
activities/projects, with complete required supporting documents, within seven
(7) working days from release of the check to the COA ATL concerned in a
sealed envelope with a visible label "CONFIDENTIAL- For the COA ATL
Only" and with a covering transmittal letter enumerating the submissions;
d. Record the liquidation in the books within five days after the receipt of the copy
of the transmittal of documents duly acknowledged by the ICFAU. He shall
submit the corresponding Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to the COA ATL; and
e. Record all the transactions of the municipality using the appropriate accounts
provided in the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs so that transactions are
faithfully and correctly presented in the financial statements which will provide
reliable data to the users thereof.

6.11 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all personnel concerned
continuously adhere to the provisions of the aforementioned DBM-DOF-DILG
JMC, to ensure that maximum attainment of desired socio-economic
development and environmental management outcomes of the Municipality
are achieved, and the intended purposes of the 20% Development Fund are
realized.

7.11 We recommended that the Municipal Engineer, Municipal Planning and
Development Officer and the rest of the members of the LGU’s inspectorate
team to render satisfactory explanation on certifying the correctness of the
Inspection Reports despite the noted deficiencies in the projects and non-
deduction thereof from the final billings of the contracts, without prejudice to
the issuance of notice of disallowance for deficiencies found in audit.

7.12 We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer direct the resident/project
engineers to:

a.) conduct thorough inspection on all infrastructure projects to validate the
actual accomplishment submitted for progress billing to make sure that all
materials and prescribed procedures conform with the Program of Work,
Detailed Estimates and Plan or Variation Orders, if any; and
b.) prepare Inspection Report accurately showing the actual work
accomplished as compared to the contractor’s SWA and make
necessary adjustments/deductions from the progress billing equivalent
to the cost of the noted deficiencies.

7.13 We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other concerned
personnel undertake measures to repair damages and ensure the immediate
utilization of the barbecue stalls at Villaba’s New Public Market.

7.14 Lastly, to protect the interest of the government and to prevent further loss
and wastage of government funds and resources, we recommend that the
Municipal Mayor thru the Municipal Engineer:

a.) strictly monitor the proper implementation of all projects, whether
contracts for infrastructure, goods or consulting services, if they are in
accord with the approved plans and specifications within the time
frame specified in the contract agreement; and
b.) identify contracts which require appropriate management actions. If
so, impose liquidated damages and/or administrative sanctions
particularly termination of contracts and blacklisting of contractors for
failure to comply with their obligations, in accordance with the
provisions of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184.

8.10 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:

a.) the Municipal Engineer to strictly adhere to the project duration
stipulated per contract and exercise diligence in the inspection of
completed projects immediately after the reported date of completion
to determine whether projects were completed on time, taking
considerations on Variation Orders or Time Extension, if any, and
make sure that all materials and prescribed procedures conform with
the Program of Work, Detailed Estimates and Plan; and
b.) the Municipal Accountant to impose Liquidated Damages, equal to
one-tenth (1/10) of one (1) percent (1%) of the cost of the unfinished
portion of the project for each day of delay.

8.11 Also, to protect the interest of the government and to prevent further loss and
wastage of government funds and resources, we recommended that the
following measures be undertaken for future projects of similar in nature:
a.) Strictly monitor implementation of all projects, whether contracts for
infrastructure, goods or consulting services, in accordance with the
approved plans and specifications within the time frame specified in the
contract agreement; and
b.) Identify contracts which require appropriate management actions. If
so, impose liquidated damages and/or administrative sanctions
particularly termination of contracts and blacklisting of contractors for
failure to comply with their obligations in accordance with the
provisions of Sections 68 and 69, and Appendix 17 of the Revised IRR
of R.A. 9184.

9.5 We recommended that the Local Development Council re-appropriate the
balances of completed projects to other PPAs so that funds are utilized to its
optimum to help achieve the desirable socio-economic development and
environmental outcomes of the LGU.

11.6 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and government officials and
employees concerned:

a) submit the lacking supporting documents noted in Appendix D.5 to
avoid suspension in audit; and
b) refund the excess amount of Daily Travel Expense claimed for their
respective travels totaling ₱215,518.37 (Appendix D.6);

11.7 We also recommended management to stop granting travel allowances more
than what is allowed under Executive Order No. 77, to avoid incurrence of
irregular expenditures and disallowances in audit.

14.6 We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee instruct the end users
to refrain from indicating in the purchase requests form and/or bidding
documents the brand names and technical specification tailor fitting to a
particular brand of the goods which the LGU intends to procure, to promote
competitiveness in all its procurement activities, pursuant to the principles
prescribed in RA 9184 and its Revised IRR.

1.7 We recommended, and the Management agreed, that the Municipal Mayor
direct:
1. the Acting Municipal Treasurer to:
a. require its Collectors to prepare their RCDs; and
b. turn-over their collections to the designated LO at end of each business
day; and
2. the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting journal entries.

2.5 We recommended, and the Management agreed, that the Municipal Mayor
require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary correcting
journal entries to adjust the erroneous booking of PPE accounts.
2.6 During the Exit Conference, the Municipal Accountant expressed his willingness
to comply with the above recommendation.

3.4 We recommended, and the Management agreed, that the Municipal Mayor
direct:
1. the former SDO to immediately settle her unliquidated CF cash
advances; and
2. the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting journal
entries upon liquidation thereof.

4.7 We recommended, and the Management agreed, that the Municipal Mayor
stop the practice of granting additional cash advances to Accountable
Officers with an existing unsettled previous cash advance.

5.6 We recommended, and the Management agreed, that the Municipal Mayor
direct the Municipal POPCOM Officer – Designate to liquidate immediately
the fund transfer receive from the POPCOM – Region VIII.

6.6 We recommended, and the Management agreed, that the Municipal Mayor:
a) submit justification on the unimplemented/unfinished projects;
b) initiate the procurement and implementation process to realize the
programmed projects as planned; and
c) ensure that development projects are well-planned and procurement-
and-implementation-ready.

7.6 We recommended, and the Management agreed, that the Municipal Mayor
direct the Municipal Engineer to:
1. submit an explanation for the delayed implementation of the said
project; and
2. submit a recommendation to her, endorsing the imposition of liquidated
damages on the delayed implementation of the said project, if
warranted.

8.5 We recommended, and the Management agreed, that the Municipal Mayor
direct the MDRRMO to ensure and expedite the implementation of
identified PPAs, to strengthen the agency’s capacity in mitigating and
preparing for, responding to and recovering from the impact of disasters.

9.4 We recommended, and the Management agreed, that the Municipal Mayor
direct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting journal
entry to correct the effects of said erroneous journal entry.

10.7 We recommended, and the Management agreed, that the Municipal Mayor
require both the Municipal Accountant and the Acting Municipal Treasurer
to:
a. reconcile the discrepancies totaling ₱607,570.79 between the amount
withheld and the amount remitted for the current year;
b. reconcile and identify with the employees’ individual ledger cards the
previous years’ outstanding balance of ₱2,095,109.69;
c. remit immediately the identified/reconciled balance of Due to GSIS, Pag-
IBIG and PhilHealth accounts as at December 31, 2023; and
d. effect the necessary accounting entries based on the result of
reconciliation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, Inventory Committee and Municipal Accountant implement and follow the prescribed guidelines and procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006, particularly the conduct of reconciliation of inventory count per RPCPPE with property and accounting records, for the purpose of one-time cleansing of PPE account balances of the LGU.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare an adjusting entry for the reclassification of Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment.

Also, we recommended that the Property Officer maintain and update the PCs on a regular basis.

Lastly, we recommended that the Property Officer and Municipal Accountant:
Undertake collaborative procedures to ensure that all PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective records and that the PCs maintained by the Property Officer and the PPELCs maintained by the Accounting Office are reconciled, and
Prepare the necessary report on the results of the reconciliation to the Municipal Mayor for his appropriate action on non- existing/ missing properties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the Municipal Engineer and MPDC and other concerned personnel ensure that the PPAs under the   20% DF are well-planned, as well as procurement-and-implementation-ready such that the factors that may impede implementation, such as lot ownership concerns shall be considered.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
terminate the unperformed portion of the contracts for abandoned road concreting projects presented in Table 4 provided that the contractors failed to proceed expeditiously and without delay despite a written notice by the Municipality;
demand the refund of the unrecovered portion of advances to contractors, if any;
consider blacklisting of the defaulting contractors; and
ensure completion of the abandoned PPAs through implementation by administration or award the same to qualified contractors.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer:
together with the municipal inspectorate team, ensure 100% completion of the implemented projects before the issuance of the Certificate of Completion and payment of final billing to contractors, and
submit justification on why the aforementioned unfinished project was approved and reported as 100% completed, for our proper audit disposition of the expenditure.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer submit certification from the Punong Barangays of Barangays Mahalo, Lewing and Look that LED lights were removed and replaced with ordinary light bulbs by the barangay personnel with explanation on doing so to prove the Municipal Engineer’s justification and for our proper audit disposition of the expenditure.

Henceforth, we recommended that Municipal Mayor together with the Municipal Engineer and other concerned personnel ensure that Variation Orders be prepared and approved within the timeframe prescribed and that the cost of the variations shall be considered on the re-computation of the cost of the project.

We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer, henceforth:
strictly inspect the PPAs based on the Program of Works and the approved Variation Orders before the preparation and signing of Final Inspection Report/Statement of Work Accomplishment/ Certificate of Completion, and
attach the approved Variation Order to the disbursement vouchers.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
together with the LDRRM Officer, ensure optimal utilization of LDRRM Fund thru implementation of all approved PPAs incorporated in the LDRRMF Investment Plan; and
stop charging not disaster related expenses to the LDRRM Funds.

We also recommended that the LDRRM Council thru the LDRRM Officer:
send letters to the source agencies of donations received and inquire whether the grants be utilized for other purpose since the intended objective of the funds is already difficult to attain due to the passing of time with reference to Item No. 5.2.4 of COA Circular 2012 dated September 12, 2012; and
discontinue the inclusion of PPAs in the LDRRM Investment Plan that are not related to disaster preparedness, mitigation and response activities.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer expedite the preparation of the final fund utilization reports of all implemented and completed PPAs funded by National Government Agencies.

It is also recommended to promptly return the unexpended balances to extinguish the Municipality’s liability in the books of accounts, and put the funds to use for other equally important projects of the Source Agencies.

We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer (MHO) and all the other municipal health workers to:
secure Certification by the Secretary of the DOH that the place of assignment/travel is a strife-torn/embattled/disease-infested/distress or isolated areas/stations, or areas declared under state of calamity or emergency, or with volcano activity or eruption, and
submit the following documents:
Special Order from the agency/ department head covering the assignment to hazardous/ difficult areas, and
lacking DTRs.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
submit the Hazard Pay, Subsistence and Laundry Allowances’ payrolls and their supporting documents for the month of February 2023, and
strictly observe the guidelines on the grant of Hazard Pay, Subsistence and Laundry Allowances.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
require the deputy collectors to remit all collections daily and intact to the Liquidating Officer or at least once a week, or as soon as the collections reach ₱5,000.00, and
issue a written authority deputizing the Barangay Treasurers to collect Community Tax on her behalf.  In the absence of said authority, require the collectors to immediately turnover collections and accountable forms to the Municipal Treasurer.  Henceforth, no accountable forms shall be issued to Barangay Treasurers without a written authority as deputy collectors.

We recommended that BAC furnish the Audit Team with copies of approved contracts and all documents forming part thereof within five days from their execution as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended that the BAC Chairman and Municipal Accountant ensure that the basic information on the Purchase Orders are completely and appropriately filled- up, including the date of conformity of the supplier.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
direct the Municipal Accountant to:
reconcile the accounting records with the property unit and determine the causes of the variance for proper accounting thereof; and
prepare and maintain PPELCs with complete significant information;
instruct the Supply Officer to prepare and maintain the property cards for each PPE account with complete significant information and reconcile the same with the ledger cards of the accounting unit;
create an Inventory Committee to conduct a complete annual physical count of all properties; and
require the Inventory Committee to prepare the RPCPPE with complete significant information in accordance with the prescribed format and reconcile the RPCPPE with the accounting records.
Make the necessary entries for PPE items costing below P15,000.0 and drop from the books PPEs account.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare appropriate journal entries to:
Recognize the cost of completed projects recorded from the Construction in Progress – Infrastructure Asset to the Infrastructure Asset account, duly supported with the Certificate of Project Completion, and
provide depreciation expense on the asset accounts that were already completed.
Trace the unsubstantiated balance of the CIP account and provide the appropriate correcting/adjusting entries thereof.
Prepare and maintain the CIPLCs with complete significant information on a per project basis.
Provide adequate disclosures such as the amount and the status of the projects under the Construction in Progress account in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to:
Require the  Municipal Treasurer to send  written notices to the payees one month before the checks become stale. Stale checks shall be reported and attached to the RCI prepared at the period of cancellation.
Require the Municipal Accountant to trace the unreconciled difference of P28,995.49 between the book balance shown in the ledger and the adjusted balance per book in the bank reconciliation statement and make the necessary adjustments thereof.
Require the  Municipal Accountant to prepare  the necessary adjusting entries for:
Stale checks over 6 months but not over 2 years amounting to P144,334.06; and
Unrecorded bank debit memo of P6,033.48, unrecorded interest earned of P38,144.39 and unrecorded prior years’ adjustment of P15,991.27

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer closely monitor the implementation of infrastructure projects and implement the calibrated measures on negative slippage as provided in GPPB Circular 03-2019 dated March 8, 2019.
We also recommended that the BAC:
Scrutinize the bidding documents submitted by the bidder and take appropriate actions on any misrepresentations discovered. Also, enforce the bidders to properly and completely fill-out the Statement of All On-going Contracts Including Contracts Awarded but not yet Started.
Undertake the post-qualification thoroughly and carefully evaluated the Statement of All On-going Contracts for any non-disclosure of previously awarded projects of the Municipality.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant obtain the necessary data from the Municipal Engineer for the computation of liquidated damages and impose the same to the contractor.
This is without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council to:
Stop the practice of appropriating and utilizing the LDRRMF for PPAs that is not within the four thematic areas of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management; and
incorporate in the LDRRMFIP and utilize the Special Trust Fund – DRRM for PPAs that align  with the four (4) thematic areas of disaster risk reduction and management.
Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that all PPAs charged to the DRRM Fund are incorporated in the LDRRMFIP; and
Municipal Accountant to ensure compliance of the rules set forth under COA Circular No. 2014-002 pertaining to the accounting and inventory for procured items out of the LDRRMF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Health Officer substantiate the payment of Hazard Pay, in compliance with DBM-DOH Joint Circular No. 1 s. 2012 dated November 29, 2012, DBM-DOH Joint Circular No. 1 s. 2016 dated July 15, 2016, and Section 2.1 of COA Circular No. 2023-004 dated June 14, 2023 by:
issuing a Special Order covering the assignment of public health workers to hazardous/difficult areas;
securing a Certification by the Secretary of the DOH that the place of assignment/travel is a disease-infested/distress area; and
require the affected employees to refund the overpayment of benefits received.
Likewise, we recommended the Municipal Accountant to henceforth ensure that disbursement vouchers are supported with complete documentation and strictly observe the guidelines in the computation of the hazard pay, subsistence and laundry allowances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Budget Officer to strictly monitor the utilization of the Peace and Order Fund to avoid disbursements in excess of the appropriation for each PPAs.
We likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the Local Peace and Order Council:
For Confidential Funds,
submit supporting documentary evidence of payment, among others, for submission to the Intelligence and Confidential Fund Audit Unit (ICFAU) in a sealed envelope signed by the Special Disbursing Officer (SDO);
submit Certification of the Accountable Officer of the Confidential Funds signed under oath containing the requirements enumerated in Item 4.15 and other necessary documentary requirements required in COA-DBM-DILG-GOCCs-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015; and
strictly comply with the provisions of COA-DBM-DILG-GOCCs-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015 on the entitlement, release, use and reporting of Confidential Funds.

Municipal Accountant to:

direct the Municipal Assessor to expedite the process of titling to prevent any possible risks of claim from third parties.

Maintain Subsidiary Ledgers (SLs) for each bank account

include in the LDRRMFIP Special Trust Fund (STF) only those PPAs that supports solely for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction and management activities; and

assess the approval process for expenditures to ensure that they are correctly charged to the appropriate accounts; and
Strengthen internal controls in check and balance to prevent similar situations in the future.

We commended the efforts made by the Municipality in the efficient and effective accomplishment of identified priority development projects in CY 2022.
