This is crucial to ensure that the desired socio-economic development and environmental management goals are met, thus fulfilling the objectives of the 20% Development Fund.
Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer review and authenticate the unobligated balance depicted in tables 18 and 19, with the possibility of reallocating it for supplemental appropriation.

We recommended that Municipal Engineer:
conscientiously carry out the detailed engineering to ensure that issues like discrepancy of actual lot location and lack of assigned skilled workers and issue and the viability of the project site are property addressed during feasibility or preliminary engineering study prior to project implementation.
Discuss with the contractor potential challenges or obstacles that may arise during the project implementation and how the contractor plans to address them. A proactive approach to problem-solving can help mitigate delays.
Request regular progress updates from the contractor to stay informed about the project's status. Address any issues or concerns promptly to prevent them from escalating into delays.
We also recommended the Municipal Mayor through the Bids and Awards Committee ensure that the contractor has adequate resources, including manpower, equipment, and materials, to complete the project within the specified timeframe.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to:
Review Post-Qualification Processes. Review and strengthen the post-qualification stage of the technical evaluation process to ensure that lapses in the verification of ongoing contracts disclosure do not compromise the integrity of the bidding process. Implement additional checks or reviews to verify bidder qualifications accurately;

Enhance Documentation Review. Strengthen the documentation review process within the BAC by assigning dedicated personnel responsible for scrutinizing bidder submissions. This includes cross-referencing the disclosed ongoing contracts with relevant supporting documents to ensure completeness and accuracy.

Automate Disclosure Verification. Explore the possibility of implementing digital tools or software solutions to streamline the verification and validation of ongoing contracts disclosure. Automation can help flag down inconsistencies or discrepancies more efficiently, reducing the likelihood of oversights

Lastly, we recommended the BAC to require the contractor to resubmit updated Statement of Statement of the bidder of all its ongoing government and private contracts, including contracts awarded but not yet started. This is without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Suspension.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Health Officer to substantiate the payment of the Hazard Pay by:
Issuing a Special Order covering the assignment of Public Health Worker to Hazardous Areas.
Securing a certification from the Secretary of Health that the place of assignment/travel is a disease-infested area.
We also recommended the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Social Worker and Development Officer to secure a Certification from the Head of Office/Bureau or Service Unit or Field Office or by the LGU/Local DILG that they are assigned in remote and depressed areas or distressed or isolated Station
Likewise, we recommended the Municipal Accountant to henceforth ensure that disbursement vouchers are supported with complete documentation and consistently observe the guidelines in the computation of Magna Carta Benefits.

We commended the efforts of the Municipal Accountant in the conduct of thorough examination/analysis/review of the financial statements and verification of the books of accounts in Calendar Year 2023. Further, we also commended his effort in the reconciliation of certain material discrepancies and significant variances emanating from previous years. Nevertheless, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the other Department Heads, to exhaust all possible means and continue to exert utmost effort to reconcile the prior years’ variances that remained unaccounted as of December 31, 2023.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to: 

Require the GSO to maintain property cards and update records regarding acquisition, description, custody, disposal, and other information about the PPE, based on the source documents of the transactions, and reconcile with the accounting records and RPCPPE. 

Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain PPELCs and update records regarding acquisition, description, custody, estimated useful life, rate of depreciation, disposal, and other information about the PPE, based on the source documents of the transactions, and reconcile with the property records and RPCPPE. 

Instruct the Inventory Committee, in coordination with the GSO and Municipal Accountant to: 

Conduct the physical count of PPE, and implement the One-Time Cleansing of PPE in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006; 

Undertake the disposal of the unserviceable properties in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006; and 

Cause the submission of a complete Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year.

We recommend the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Health Officer, to: 

secure Certification by the Secretary of the DOST/DOH/DND/Director of the PHILVOCS that the place of assignment/travel is a strife torn/embattled/disease-infested/distress or isolated areas/stations, or areas declared under state of calamity or emergency, or with volcano activity or eruption; and 

attach the complete supporting documents to the payrolls/vouchers. We likewise recommend the Municipal Accountant to process disbursement vouchers only when they are supported with complete documentation, and adhere strictly to the guidelines on the grant of hazard pay, subsistence, and laundry allowance. 
We recommend the Public Health Workers presented in Table 2 to refund the amount equivalent to the overpayment. This is without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Suspension and/or Suspension.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the Local Peace and Order Council, to direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to: 

Conduct a thorough review of the AIP of the LGU incorporating therein detailed list of PPAs and their corresponding appropriation for inclusion in the local government budget; and 

Utilize the POPS fund according to the approved AIP, Annual Budget, POPS plan to implement PPAs which directly contribute to the maintenance of the peace and order and public safety of the community.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the MDRRMO, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to transfer all unexpended balances of the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund at the end of each year, henceforth.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendation that earnest effort to implement all the approved PPAs should principally come from the Municipal Mayor being the head of agency identified as the responsible person in the Joint Memorandum Circular from DBM-DOF-DILG.
Likewise, we recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the MPDC to:

Address the issues affecting the delay on the bidding-related activities and ensure that all approved PPAs are procurement and implementation-ready and warrant the implementation of all approved PPAs for the optimal utilization of the appropriation for development projects; and

Continuously adhere to the provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1, dated November 4, 2020 to ensure that maximum attainment of desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcome of the Municipality is achieved and for the realization of the intended purpose of the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the BAC Chairperson to:

Submit justification why the three contractors were awarded with contracts despite the non-disclosure of the percentage of actual accomplishment vis-à-vis percentage of planned accomplishment of its on-going projects and despite the incomplete disclosure of its ongoing projects and the non-disclosure of value of its outstanding works including contracts awarded but not yet started in the computation of the Net Financial Contracting Capacity; and

Require submission of the latest Business Tax Returns within five calendar days from receipt by the bidder of the notice from the BAC that the bidder has the Lowest Calculated Bid or Highest Rated Bid.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

1.Appoint a qualified employee to be the General Services Officer who: (a) will take custody of and be accountable for all properties, real or personal, owned by the local government unit and those granted to it in the form of donation, reparation, assistance and counterpart of joint projects; and (b) shall prepare and maintain the records on the acquisition, description, custody, disposal and other information about the property, plant and equipment based on the source documents of the transactions in the Property Cards;

2. Instruct the Inventory Committee to:

a. Formulate a Physical Inventory Plan and schedule for the conduct of physical inventory that will complement with the time of the members of the committee in order not to prejudice the operations of the Municipality. Also, conduct the physical count pursuant to 6.1 and 6.2 of COA Circular No. 2020-006; and

b. Cause the submission of a complete Report of the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year.

3. Require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Trace the differences in the account balances and reconcile her records with the financial statements;  

b. Act on the preparation and maintenance of the PPE ledger cards based on the source documents of the transactions; and

c. Provide the necessary disclosures required in IPSAS 17 on PPE.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the differences among the details/schedule, General Ledger, Subsidiary Ledger, Statement of Financial Position, and Notes to Financial Statement of the Due to NGA account as required by Section 121 of Presidential Decree 1445 and Paragraph 27 of the International Public Sector Accounting Standards.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the differences among the General Ledger, Subsidiary Ledger, Statement of Financial Position, and Notes to Financial Statement of the Due to LGU account and draw an adjusting entry to record the cash donations amounting to P5,017,350.00 to the Trust Liabilities – DRRMF account instead of the Due to LGU account.

In view of the foregoing audit observations, we recommended that:

1. Earnest effort to implement all the approved PPAs should principally come from the Municipal Mayor being the head of agency identified as the responsible person in the Joint Memorandum Circular from DBMDOF-DILG. 

2.  Ensure the implementation of the approved PPAs for the optimal utilization of the appropriation for development projects.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that Municipal Mayor direct the LGU’s Project Monitoring Committee in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Officer - Designate to strictly implement and observe the guidelines on the monitoring and evaluation of the development projects and prepare and submit the quarterly progress reports and monitoring and evaluation program, among others, as required in DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2020-01 and Executive Order No. 17 s. of 2022 dated October 12, 2022 to ensure the efficient implementation of the development projects.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the:

2. Municipal Assessor to:

a. Update and reconcile records relative to real properties within the Municipality and its owners;


  3. Municipal Treasurer to: 
 a. Reconcile the updated tax payer’s index card with the records of the Municipal Assessor; 
 b. Submit the Tax payer’s index card to the Municipal Accountant at the start of year as basis in recording RPT receivables; and 
 c. Develop collection strategies and intensify the collection of real property taxes and special education taxes to provide additional sources of funds of the Municipality.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to:

Prepare and maintain subsidiary ledgers for transfers of agency’s unutilized DRRMF to the special trust fund by year of transfer’ among others

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council ensure that the municipality is prepared for calamities as prescribed in NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 by procuring standard disaster response equipment required in DILG Memorandum Circular 2012-73.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor commission the General Services Officer and Municipal Assessor or create a Team to: 
a. Gather and secure all the required legal documents necessary for registration such as but no limited to original deed of sale, deed of donation, original certificate of titles, etc; and 
b. Cause the registration and transfer the land in the name of the Municipality.

We also recommended that the Municipal Assessor make representation with the Sangguniang Bayan to appropriate funds to be used for the expenses in processing the Transfer Certificate of Title.

We recommended that the Municipal Social and Development Officer together with the Property Supply Officer: 

Conduct physical count of all the remaining donated items 

Prepare inventory list of the remaining donated items for DRRM every month or every quarter or until all donations are fully distributed; and  

Henceforth, prepare all the reports and documents required to account and monitor the receipt and issuances of all donations in kind and submit a copy of all the reports to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Supply Officer furnish the Municipal Accountant with the duplicate copies of all Acknowledgment Receipt of in-kind donations  and Inventory List of donated Items for DRRM.  

We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant summarize the data from the documents submitted by the MSWDO, and disclose the necessary information in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

Submit the lacking documentary requirements presented in Table 1, such as the Abstract of Quotation, BAC Resolution recommending award of contract to LCRB, Proof of Distribution to the Beneficiaries/Distribution List, and Liquidation Report. This is without prejudice to the issuance of Notices of Suspension as a result of our audit; and

Henceforth, secure complete documentation on all disbursements to ensure the validity and propriety of the disbursement transactions. Significantly, adhere to the policy of non-payment of claims without complete documentation.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and certify a monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of Local DRRM Fund;
We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to charge depreciation annually on all depreciable assets in accordance with the provisions of IPSAS 17.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council to optimize the utilization of the LDRRMF in order to strengthen its disaster preparedness and avoid potential damages during disasters.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to:

send within 10 days before the expiration of the prescribed period a written reminder under signature of the Mayor or his duly authorized representative enjoining officials and employees concerned to settle their cash advance.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to request for the write off of the aforementioned accounts in accordance with COA Circular No, 2016-005 supported with an aging of dormant receivables and fund transfer, indicating in the remarks column the existence of the applicable column the existence of the applicable conditions, as follows:

a. Absence of records or documents to validate/support the claim and/or unreconciled reciprocal ac counts
b.	Death of the accountable officer/employee/debtor
c.	Unknown whereabouts of the accountable officer/employee/debtor, and that he/she could not be located despite diligent efforts to find him/her
d.	Exhaustion of all possible remedies by the Management to collect the receivables and to demand liquidation of cash advances and fund transfers
e.	No pending case in court involving the subject dormant accounts.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to 

confirm with the GSIS, PAG-IBIG and PhilHealth the veracity of the negative balances and properly disclose the abnormal balances in the Notes to Financial Statements,

adapt monitoring mechanism to detect variances in the amount to be remitted and withheld, and 

accounting records such as cash receipts journal, check disbursement journal, cash disbursement journal, general and subsidiary ledgers must be updated monthly in order to determine the exact amount to be remitted to the GSIS, PhiHealth and PAG-IBIG.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Treasurer to submit a Status Report on the Revenue Code and coordinate with the Sangguniang Bayan to look into the possible updating of the Revenue Code based on the report of the Municipal Treasurer.
We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Assessor to facilitate the titling of untitled lots.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to effect immediately the Adjusting Journal Entries (AJEs) for the provision of the depreciation of the listed PPEs in consonance with the rules and regulations stated in the IPSAS 17.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the officials concerned to implement the projects listed under the appropriation ordinance so as to effect timely delivery of services to the constituents.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor adopt stern regulations in the pursuit of zeroing-in the due and demandable cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to exert effort in enforcing on the immediate settlement of audit disallowances.  Likewise, ensure that all employees who are retiring or transferring to other agencies shall first settle the disallowances and charges for which they are liable in accordance with Section 7 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Solid Waste Management Focal Person to maintain disposal site records in the Residual Containment Area.

We recommended that a mechanism to measure the volume of waste and type of waste should be placed at the RCA, which will provide baseline data to assist in developing effective waste minimization strategies and future plans for waste management.

We recommended that the RCA be fenced with a secured gate at the entrance, to minimize illegal visitors or scavengers.  This will also help address the occupational health and safety issues by limiting access.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Assessor to expedite the processing of documents for the titling of lands owned by the Municipality.  We further recommend that before any construction is made, land ownership thru a certificate of title is obtained.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to make reconciliation and verify who among the employees is deficient in paying the loan obligations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive: 1.) Compel the Municipal Accountant to review the details of the Due from NGAs, Due from LGUs and Other Receivable accounts and exert all efforts to identity the purpose of the fund transfers; 2.) Direct the Municipal Accountant to comply with the reportorial requirements on funds transferred to local or national government agencies or and other debtors in order to avoid accumulation of unliquidated balances of the accounts.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council to maximize the utilization of the MDRRMF in order to achieve the objectives of the fund and ensure readiness of the LGU in times of calamity.

Due to the aforementioned observations which remained unimplemented, we
hereby reiterated our previous audit recommendations that the Municipal
Mayor:
1. direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. reconcile the accounting records with the property unit and
determine the causes of the variance for proper accounting
thereof;
b. compute and recognize depreciation on all of the depreciable
assets of the Municipality;
c. prepare and maintain PPELCs with complete significant
information; and
d. coordinate with the GSO and undergo disposition procedures for
non-existing/missing PPE.
2. require the GSO to prepare and maintain property cards for
reconciliation with the Accounting Unit; and
3. create an Inventory Committee to henceforth, conduct a complete annual
physical count of the PPE and prepare the RPCPPE duly signed by
appropriate signatories; and
4. require the Inventory Committee to properly identify the PPE in the
RPCPPE based on its proper account classification, and reconcile the
RPCPPE with the accounting records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to:
1. prepare the appropriate adjusting journal entries to transfer the
amounts deposited from the General Fund that were due to other
funds;
2. draw journal entry vouchers to record all valid reconciling items that
require adjustment, duly supported with complete documentation to
correct the balance of the Cash in Bank-Local Currency, Current
Account; and
3. trace the discrepancy of P22,712.15 between the adjusted balance per
books reported in the BRSs and per SL to ensure proper
reconciliation of the accounts;
4. reconcile the variance of P19,447.73 between the balance per books
and per bank through preparation of the BRS; and
5. henceforth, monthly prepare and submit the BRSs to reconcile any
errors or items for adjustment in the balance per bank and per books.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
 Provide the appropriate journal entry upon disbursement of funds and
only debit the Due to NGAs account upon liquidation of funds to the
source agency, pursuant to Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009
dated December 1, 2015;
 Implement only program, projects and activities from NGAs upon
actual receipt of funds; and
 Provide the appropriate journal entry for the liquidation of funds from
DOLE amounting to P1,500,000.00.

1.23 We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor order the:

a. GSO-Designate to maintain and update records regarding acquisition,
description, custody, disposal, and other information about the PPE,
based on the source documents of the transactions, and reconcile with the
Accounting Records and RPCPPE.

b. Municipal Accountant to update the record regarding acquisition,
description, custody, estimated life, rate of depreciation, disposal, and
other information of PPE in the PPELCs and ensure that the total balance of
PPELCs tally with the balances of controlling PPE accounts in General
Ledger and the Financial Statements.

c. Inventory Committee, in coordination with the GSO and Municipal
Accountant:

i. To complete the physical inventory within the prescribed period for one-time cleansing of the PPE accounts.

ii. Prepare a report on the result of the physical count through RPCPPE including list of unserviceable PPEs, missing PPEs, and items found at station.

iii. Any discrepancy between the RPCPPE, PPELCs, and PCs shall be
investigated, verified, and adjusted. The total reconciled balances
for each PPE account shall be established as the correct balance of
PPE at year-end; and;

iv. To undertake the disposition of the non-existing and unserviceable
PPEs in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006.

d. Temporary relief of the Inventory Committee from their daily task to
devote their full time in the conduct of the physical inventory taking until
the same is completed.

2.14 We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct:

a. The Tomas Oppus Municipal Waterworks System Administration
(TOMWASA) to submit the Monthly Consumer Account Reports
indicating therein the consumption and amount due per consumer to the
Office of Municipal Accountant.

b. The Municipal Treasurer to submit the duly notarized Lease Contract for
every tenant of the Commercial Building and Public Market to the
Municipal Accountant within 5 days from the notarization and a copy of
the cancellation of the contract.

c. The Municipal Accountant to record the uncollected Waterworks System
Fees and Rental Fees after verifying the accounts of the consumers and
tenants. Set up monthly receivables and recognize income based on the
Monthly Consumer Account Reports and Lease Contracts.

3.20 We recommended the Municipal Mayor, through the Local Development
Council (LDC) and Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator
(MPDC) to prioritize in the planning and approval of PPAs that are well-
planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready. Conduct due diligence
before a purchase of a lot is funded to ensure that the lots are free from any
claims to avoid interruptions in the implementation.

3.21 We also recommended to the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Budget
Officer and the MPDC to include in the annual budget preparation, the review
of continuing appropriation unexpended balances and those PPAs that cannot
be implemented, so that these could be reverted to the unappropriated surplus
and re-appropriated to other priority PPAs embodied in the Annual
Investment Program in order to prevent idle funds.

4.24 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the BAC Chairperson,
the following:

a. Send a written invitation to observers at least 5 days before each
procurement activity. In the event that a procurement activity has to be
postponed, the observers shall be notified immediately of the change in
schedule, either through email or by personal service.

b. Submit justification on the failure to conduct a pre-bid conference on the
two Public Bidding: Construction of Commercial Building B Phase II and
Improvement/Rehabilitation of Felicity Dream Square. Henceforth,
conduct pre-bid conference for contracts to be bid with an ABC of One
Million Pesos (₱1,000,000.00) or more.

c. Require the submission of the latest Quarterly Income Tax Return and
Business Tax Return within five (5) calendar days from receipt by the
bidder of the notice from the BAC that the bidder has the Lowest
Calculated Bid or Highest Rated Bid.

d. Carefully verify, validate, and ascertain all statements made in the
documents submitted by the bidder so that any misrepresentations will be
detected and to declare as eligible only those who strictly comply with the
provisions of the RIRR of RA 9184.

e. Submit justification why the contractor was awarded with five (5) new
contracts despite having negative slippage and misrepresenting itself by
not declaring all its on-going contracts.

5.17 We recommended the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Engineer to
ensure that projects are meticulously and judiciously planned, and:

a. Monitor compliance of the contractor to the terms of the contract and
accomplishment based on the approved plans and specifications, and any
variation shall be authorized through an approved Variation Order, either
a Change Order or Extra Work Order, whichever is applicable.

b. Any changes of requirements for projects implemented by Administration
shall be reflected in the Annual Procurement Plan to avoid the
procurement of unnecessary materials.

c. Return to GSO any unused materials for safekeeping and proper
accounting.

5.18 We further recommended the Municipal Mayor to establish Constructors
Performance Evaluation System (CPES) Implementing Units (IUs) who shall
be responsible for evaluating the performance of their contractors using the
NEDA-Approved CPES Guidelines during construction and upon completion
for the type of project being implemented, regardless of the contract amount
and funding source to ensure that the contractor will faithfully perform its
obligations under the contract.

6.16 We recommended the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Planning and
Development Coordinator (MPDC) that in the preparation of the Annual
Investment Plan, conduct a thorough review of the Peace and Order and
Public Safety (POPS) Plan of the LGU and ensure that all proposed PPAs are
aligned with the priorities outlined in the POPS Plan and duly supported by
the Peace and Order Plan.

6.17 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the Local Peace
and Order Councils to include only those PPAs that are directly related to
crime prevention and law enforcement activities, capability development for
personnel of law enforcement agencies, volunteers/partners, or programs for
anti-illegal drug, illegal gambling, counter-insurgency, and/or counter-
terrorism efforts, among others that will directly contribute to the
maintenance of the peace and order and public safety.

7.17 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Health
Officer:

a. issue a Special Order covering the assignment of public health workers
to hazardous/difficult areas,

b. secure Certification by the Secretary of the DOH that the place of
assignment/travel is a strife-torn/embattled/disease-infested/distress or
isolated areas/stations, or areas declared under state of calamity or
emergency, or with volcano activity or eruption, and

c. attach the complete supporting documents to the disbursement
vouchers.

7.18 We likewise recommended the Municipal Accountant approve disbursement
vouchers that are supported with complete documentation and strictly observe
the guidelines on the grant of hazard pay, subsistence and laundry allowances.

7.19 This is without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.

8.10 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer
to return all unexpended balances of funds received to the source agencies.
Likewise, submit Liquidation Reports upon completion of the purpose of fund
transfers, together with all supporting documents and official receipt of
returned unexpended balance, if any, to the Auditor and furnish the same to
the source agency.

observations.managementAction: Submit for distribution sheet to the office of the Municipal Accountant. Submitted the lacking documents and review the documents and review the documents if its complete. Review the completeness of sinatures and dates of vouchers.
observations.reason:
Distribution sheet had been submitted.
Documents are submitted and recorded. 
Vouchers are being reviewed before check is prepared

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:

1. the CE Technical Enterprises to promptly accomplish the delivery of the 121 non-motorized/ paddled banca, and ensure renewal and supplemental of performance security pursuant to Section 39 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184;

2. the PESO Manager and Municipal Agriculturist to closely monitor the contractor’s fulfillment of the above recommendation. 

In case of noncompliance, the contractor should refund the advance payment for mobilization and undelivered billed items, the LGU should terminate the contract, forfeit the performance security and further recommend for blacklisting and perpetual disqualification from participating in any public bidding as provided under Section 69 and Appendix 17 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184; and

3. the Municipal Accountant to impose Liquidation Damages, equal to one-tenth (1/10) of one percent (1%) of the cost of the delayed goods scheduled for delivery for every day of delay until such items are completely delivered and accepted by the Municipality.

Also, to protect the interest of the government and to prevent further loss and wastage of government funds and resources, we recommended that the following measures for future projects of similar nature be undertaken:

1. Strictly monitor implementation of all projects, whether contracts for infrastructure, goods or consulting services, in accordance with the approved plans and specifications within the time frame specified in the contract agreement; and 

2. Identify contracts which require appropriate management actions. If so, impose liquidated damages and/or administrative sanctions particularly termination of contracts and blacklisting of contractors for failure to comply with their obligations in accordance with the provisions of Sections 68 and 69, and Appendix 17 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184.
observations.managementAction: We will adhere to the recommendations.
observations.reason: All non-motorized paddled bancas have been delivered.  Liquidated damages have been imposed on the contractors.  PPAs are now being monitored so that it will adhere with all rules and regulations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to (i) require the daily remittance by the collectors of the collections for the day, duly supported with the Daily Statement of Collections and Accountable Forms (DSCAF) to preclude the misuse of cash in their possession; and (ii) stop the practice of weekly remittance/turn over by the collectors of collections to the Municipal Treasurer, retaining collections more than the maximum accountability.

We also recommended strict compliance with the pertinent rules and regulations in handling collections and deposit provided in COA Circular No. 92-382, New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual, Volume I, and the bonding requirement in Treasury Circular No. 02-2019.
observations.managementAction: We will adhere to the recommendations.
observations.reason: All collections are remitted and deposited on time.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to:

1. implement other tax collection mechanisms and strategies to encourage delinquent taxpayers to settle their tax liabilities and increase RPT collections of the Municipality;

2. prepare a certified list of all real property tax delinquencies which remained uncollected or unpaid for at least one (1) year including reasons for such non-collection; and

3. submit such list to the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) on or before December 31 of the year immediately succeeding the year in which the delinquencies were incurred, and request for assistance from the SB in the enforcement of the remedies for collection provided in RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: We will adhere to the recommendations.
observations.reason: List of RPT delinquent taxpayer is regularly submitted to the Provincial Treasurer.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant to:

1. submit to the Audit Team within five (5) days, the required lacking documents enumerated above to prove regularity and legality of the corresponding disbursements.  This is without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Suspension and/ or Disallowance, if warranted; 

2. ensure that all disbursement vouchers are properly supported with complete documentation and in no case shall a disbursement be paid without the necessary supporting documents; and

3. strictly observe the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, COA Circular 2012-001 and relevant/applicable GPPB Resolutions to ensure adherence to the principles of transparency, competitiveness and public monitoring.
observations.managementAction: We will adhere to the recommendations.
observations.reason: Documents have been submitted to COA and all transactions are now being checked for completeness of supporting documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the (i) Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) require the winning bidder/contractor to submit Construction Safety and Health Program approved by DOLE in compliance DOLE DO No. 13 in the succeeding infrastructure projects; and ii) Municipal Engineer to require inclusion of Occupational Safety and Health as separate pay item in every infrastructure project implemented in compliance with DPWH D.O. No. 56 s. 2005 dated April 20, 2005 and monitor the proper implementation of the said program to ensure its compliance to with the requirements of the DOLE and that workers are adequately protected against occupational risks.
observations.managementAction: We will adhere to the recommendations.
observations.reason: Contractors are now required to submit the CSHP.

observations.managementAction: The responsible department and persons will adhere to the said recommendations.
observations.reason: All vaccines from DOH are properly documented already and there were strategies in place for distribution of vaccines.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

1. Municipal Accountant to (i) submit to the Audit Team the lacking documents and a written explanation why the disbursement vouchers were approved despite the deficiency; (ii) ensure the accrual basis of accounting were adopted in recording of transactions; and (iii) review the completeness of the supporting documents of the disbursement vouchers before forwarding these to the Municipal Mayor for approval;

2. Municipal Health Officer to ensure completeness of distribution list and the required documentation of events for future reference and proof in validating the transactions.

These recommendations are without prejudice to the issuance of Notice(s) of Suspension/ Disallowance, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: We will adhere to the recommendations.
observations.reason: Vouchers are now thoroughly checked for completeness of supporting documents.  All of the documents are checked for completeness.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to:

1. issue an Acknowledgment Receipt for In-Kind Donations for DRRM or his authorized representative for the receipt of in-kind donations.

designate the office to receive all in-kind donations who will:

2. establish an inventory management system on receipt, distribution and/ or utilization of in-kind donations, and
  
3. prepare the Summary/ List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances every month or every quarter supported by Acknowledgment Receipts, Proof of Receipt by and Distribution to the Beneficiaries/ end-users, and Inventory of Remaining Balances, and 
4. submit to the Property Custodian copy of the Acknowledgment Receipt for donated equipment and other tangible items which costs ₱15,000 or less and has a life of more than a year.

require the designated Property Custodian or direct the Municipal Treasurer, in the absence of a duly appointed GSO to:

1. Maintain Stock Cards/Property Card for all donations in-kind received and update regularly;

2. Maintain Registry of Donated Relief Goods for each kind of inventory items; 

3. Prepare Property Acknowledgment Receipt (PAR) for issuance of donated PPEs and Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) for the issuances of tangible items which costs ₱15,000.00 or less and have a life of more than a year and are not considered as PPE to establish accountability over them and furnish the Municipal Accountant with the PAR and ICS; and

4. require the departments responsible for the distribution of relief goods and other supplies (MDRRMO, DSWDO and RHU) to submit Proof of Receipt by and Distribution to the Beneficiaries/ end-users to the Office referred to in letter (b);

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant summarize the data from the documents obtained, compare records with Registry of Donated Relief Goods maintained by the Property Custodian or by the designated MDRRMO for check and balance, and accordingly disclose all necessary information in the Notes to the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: We will adhere to the recommendations.
observations.reason: GSO office had been established and is responsible for all kind of donations.   All donations are monitored by GSO.  Fast track the preparation of reports.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, as the responsible officer, ensure that all paid disbursement vouchers and the supporting documents are properly stamped “PAID”.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure the completeness of the supporting documents attached to the disbursement voucher, and that the latter is properly accomplished before forwarding the same to the LCE for approval.

Finally, we recommended that the LCE ensure strict compliance by the officials concerned with the rules and regulations under COA Circular Nos. 92-389 and 78-79, and Volume II of NGAS Manual.
observations.managementAction: We will adhere to the recommendations.
observations.reason: Disbursement Vouchers (DVs) are now stamped "paid".  DVs are thoroughly checked for complete SDs.  The Local Chief Executive had mandated all officials concerned to follow recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

1. Review at all times the correctness of the lapsing schedule as the basis in recording their monthly depreciation expense.
observations.managementAction: Recompute the depreciation expense for Road Networks.  Prepare adjusting entries to correct the carrying amounts of PPEs.  Review and correct the depreciation expense schedule.
observations.reason: Recompute and adjust the depreciation expense.  Prepared adjusting entries and adjust the PPE balances.  Depreciation schedules were reviewed and corrected.

observations.recommendation: Henceforth, adhere to the bidding process prescribed under RA 9184 and its RIRR, including the proper dissemination of bid opportunities and its result, the conduct of a strict evaluation of bids until the post qualification activity and the submission of contract documents with complete supporting documents.
observations.managementAction: The BAC will meticulously and strictly adhere to the bidding processes prescribed under RA 9184 and its IRR.
observations.reason: Required documents were submitted in 2022

observations.recommendation: We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as head of the agency, to instruct all department heads with fuel allotment to strictly monitor their fuel consumption in accordance with their approved budget and to utilize the fuel solely and strictly for its intended purpose.
observations.managementAction: The LGU will adhere to and comply on the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Procured fuel per approved APP.  The Mayor instructed the fuel custodian to separate the fuel  storage per office for proper utilization

observations.recommendation:
We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer:

1. Determine the cost or depreciated replacement cost of demolished pavements affected by road re-blocking projects undertaken by the LGU and submit the said data to the Municipal Accountant for de-recognition in the books; and 

2. Prepare the Certificate of Turn-Over of all barangay roads previously constructed by the LGU for acceptance by the respective Punong Barangay with information of its current appraised value for recording in Barangay books of accounts, copy furnished the Municipal Accountant, as basis of de-recognition in the LGU books;

3. We further recommended that the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant update, tally and harmonize their respective records so that the complete description and cost segregation of each road component of the road networks in the LGU will support the amounts in the books. 

4. Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant: (1) record the different components of the municipal roads, including the Road Lot components; and (2) maintain property ledger cards for each municipal road with complete description and cost segregation of its components based on the Municipal Engineer’s report.
observations.managementAction: The LGU will adhere to and comply on the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: Reconciliation is on-going

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create the Inventory, Disposal and Appraisal Committee to initiate the disposal of all unserviceable and damaged properties of the LGU following the procedures outlined in COA Circular No. 89-296 to prevent further deterioration and exposure to theft of these properties.
observations.managementAction: Issue an executive order creating the inventory, disposal, and appraisal committee.
observations.reason: Conduct the inventory count and inspection of unserviceable PPEs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the Disbursement Voucher and the Municipal Treasurer prepare the check, in favor of the Bureau of Treasury, to remit the unutilized balances of previous years’ fund transfers.
observations.managementAction: Return the balances of the funds to source agencies.
observations.reason: Balances have been returned to the National Treasury.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Chairman of the BAC and the BAC Secretariat submit copies of perfected contracts/purchase orders to the Auditor within five (5) days after execution/issuance thereof, to afford timely review and evaluation of the contracts.
observations.managementAction: BAC will strictly adhere to and comply on the recommendations.
observations.reason: Regularly submit the copies of perfected contracts to COA within the prescribed time.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant transfer the cost of all completed projects to the appropriate accounts in the Property, Plant and Equipment to close the Construction in Progress accounts; and record the related depreciation on the said assets.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant deduct retention money or require special bank guarantee from payments to suppliers/contractors as a form of warranty security.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant-Designate trace back the accounting entries in the Accounts Payable to determine the composition of the account and the causes of the discrepancy of ₱770,765.70 and prepare the adjusting entries, where necessary.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant-Designate reclassify to the proper Liability accounts those erroneously recorded as Accounts Payable.

We recommended that the MDRRMO and Municipal Budget Officer refrain from using the LDRRMF for administrative expenses and those that are not related to disaster management.

We also recommended that the Local DRRM Council consider JMC 2013-1 in the preparation of the LDRRMIP in order that all PPAs listed therein are in accordance with the said JMC.

We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer:

Request for the enactment of an Ordinance to make available the unexpended appropriation of CYs 2020 and 2021 totaling ₱1,101,476.05, which were reverted to the surplus of the General Fund, PRIOR to payments in CY 2022.

We recommended that the Program Coordinator of the TUPAD Program:

submit  to the Audit Team the lacking necessary documents listed in paragraph 10.4, to validate the disbursements totaling ₱487,500.00.
We recommend that the Municipality through its MSWMB should comply with the law regarding the establishment of Materials Recovery Facilities.
We recommend that the Municipal Mayor should expedite the hiring of the said vacant positions, and solicit some advice from the CSC in order to facilitate its immediate hiring

observations.reason: No applicants yet have applied for the position of Municipal Assessor.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that:

The Municipal Accountant-Designate coordinate with the former Municipal Accountant, in tracing the original accounting entries made and if warranted, prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries, to de-recognize the accounts from the books.
observations.managementAction: The previous designated Municipal Accountant concerned has already died.  But, the books were already adjusted accordingly, for those with supporting records.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that management require the Municipal Accountant:

To coordinate with the previous Municipal Accountants on the details of the beginning balance of P86,279.23 of the Due to Other Funds under the Special Education Fund and to make the proper adjustment in the books, duly supported with the necessary documents.

We recommend that management:

Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain complete subsidiary ledger for each special account in the General Fund, including the 20% Development Fund, and prepare the corresponding financial statements for each economic enterprise to form part of the schedules of the General Fund trial balance, as required under Sections 105, 107 and 110, Volume I of the NGAS Manual for LGUs.

We recommend that management:

Require the Office of the Municipal Accountant to:

Record the purchase of supplies and materials as inventory in corresponding General Ledgers and Supplies Ledger Cards.

Record promptly the submitted Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued from the Supply Office through the preparation of a Journal Entry Voucher.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record in the books of accounts the road construction projects totaling ₱9,228,860.00 in the General Fund under Road Networks account of the Property, Plant and Equipment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor secure immediately an authorization from the Sanggunian Bayan, by way of ratification, for the contracts covering the transactions totaling ₱21,602,121.60 which were charged against lump-sum and generic appropriations of the 20% Development Fund.

We also recommend that the Municipal Mayor and the Local Development Council specify in the Annual Investment Plan the completed details of each PPA that are to be funded from the 20% Development Fund and avoid lump-sum appropriations with generic terms, otherwise, obtain the corresponding SB Resolution prior to the implementation of such PPAs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Secretariat of the Bids and Awards Committee, require all winning bidders of infrastructure projects to submit their performance securities prior to the signing of contracts.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit justification for processing payments without the acceptance of goods delivered. Henceforth, process payments with duly filled-out and signed Acceptance and Inspection Report.

We reiterated our recommendation that the MGSO as head of the inventory committee, perform the following actions, to wit:
Conduct a complete physical count of all inventory items per semester, prepare and submit the Report on Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) to the Municipal Accountant for reconciliation and submit the RPCI to the Auditor concerned not later than January 31 of each year per Section 124 Chapter 7 Volume 1 of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual.
Cause the preparation of the Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) upon distribution/issuance of the stocks to the end users and summarize all RIS in the monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued and submit the same to the Municipal Accountant for recording purposes on a monthly basis.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the Municipal Engineer and MPDC and other concerned personnel ensure that the PPAs under the   20% DF are well-planned, as well as procurement-and-implementation-ready such that the factors that may impede implementation, such as lot ownership concerns shall be considered.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
terminate the unperformed portion of the contracts for abandoned road concreting projects presented in Table 4 provided that the contractors failed to proceed expeditiously and without delay despite a written notice by the Municipality;
demand the refund of the unrecovered portion of advances to contractors, if any;
consider blacklisting of the defaulting contractors; and
ensure completion of the abandoned PPAs through implementation by administration or award the same to qualified contractors.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer:
together with the municipal inspectorate team, ensure 100% completion of the implemented projects before the issuance of the Certificate of Completion and payment of final billing to contractors, and
submit justification on why the aforementioned unfinished project was approved and reported as 100% completed, for our proper audit disposition of the expenditure.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer submit certification from the Punong Barangays of Barangays Mahalo, Lewing and Look that LED lights were removed and replaced with ordinary light bulbs by the barangay personnel with explanation on doing so to prove the Municipal Engineer’s justification and for our proper audit disposition of the expenditure.

Henceforth, we recommended that Municipal Mayor together with the Municipal Engineer and other concerned personnel ensure that Variation Orders be prepared and approved within the timeframe prescribed and that the cost of the variations shall be considered on the re-computation of the cost of the project.

We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer, henceforth:
strictly inspect the PPAs based on the Program of Works and the approved Variation Orders before the preparation and signing of Final Inspection Report/Statement of Work Accomplishment/ Certificate of Completion, and
attach the approved Variation Order to the disbursement vouchers.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer attached Certification from the Punong Barangay involved.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

together with the LDRRM Officer, ensure optimal utilization of LDRRM Fund thru implementation of all approved PPAs incorporated in the LDRRMF Investment Plan; and
stop charging not disaster related expenses to the LDRRM Funds.

We also recommended that the LDRRM Council thru the LDRRM Officer:
send letters to the source agencies of donations received and inquire whether the grants be utilized for other purpose since the intended objective of the funds is already difficult to attain due to the passing of time with reference to Item No. 5.2.4 of COA Circular 2012 dated September 12, 2012; and
discontinue the inclusion of PPAs in the LDRRM Investment Plan that are not related to disaster preparedness, mitigation and response activities.

secure Certification by the Secretary of the DOH that the place of assignment/travel is a strife-torn/embattled/disease-infested/distress or isolated areas/stations, or areas declared under state of calamity or emergency, or with volcano activity or eruption, and

submit the following documents:

lacking DTRs.

submit the Hazard Pay, Subsistence and Laundry Allowances’ payrolls and their supporting documents for the month of February 2023, and
strictly observe the guidelines on the grant of Hazard Pay, Subsistence and Laundry Allowances.

require the deputy collectors to remit all collections daily and intact to the Liquidating Officer or at least once a week, or as soon as the collections reach ₱5,000.00, and
issue a written authority deputizing the Barangay Treasurers to collect Community Tax on her behalf. observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Assessor:
Update and reconcile all records pertaining to real properties within the Municipality and its owners. 
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer.
Submit the Taxpayer’s Index Card to the Municipal Accountant at the start of each year as basis in recording RPT Receivables; 

Submit the Certified list of Real Property Tax Delinquencies to the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) on or before December 31 of the year immediately succeeding the year in which the delinquencies were incurred, and request for assistance from the SB in the enforcement of the remedies for collection provided in RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
Property and Supply Unit to prepare the Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM for each kind of relief goods to document the receipts, issuances and balances of in-kind donations for proper monitoring pursuant to COA Circular 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014; 
MSWDO to prepare the: 

Distribution Lists with complete important details in order to properly document the in-kind donations distributed to the beneficiaries; and 

Acknowledgment Receipts for donated items received containing complete significant information such as the date, control numbers, quantity, unit, article, description and value as required in COA Circular 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014; and 

Municipal Accountant to provide adequate disclosures pertaining to DRRM not evident on the face of the financial statements such as but not limited to the quantity of received, issued and balance of donated relief goods per category such as food items, medicines and medical supplies, hygiene kits, and others pursuant to COA Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the:
MDRRMO to conduct collaborative planning with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator in prioritizing of PPAs prescribed under the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan and initiate the utilization of the Cash donations received in accordance with its purpose.

MDRRMO to fast-track and monitor the implementation of the appropriated PPAs and prioritize the re-channeling of funds of PPAs that could no longer be implemented to other prioritized PPAs as incorporated in the LDRRMFIP and AIP.

The MDRRMO to prepare a Supplemental LDRRMFIP to incorporate therein the prioritized PPAs to be approved by the DRRM Council, and make sure that all PPAs to be implemented for the year must be incorporated in the LDRRMFIP.
observations.managementAction:

create an Inventory Committee to conduct a complete annual physical count of all properties; and

observations.recommendation: The existence and accuracy of the Cash in Bank - Local Currency Account balances totaling P144,120,624.48 as of December 31, 2022 was not ascertained due to: a) non-reversion of stale checks totaling P194,748.14 from CYs 2019 to 2022; b) misclassification of a savings account amounting to P3,139,780.71 as Cash in Bank-Local Currency, Current Account; c) discrepancy of P516,967.74 between the balances reported in the subsidiary ledgers and the adjusted balances per books in the BRS; d) two bank accounts present in the books but not confirmed by the bank with balances totaling P296,335.49; and e) reconciling items which remained unrecorded/unadjusted totaling P63,868.84, contrary to Section 97 of PD No. 1445, Section  59, Chapter 3, of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, Items 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996, and COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015, thus resulting in doubtful balances of the accounts reported as at year end.

observations.recommendation: The reliability of the Due to National Government Agencies (NGAs) balance of P34,352,460.47 is doubtful because : a) completed project amounting P4,193,718.44 was not transferred to the General Fund upon completion; b) Unused balances of fund transfers received totaling P166,933.33 were not returned to the Source Agencies upon completion of projects/purpose; and c) balances of Due to NGAs totaling P192,407.13 cannot be substantiated due to lack of records, contrary to Section 97 of NGAS Manual for LGUs, Item 4.9 of COA Circular 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 and Section 111 of PD 1445, thus, the asset is understated and the liabilities is overstated by P4,193,718.44, the source agencies were deprived of the use of the funds and rendered the reported balance doubtful as to correctness.

observations.managementAction: We recommended to the Municipal Accountant to:

observations.recommendation: Various deficiencies were noted in the procurement of infrastructure projects totaling P44,074,491.54, such as: a) Non-imposition of liquidated damages for the delay of project completion; b) The award of contract to a contractor despite the latter’s negative slippage in its on-going contracts; c) Misrepresentation in the bidding documents; and d) Improper release of Retention Money, contrary to pertinent provisions of the IRR of RA 9184, thus, casting doubt as to the validity of the procurement as well as non collection of penalties that is due from the contractor for the delays incurred in the delivery of goods and infrastructure projects.
