observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the Inventory Committee to (i) conduct a comprehensive physical
inventory of PPE owned by the Municipality, identifying the proper
classification, individual identification, status of serviceability,
accountability and location thereof; (ii) prepare and submit a complete
RPCPPE to the Municipal Accountant not later than January 31 of each
year, copy furnished the Audit Team; and (iii) prepare and submit IIRUP
together with its supporting documents for disposal of unserviceable
properties;
b. require the Municipal Accountant to (i) expedite substantiation of recorded
unidentified PPE items based on the result of inventory count as
recommended above; (ii) drop from the PPE account the identified items
below the ₱15,000.00 threshold totaling ₱2,524,215.01; (iii) initiate the
proper turn-over of the completed projects to the respective Barangay-
Recipients supported with the properly accomplished turn-over documents,
and effect adjustments in the books; and (iv) maintain and update PPE
Ledger Cards for each category of assets and Real Property Ledger Cards
for land; and
c. consider the creation of a General Services Officer or direct the Municipal
Treasurer, in the absence of a duly appointed GSO, to take charge in setting
up and updating of Property Cards for proper monitoring, control and
accountability of purchased/acquired and issued PPEs.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipality avail the one-time cleansing of
the PPE Account and strictly adhere with the provisions of COA Circular No.
2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to establish PPE balances that are verifiable
as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. MSWD and LDRRMO to submit the distribution documents such as the
Relief Distribution Sheet, List of Affected individual/families and pictures
of actual distribution of relief goods to the Municipal Accountant for
proper recognition in the books of account;
b. Municipal Accountant to (i) submit to the Audit Team the lacking
documents and a written explanation why the disbursement vouchers were
approved despite the deficiency; and (ii) review the completeness of the
supporting documents of the disbursement vouchers before forwarding
these to the Municipal Mayor for approval;
c. Municipal Treasurer to review the completeness of the signatures and dates
on the disbursement voucher before a check is prepared and signed.

These recommendations are without prejudice to the issuance of Notice(s) of
Suspension/Disallowance, if warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. conduct verification, analysis, and validation of the balances of the Due
to NGAs accounts, determine the source agencies and the status of
programs, projects and activities for which funds were received and
maintain complete records of the same;
b. exhaust possible remedy to reconcile the balances of the account and
effect the necessary adjustments, as warranted; and
c. make representations with identified source agencies for the return of
unutilized balances.

Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor mandate the LGU
officials designated as Project-in-Charge to:
a. implement immediately the projects in accordance with the timeframe
provided for in the MOA to maximize the beneficial use of government
projects/program and the eventual return of unused funds, if any;
b. prepare the corresponding reports for each of the completed projects
and submit the same to the Accountant within one month from
completion; and
c. report to the Municipal Mayor any project/program which cannot be
immediately implemented for proper disposition or referral to the
Source Agency/ies.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a. the CE Technical Enterprises to promptly accomplish the delivery of the
121 non-motorized/ paddled banca, and ensure renewal and
supplemental of performance security pursuant to Section 39 of the
Revised IRR of R.A. 9184;
b. the PESO Manager and Municipal Agriculturist to closely monitor the
contractor’s fulfillment of the above recommendation.

In case of noncompliance, the contractor should refund the advance
payment for mobilization and undelivered billed items, the LGU should
terminate the contract, forfeit the performance security and further
recommend for blacklisting and perpetual disqualification from
participating in any public bidding as provided under Section 69 and
Appendix 17 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184; and
c. the Municipal Accountant to impose Liquidation Damages, equal to one-
tenth (1/10) of one percent (1%) of the cost of the delayed goods
scheduled for delivery for every day of delay until such items are
completely delivered and accepted by the Municipality.

Also, to protect the interest of the government and to prevent further loss and
wastage of government funds and resources, we recommended that the
following measures for future projects of similar nature be undertaken:
a. Strictly monitor implementation of all projects, whether contracts for
infrastructure, goods or consulting services, in accordance with the
approved plans and specifications within the time frame specified in the
contract agreement; and
b. Identify contracts which require appropriate management actions. If so,
impose liquidated damages and/or administrative sanctions particularly
termination of contracts and blacklisting of contractors for failure to
comply with their obligations in accordance with the provisions of
Sections 68 and 69, and Appendix 17 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to (i)
require the daily remittance by the collectors of the collections for the day, duly
supported with the Daily Statement of Collections and Accountable Forms
(DSCAF) to preclude the misuse of cash in their possession; and (ii) stop the
practice of weekly remittance/turn over by the collectors of collections to the
Municipal Treasurer, retaining collections more than the maximum
accountability.

We also recommended strict compliance with the pertinent rules and
regulations in handling collections and deposit provided in COA Circular No.
92-382, New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual, Volume I, and
the bonding requirement in Treasury Circular No. 02-2019.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer
to:
a. implement other tax collection mechanisms and strategies to encourage
delinquent taxpayers to settle their tax liabilities and increase RPT
collections of the Municipality;
b. prepare a certified list of all real property tax delinquencies which
remained uncollected or unpaid for at least one (1) year including reasons
for such non-collection; and
c. submit such list to the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) on or before December 31
of the year immediately succeeding the year in which the delinquencies
were incurred, and request for assistance from the SB in the enforcement
of the remedies for collection provided in RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer
and Municipal Accountant to:
a. submit to the Audit Team within five (5) days, the required lacking
documents enumerated above to prove regularity and legality of the
corresponding disbursements. This is without prejudice to the issuance
of Notice of Suspension and/ or Disallowance, if warranted;
b. ensure that all disbursement vouchers are properly supported with
complete documentation and in no case shall a disbursement be paid
without the necessary supporting documents; and
c. strictly observe the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184,
COA Circular 2012-001 and relevant/applicable GPPB Resolutions to
ensure adherence to the principles of transparency, competitiveness and
public monitoring.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the (i) Bids and Awards
Committee (BAC) require the winning bidder/contractor to submit
Construction Safety and Health Program approved by DOLE in compliance
DOLE DO No. 13 in the succeeding infrastructure projects; and ii) Municipal
Engineer to require inclusion of Occupational Safety and Health as separate
pay item in every infrastructure project implemented in compliance with
DPWH D.O. No. 56 s. 2005 dated April 20, 2005 and monitor the proper
implementation of the said program to ensure its compliance to with the
requirements of the DOLE and that workers are adequately protected against
occupational risks.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Rural Health Officer
and Vaccination Coordinator to:
a. Ensure the preparation and submission of Vaccination Arrival Report,
Property Transfer Report and RIS on all the received COVID-19
vaccines;
b. Submit a written justification on the following deficiencies: (1)
discrepancy of 6,304 doses of COVID-19 vaccines between the vaccines
received and administered excluding the returned vaccines reported in
the Incident Report; and (2) failure to monitor the receipt and use of
Ancillaries Supplies for the implementation of the LGU’s Vaccination
Program;
c. Monitor the distribution or use of COVID-19 vaccines in order to
accurately report inventory balances at any given period of time such as
use of stock cards by correctly maintaining the VORS, and
d. Develop distribution plan or strategy appropriate to their situation,
including inspection process prior to acceptance of vaccines and
timelines to avoid vaccine spoilage;
e. Strictly adhere to the preparation of the required reports and forms per
DOH Department Circular No. 2021-0439 dated September 15, 2021 and
DOH Administrative order No. 2022-0005 dated March 09, 2022, in
order to effectively and efficiently implement the COVID-19
Vaccination Program

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Accountant to (i) submit to the Audit Team the lacking
documents and a written explanation why the disbursement vouchers
were approved despite the deficiency; and (ii) review the completeness
of the supporting documents of the disbursement vouchers before
forwarding these to the Municipal Mayor for approval;
b. Municipal Health Officer to ensure completeness of distribution list and
the required documentation of events for future reference and proof in
validating the transactions.

These recommendations are without prejudice to the issuance of Notice(s) of
Suspension/Disallowance, if warranted.

We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to:
1. issue an Acknowledgment Receipt for In-Kind Donations for DRRM or his
authorized representative for the receipt of in-kind donations.
2. designate the office to receive all in-kind donations who will:
a. establish an inventory management system on receipt, distribution
and/ or utilization of in-kind donations,
b. prepare the Summary/ List of Donations Received, Distributed and
Balances every month or every quarter supported by
Acknowledgment Receipts, Proof of Receipt by and Distribution to
the Beneficiaries/ end-users, and Inventory of Remaining Balances,
and
c. submit to the Property Custodian copy of the Acknowledgment
Receipt for donated equipment and other tangible items which costs
₱15,000 or less and has a life of more than a year.
3. require the designated Property Custodian or direct the Municipal
Treasurer, in the absence of a duly appointed GSO to:
a. Maintain Stock Cards/Property Card for all donations in-kind
received and update regularly;
b. Maintain Registry of Donated Relief Goods for each kind of
inventory items;
c. Prepare Property Acknowledgment Receipt (PAR) for issuance of
82
donated PPEs and Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) for the issuances
of tangible items which costs ₱15,000.00 or less and have a life of
more than a year and are not considered as PPE to establish
accountability over them and furnish the Municipal Accountant with
 the PAR and ICS; and
d. require the departments responsible for the distribution of relief
goods and other supplies (MDRRMO, DSWDO and RHU) to submit
Proof of Receipt by and Distribution to the Beneficiaries/ end-users
to the Office referred in number 2;

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant summarize the data from
the documents obtained, compare records with Registry of Donated Relief
Goods maintained by the Property Custodian or by the designated MDRRMO
for check and balance, and accordingly disclose all necessary information in the
Notes to the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Human Resource and
Management Officer to:
a. review, assess and evaluate the existing manpower, in coordination with
the department heads, to prepare an updated Organizational
Structure/Staffing Pattern Plan so that the ideal number of plantilla
personnel will be established and submit a report with recommendations
thereon to the Local Chief Executive;
b. formulate uniform policies and guidelines to address the issue on the
necessity and propriety of hiring JOs, taking into consideration the
existing regular employees;
c. limit the hiring of personnel under Job Order contracts to those
extremely necessary and deploy only to offices that actually are in need
of their services;
d. require Job Orders to submit primary personal identification, undergo
interview and evaluate the performance of each to give assurance that
86
the risk identified above are addressed; and
e. incorporate in the contract the number of man hours, quantity and
duration of the work to be performed by the JO personnel and require
them to submit quantified accomplishment reports to support payments
of wages.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor designate a qualified employee as
SWM-Focal Person with the least responsibilities after conducting thorough
evaluation of their job requirements and send them to training programs that
will strengthen his/her capacity in managing the solid waste problems of the
Municipality or, likewise, consider hiring a Municipal Environment and
Natural Resources Officer (MENRO);

We also reiterated and recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the
SWM – Focal Person (i) expedite the processing of documentary requirements
for submission to the EMB in securing Environmental Compliance Certificate
for the immediate operation of the sanitary landfill; (ii) devise local ordinances
and strictly enforce implementation of the provisions of Section 21 and 22 of
RA 9003 on the mandatory segregation of solid wastes and carry out the
following:
a. strengthen information and education campaign activities by using LGU
website or social media to promote awareness and encourage
participants from the community in managing solid wastes;
b. establish close coordination with the barangay officials to ensure that
efforts to improve the SWM will reach the constituents at the barangay
level; and
c. strictly impose fines and penalties for any offence made or in violation
of Section 48 of RA 9003.

Lastly, we recommended that the MSWMB through SWM – Focal Person:
a. review every two years or as the need arises to ensure its sustainability,
viability, effectiveness and relevance in relation to local and
international developments in the field of solid waste management;
b. incorporate in the SWM Plan the conduct of necessary training for
SWM Personnel to ensure that solid wastes are properly handled and
that the risk of hazards to them and to the public are avoided or
minimized; and
c. provide complete personal protective equipment and paraphernalia to
protect the garbage collectors and other SWM Personnel from the
hazards of handling solid wastes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. ensure that all vehicles owned by the Municipality are promptly
registered as government vehicles with the LTO under the name of the
Municipality, include in the annual appropriate the payment for renewal
of registration of all vehicles;
b. registration be made within the period prescribed by the LTO to avoid
payment of penalties and other sanctions;
c. direct every Department Head or office to effect immediate renewal of
the eight (8) vehicles operating with invalid LTO registration; and
d. instruct the Property Custodian or Driver of the Motor Vehicle to stop
the operation/use of the vehicles with expired LTO registration.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, in the absence of a duly
appointed GSO:
a. Investigate, locate records and submit copies of registration documents
for the 23 motor vehicles;
b. Keep the records of registration for all LGU vehicles, monitor their
expiry dates and schedule the annual renewal of LTO registration in
coordination with the Property Custodian or Driver of the vehicle; and
c. Evaluate the any unserviceable vehicles for possible disposal; and in
coordination with the Municipal Accountant, follow the disposal
procedures unserviceable vehicles, pursuant to pertinent provisions in
COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, as the responsible officer,
ensure that all paid disbursement vouchers and the supporting documents are
properly stamped “PAID”, serially numbered and dates are indicated thereof.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure the
completeness of the supporting documents attached to the disbursement
voucher, and that the latter is properly accomplished before forwarding the
same to the LCE for approval.

Finally, we recommended that the LCE ensure strict compliance by the officials
concerned with the rules and regulations under COA Circular Nos. 92-389 and
78-79, and Volume II of NGAS Manual.

observations.managementAction:
Fast Tracked the updating of the Local Road Network data.    





















The LGU is still in the process of preparing the Certificates of Turn-Over to the barangays for those barangay roads constructed by the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE):

 Limit the hiring of Job Order workers to indispensable functions only and pay the prescribed daily minimum wage as prescribed by the Regional Tripartite Wages and Productivity Boards for Region VIII; and
observations.managementAction: Submitted proposal for wage increase to the Mayor.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

 Direct the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board to expedite the processing of documentary requirements and its submission to the Environmental Management Bureau (EMB) for the grant of Environmental Compliance Certificate (ECC)  for the immediate operation of the sanitary landfill of the LGU in CY 2020; and

 Require all Barangays to comply with the requirements on waste segregation and establishment of Materials Recovery Facility and provide assistance for its compliance as coordinated efforts in protecting the entire LGU residents from health hazards.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Sanitary Landfill plan is still being prepared and updated.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: 

require the submission of Fuel Consumption Reports and Driver’s Trip Tickets as basis for recording issuances/utilization of fuel; and 



comply strictly with the provisions of COA Circular No. 75-6 dated November 7, 1975 on fuel consumption.
observations.managementAction: Required the submission of all Fuel Consumption Reports and Driver’s Trip Tickets.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create the Inventory, Disposal and Appraisal Committee to initiate the disposal of all unserviceable and damaged properties of the LGU following the procedures outlined in COA Circular No. 89-296 to prevent further deterioration and exposure to theft of these properties.
observations.managementAction: An Inventory, Disposal and Appraisal Committee was created.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Chairman of the BAC and the BAC Secretariat submit copies of perfected contracts/purchase orders to the Auditor within five (5) days after execution/issuance thereof, to afford timely review and evaluation of the contracts.
observations.managementAction: Enforced submission of contracts and POs within the reglementary period

observations.recommendation: We recommended for the conduct of the physical inventory of all properties of the LGU by the Inventory Committee and for the preparation of the Report on the Physical Inventory of Property, Plants and Equipment. Reconciliation between the Inventory Report and the accounting records should be conducted by the Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee and for the former to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reflect an accurate balance of the PPE accounts.
observations.managementAction:
Physical count of PPE was done and reports have been submitted to the municipal accountant and reconciliation with accounting records is still on-going.

Necessary adjustments were made to the accounting records, subject to compliance of procedures for the disposal of all unserviceable PPEs.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, as head of the inventory committee, initiate the conduct of physical count of all inventory items within 30 days from receipt of the Annual Audit Report (AAR) for CY 2021 and to prepare and submit the Report on Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) to the Municipal Accountant for reconciliation purposes.  Henceforth, conduct the annual count of all inventory items of the Municipality.
We also recommended that the Supply Officer-Designate prepare the RIS upon distribution/issuance of the stocks to the end users and summarize all RIS in the monthly SSMI and submit to the Municipal Accountant for recording purposes.
Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant stop the practice of recording immediately to expense accounts the inventories received by the municipality. Instead, recognize the expenses based on the monthly SSMI, supported by duly approved RIS.
Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, prioritize the establishment of a Supply Office to ensure the safekeeping of goods delivered to the Municipality and which will greatly help in the proper management of the inventories of the municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Compute depreciation of PPEs over its estimated useful life and exclude residual value in the computation of depreciation for Road Networks account; and
Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reflect in the books the correct carrying amounts of PPEs.
Review at all times the correctness of the lapsing schedule as the basis in recording their monthly depreciation expense.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the adjusting entries for the deficiencies noted and henceforth, ensure that all transactions are properly recorded in their appropriate accounts.

We recommended that the BAC Chairman should submit the lacking supporting documents to the Audit Team for further evaluation within 3 days from receipt of this AOM. This is without prejudice to the issuance of the Notice of Suspension/Disallowance, if warranted.
Henceforth, adhere to the bidding process prescribed under RA 9184 and its RIRR, including the proper dissemination of bid opportunities and its result, the conduct of a strict evaluation of bids until the post qualification activity and the submission of contract documents with complete supporting documents.

We recommended that:
the Municipal Mayor as Chairman of the Local Development Council (LDC) convene the council and reevaluate the Municipality’s priority PPAs and its capability to implement them with regard to the Municipality’s existing finances so as to come-up with a feasible and achievable Annual Investment Plan.
the Offices of the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development, being the implementing offices of the projects under the 20% DF, prepare the engineering design and cost estimate during the pre-procurement phase in order to accelerate the conduct of the procurement and implementation of these projects; and
the Municipal Engineer inform the contractor to submit the documents to support its claim for two completed projects and one partially completed project to the Municipal Budget Officer for recording in the SAAOb and other financial reports for CY 2021.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the Municipal DRRM Council, through the MDRRM Officer:
Ensure that the PPAs in the Municipal DRRMFIP are specific and aligned with the National DRRM Plan and the corresponding performance indicators are definite, measurable, and achievable; and
Monitor and evaluate the PPAs incorporated in the MDRRMFIP so as to avoid duplication of programs and identify projects that can address the current state of calamity or any calamity that may possibly arise.  Revise the Plan, if necessary and indicate specific timeframe of implementation.

To ensure the efficient use of the MDRRM Fund, we recommended that the MDRRM Officer re-evaluate the municipality’s capability in disaster preparedness in order that situation and living conditions of the people in the municipality stricken by calamities maybe normalize as quickly as possible in the shortest possible time.
We also recommended that the MDRRM Officer, in order to fast track   repacking of relief goods, make use of volunteers and members of the Barangay DRRM Councils to ensure the timely distribution of relief goods to calamity victims.
Further, we recommended that the DRRM Officer should maintain standby relief goods especially during the typhoon season to provide timely response to calamity victims.

We recommended that the BAC Chairman conduct the procurement of fuel per approve consolidated Annual Procurement Plan (APP) and not per office budget allocation to avoid constraints on the release of fuel and for the monitoring/identification of the utilization for fuel.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as head of the agency, to instruct all department heads with fuel allotment to strictly monitor their fuel consumption in accordance with their approved budget and to utilize the fuel solely and strictly for its intended purpose.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
as head of the agency, require the designated Supply Officer to prepare the monthly report of fuel consumption and to submit the reports to the Audit Team for audit purposes.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct all driver personnel of the municipality to properly accomplish the Driver’s Trip Tickets and prepare the report of official travel on a monthly basis and to submit the same to the Supply Officer for the preparation of the monthly report of fuel consumption.

1.5 We recommended that the Provincial Engineer:
a.) inspect the projects listed in Table 2 which are reported in the books of
the Province as ongoing in the Construction in Progress – Infrastructure
Asset account as of December 31, 2021;
b.) prepare a report as to the status of each project duly certified to as
accurate and correct; and
c.) submit the said report to the Provincial Accountant for the appropriate
adjustment in the books, where necessary.

1.6 We further recommended that the Provincial Accountant prepare an
adjusting entry to record in the appropriate Infrastructure Asset account the
completed projects totaling ₱41,296,461.06 and compute its corresponding
depreciation from their completion date to reflect the correct carrying
values; and adjust the Construction in Progress - Infrastructure Asset
account based on the report of the Provincial Engineer on the projects listed
in Table 2.

2.13 We recommended that the Provincial Accountant:
a) Henceforth, record procurement of properties upon receipt and
acceptance, using the appropriate account classifications in the books of
accounts; and
b) derecognize from the books the ten units Mini Rescue Vehicle
(Ambulance) totaling ₱9,500,000.00 donated to various LGUs in order to
reflect the correct balances of PPE and Equity accounts.

3.11 We recommended that:
a) Henceforth, the Provincial SWD Officer update their records on
distribution of relief goods after every relief operation and submit at the
end of the month the report of distribution supported with the RIS signed
by the recipients;
b) the PGS Officer consolidate in the SSMI the RIS received from the
Provincial SWD Officer and submit the same to the Provincial
Accountant to account for the 1,579 bags of commercial rice already
distributed; also, monitor the distribution of the other remaining stocks
by requiring from the Provincial SWD Officer the pertinent reports; and
c) the Provincial Accountant record the total cost of sacks of rice already
distributed based on the SSMI submitted by the PGS Office, duly
supported with the RIS of the recipient LGUs in order to reflect the
equivalent amount of expenses and the correct balance of the Inventory
account.

4.10 We recommended that the Provincial Governor and the members of the
Local Development Council, through the Provincial Planning and
Development Officer, indicate the specific name of the project, its exact
location and estimated cost of each individual project to be funded from the
20% DF, both current and continuing appropriations, during the
preparation of the AIP based on the priority needs of the LGUs in the
Province. Otherwise, obtain prior approval from the Sangguniang
Panlalawigan and revise the AIP accordingly, before implementing a specific
project to avoid unauthorized disbursements and disallowances in audit.

5.12 We recommended that in the granting of financial assistance to NGOs/POs,
the management shall strictly adhere to the procedures and requirements
prescribed in COA Circular No. 2007-001 and Section 36, Volume IV of RA
No. 7160.

5.13 We also recommended that the Provincial Accountant issue demand letters
to all NGOs/POs with unliquidated financial assistance and process claims
for additional financial assistance only if the previous ones are already fully
accounted.

6.7 We recommended that the Provincial Governor sign the Contract Agreement
and the NTP of future projects within the reglementary period to ensure
timely implementation of projects.

7.6 We recommended that the Provincial Governor shall ensure that the CSHP
attached to the contract is approved by the DOLE before signing the contract
agreements, as required under RA No. 9184 and its 2016 Revised IRR.

8.11 We recommended that the Chiefs of Hospital of DMBVMH and LPH as well
as the Head of Isolation Facilities of the Leyte Provincial Mega Isolation and
LAC, and other signatories of the payrolls, refund the amount of SRA paid to
the consultants and PNP personnel without legal basis.

8.12 We also recommended that the Provincial Accountant require the Chief of
Hospital of WLPH, through the City Government of Baybay, to submit the
lacking documents to substantiate the payment of AHDP and SRA.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the:
a. Provincial Accountant and Provincial Treasurer, to reconcile their
records pertaining to Cash in Bank- Local Currency, Current Account
particularly on the reconciling items which remained unrecorded for
several years;
b. Provincial Treasurer, to facilitate the timely submission of copies of RCI
supported with copies of cancelled checks and original copies of DVs to
the accounting section, for the preparation of the necessary JEV;
c. Provincial Accountant, to revert the stale checks to Cash in Bank,
payable and surplus accounts; and to recognize the proper payable/
liability account using the following entry:
Cash in Bank- Local Currency, Current Account xx
Appropriate Payable/ Liability account xx
d. Provincial Accountant, to record paid DVs presented as debits in the BS,
using the following entry:
Accumulated Surplus/ (Deficit) or Appropriate Payable/ Liability account xx
Cash in Bank- Local Currency, Current Account xx

We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the:
a. Inventory Committee, to complete the conduct of physical inventory
of all PPEs and submit the RPCPPE not later than January 31, 2023;
and
b. PGSO and PAO, to reconcile the balances of the RPCPPE with the
property and the accounting records.

We recommended that the Provincial Accountant prepare the following
entries:
To adjust the balance of the affected liability, income and expense accounts -
Account Code Account Title Debit Credit
2-04-01-010 Trust Liabilities P56,859,242.00
2-04-01-020 Trust Liabilities-Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Fund 1,000,000.00
2-02-01-050 Due to National Government Agencies P55,859,242.00
To recognize the unrecorded cash donations as of December 31, 2021 -
Account Code Account Title Debit Credit
1-01-01-010 Cash-Local Treasury P5,590,000.00
2-04-01-020 Trust Liabilities-Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Fund P5,590,000.00

We recommended that the Provincial Accountant:
a. Coordinate with the Municipal Accountants and Municipal Treasurers
concerned to facilitate reconciliation of the provincial records with those
of the municipalities concerned;
b. Send billing statements to the municipalities for the unremitted shares
from RPT and Special Education Tax collections;
c. Serve demand letters to municipalities concerned and NGOs/POs for the
immediate liquidation of fund transfers particularly the long
outstanding accounts;
d. Analyze the accounts that are dormant, long outstanding, and with
abnormal balances and take the necessary actions for their proper
disposition; and
e. Henceforth, conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and
validation of the existence of the receivables

In view of the foregoing observations we hereby reiterate our
recommendation that the Provincial Governor require the:
a. Inventory Committee, PGSO and PAO including the implementing
departments such as PSWDO and PHSO to conduct complete physical
count of inventories for distribution to be incorporated in the RPCI
and to reconcile with the property and accounting records;
b. Weekly submission of RIS by the PSWDO and PHSO to PGSO for
consolidation and recording in property cards and submission of SSMI
to PAO for the recording of the issuance and distribution;
c. PGSO to instruct all the suppliers to deliver the procured items
directly to them; and
d. Provincial Accountant to prepare JEV for the recording of supplies
expense based on the SSMI submitted by PGSO in order to present the
actual balance of Inventories for Distribution in the financial
statements.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor make representation with
the NGAs concerned to secure copies of the MOAs covering the funds
received to facilitate the implementation of the intended projects.

We recommended that the:
a. Department Heads of PEO, PAGSO, PVet and PENRMO, monitor and
fast-track the implementation of all approved PPAs in the Annual
Investment Plan (AIP) so that any cause of suspensions or problems that
would delay the project implementation will immediately be addressed
and the desired benefits could promptly be delivered to the constituents;
and
b. Planning and Development Officer re-assess the unimplemented PPAs to
ascertain the viability of implementation, otherwise realign and
reprogram for more viable and significant PPAs in accordance with the
socio-economic development and environmental management.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the:
1. Provincial DRRM Office Head, to ensure full utilization of funds and
implementation of approved PPAs incorporated in the Local DRRM
Investment Plan and AIP;
2. PEO Head, to implement all the DRRM projects under the Special Trust
Fund programmed in the previous years; and
3. PAGSO Head, to implement the PPAs as disaster response to the effects
of Typhoon Odette.

We recommended that the:
1. Designated Collecting Officer prepare an accurate Report of Collections
and Deposits (RCD) for DRRM and submit to the Accounting Unit for
recording in the Cash Receipts Journal (CRJ) as Trust Liabilities -
Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund and furnish a copy of
the RCD to the Budget Office as basis in the preparation of the RCDU;
2. PBO prepare and maintain the RCDU per donor and per purpose and
updated regularly. Also, the PBO should liquidate the amount of
P300,000.00; and
3. Project in charge of DRRM prepare the Cash Donation Utilization
Request (CDUR) to reflect charges and utilization of the fund with
original copy to be attached to the DV.

In order to establish the regularity of the grant of the AHDP/SRA, we
recommended that the Provincial Governor require the Provincial
Accountant to immediately submit the lacking supporting documents for the
proper disposition in audit of the disbursement. Henceforth, only
disbursement vouchers/payrolls with complete supporting documents should
be processed for payment.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the:
1. Medical Center Chief of SOYMPH and the Provincial Health Officer II,
to take action to make the Auto Hematology Analyzer operational;
2. The Medical Technologist III, Head of the Laboratory Department, to
establish procedural controls to avoid the laboratory from running out of
essential medical supplies for hospital operation; and
3. The Administrative Officer, to check on the level of supplies and
inventory needed and their availability in the hospital operation as this
is one of his responsibilities.

We recommended that the:
1. PSWDO a) coordinate with the accounting office on the reconciliation of
records for the receipt and disposition of donations in kind; b) prepare
the Issuance Form for In-Kind Donations, Inventory List of Donated
Items for DRRM based on the updated Stock Cards and Property
Cards, Acknowledgment Receipts, and Registry of Donated Relief Goods
for DRRM containing complete significant information such as the unit
cost, reference numbers/control numbers, condition of the items, and
the like as required in COA Circular 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014;
2. PGSO a) coordinate with the property custodian and secure all documents
to warrant the ownership of the donated PPE; and b) issue the
Acknowledgment Receipt for Equipment on the custodian of the
properties; and
3. Provincial Accountant to record in the agency books the donated PPE
based on the declared value in the deed of donation or the bill of
lading/airway bill/parcel notice and other related documents. The fair
market value/appraised value shall be used in the absence of declared
value

We recommended that the Designated Property/Supply Officer:
1. Prepare and maintain a Summary/List of donated vaccines received,
distributed and balances based on the updated stock cards;
2. Prepare and maintain complete and duly signed RISs to document all
issuances/distribution of vaccines; and
3. Prepare acknowledgement receipts for in-kind donations as proof of receipt
and ARE for equipment issued to the recipient or end user duly signed and
dated as to the issuance.

observations.managementAction: The municipal accountant already made several adjustments to the account balances under the Due to NGAs and made the necessary adjusting entries. Total balance of the Due to NGAs as of December 31, 2021 is P44,635,888.82.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant determine the cost of all purchased medicines and medical supplies actually on hand as at December 31, 2020 and restore the same to the Inventories account in the books of the LGU. 	
The amount shall represent the beginning balance of Medical, Dental and Laboratory Supplies Inventory as of January 1, 2021. Adjusting entries shall be made to correct the overstatement of the Expense accounts in the financial statements.

We also recommended that the General Services Officer maintain a Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM and record the donated medicines and medical supplies actually on hand as at December 31, 2020.


For similar transactions in the future, we recommended that:

The Municipal Accountant should record the medicines as Inventories upon purchase, and only recognize as expense medicines which were actually consumed on the basis of the Report of Supplies and Materials Issued submitted to him 	within five days after the end of the month;

The Municipal Health Officer should notify immediately the Municipal Accountant all donations directly delivered to the RHU from other national or private agencies for recording in the books of accounts; and,

The Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances must be submitted supported with the acknowledgment receipts of the donations-in kind, proof of receipt by and distribution to the beneficiaries, not necessarily in the required form, and, the inventory of remaining undistributed items, if any.

We recommended that the BAC and its Secretariat submit justification on the deficiencies noted for Auditor’s evaluation. 

For similar transaction in the future, we recommended that the BAC and its Secretariat should comply substantially the requirement of the laws, rules and regulations applicable in the circumstances to avoid audit suspension.

We recommended that the MDRRM Officer submit the lacking supporting documents to avoid audit disallowance and ensure submission of complete supporting documents for every transaction to establish its propriety and validity.

We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer double check all entries in the Record of Appropriation and Obligations as it is crucial in determining the balances of appropriations and ultimately the amount to be transferred to the Trust Fund for unexpended balance of the LDRRMF.
observations.managementAction: The municipal accountant has already transferred the unexpended balance of P128,790.00 as of March 2021 per GJ # 034.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Engineer submit the approved Variation Orders or Suspension Orders, if any, for the projects that incurred delays of completion, otherwise, liquidated damages should be imposed and collected from the contractor.

Also, the Municipal Engineer should submit justification for contracts with delay that exceed a time duration equivalent to ten percent (10%) of the specified contract time which are not rescinded and the contractor’s performance security forfeited and award the same to a qualified contractor through negotiated contract.

Further, the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant should see to it that all infrastructure contracts of the LGU incurring delays in project completion be imposed with liquidated damages equivalent to 1/10 of one percent of the cost of the unfinished portion of the project for each day of delay.

Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer should strictly adhere to the project duration stipulated per contract and should conduct inspection of work accomplishment or Punch Listing on the date the projects are to be completed as agreed in order to determine cost of unfinished portion of the project, if any.
observations.managementAction: Liquidated damages were imposed and collected by the LGU on delayed completion of projects.

observations.recommendation:
1.13 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue a directive to the Municipal
Engineer, Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer -Designate
to execute their required tasks as enumerated in COA Circular No. 2015-008,
complete and update the recognition of all road networks within the
jurisdiction of LGU-Leyte.

1.14 We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer:
1.14.1. Complete the survey of road networks under the LGU’s jurisdiction
and provide the Accountant and the GSO with the complete description
and cost segregation of each component of the municipal roads. For
road components with no available cost, compute the depreciated
replacement cost using the formula prescribed in the Circular;
1.14.2. Determine the cost or depreciated replacement cost of demolished
pavements affected by road re-blocking projects undertaken by the
LGU and submit the said data to the Municipal Accountant for de-
recognition in the books;
1.14.3. Prepare the Certificate of Turn-Over of all barangay roads previously
constructed by the LGU for acceptance by the respective Punong
Barangay with information of its current appraised value for recording
in Barangay books of accounts, copy furnished the Municipal
Accountant, as basis of de-recognition in the LGU books; and
1.14.4. Assist the GSO in the preparation of the Road Map covering all roads
within the jurisdiction of LGU-Leyte, duly identified and labeled.

1.15 We further recommended that the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal
Accountant update, tally and harmonize their respective records so that the
complete description and cost segregation of each road component of the road
networks in the LGU will support the amounts in the books.

1.16 Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant: (1) record the
different components of the municipal roads, including the Road Lot
components; and (2) maintain property ledger cards for each municipal road
with complete description and cost segregation of its components based on the
Municipal Engineer’s report.

2.8. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
2.8.1 Prepare an accounting entry to write-off from the books the three DBP
bank accounts which have been confirmed closed by the bank; and
2.8.2 Trace the previous years’ accounting and treasury records to verify the
discrepancies between book and bank balances and record the
necessary adjustments in the books.

3.8. We recommended again that the Municipal Treasurer:
3.8.1 Submit to the Municipal Accountant the monthly Billing Statements of
water consumers;
3.8.2 Collect on the specified dates all amounts due from water
consumers;
3.8.3 Prepare monthly a report that summarizes unpaid accounts and the
delinquent payees and submit to the Municipal Accountant;
3.8.4 Prepare Demand Letters monthly to all delinquent water consumers for
signature of the Mayor; and
3.8.5 Issue Notices of Disconnection to water consumers who fail to adhere
with the Demand Letters.

3.9. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant record in the books the
receivables from Waterworks System services based on the billing statements
submitted by the Municipal Treasurer and provide for the necessary
allowance for doubtful accounts.

4.10. We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer update the Taxpayer’s Index
Card and reconcile the same with the records of the Municipal Assessors and
submit the Taxpayer’s Index Card to the Municipal Accountant at the start of
each year as basis in recording RPT Receivables.

4.11. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant record correctly the
collections from payments of Real Property Taxes, and prepare the necessary
accounting entries to adjust the overstatement of Real Property Tax/Special
Education Tax Receivable accounts and other related accounts in the financial
statements.

5.13 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor convene the Municipal
Development Council, so as to review the projects under the 20% Development
Fund Continuing Appropriations and identify which should be prioritized and
included in the Annual Investment Plan.

5.14 We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer review the balances
of the Continuing Appropriations in the preparation of the annual budget so
that any amount no longer needed can be reverted to the Unappropriated
Surplus of the LGU or re-appropriated for other development projects.

5.15 We further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer confirm the
availability of cash from the Continuing Appropriations of the 20%
Development Fund and monitor closely its utilization to ensure that projects
identified in the AIP are adequately funded.

6.11 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue a directive to the Municipal
Agriculture Officer, Municipal Engineer, BAC Chairman and other LGU
personnel involved in the implementation of programs/projects funded by the
National Government, to procure, distribute and implement the requirements
and purpose of the programs/projects within the timeframe prescribed in the
MOA, submit the status reports monthly for the Mayor’s monitoring and
utilize the funds in accordance with its purpose.

6.12 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the
Disbursement Vouchers for the immediate return of the funds totaling
P2,852,765.00 to the national government through the Bureau of Treasury.

7.12 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
7.12.1 Prepare a quarterly schedule on the composition of the Due From LGU
account to identify which LGU has not submitted its Utilization Report;
7.12.2 Send demand letters to Punong Barangays and Barangay Treasurers
of recipient Barangays for the submission of Utilization Reports;
7.12.3 Provide the Municipal Mayor with the list of LGUs who failed to
submit Utilization Reports despite the demand as basis in the
deferment of additional fund transfer; and
7.12.4 Record immediately the liquidation of fund transfers for the KALAHI
projects with available DVs and other appropriate documents to
support utilization, and demand for the refund of any unutilized
amounts.

7.13 We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
7.13.1 Send demand letters to the Barangay Officials concerned to submit
certification under oath that documents pertaining to projects
implemented from fund transfers were destroyed/lost to typhoon
Yolanda or other fortuitous events, if warranted;
7.13.2 File a request with COA for authority to write off dormant
unliquidated fund transfers which have been non-moving for ten years
or more pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19,
2016; and
7.13.3 Sign a Memorandum of Agreement when funds are transferred to
barangays, NGOs and other LGUs, with specific provisions on the
timelines of implementation and liquidation, among others.

8.6 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, Municipal Budget Officer and
the MDRRMO utilize the LDRRMF solely and strictly, for disaster-related
activities as mandated in the NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 and
NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014.

9.11 We recommended that the HRMO update and reconcile the records of all
LGU-Leyte employees with the GSIS records to confirm any under-remittance
of premium contributions and loan amortizations. Also, provide the GSIS
immediately with appropriate documents on changes of employees’ salaries so
that GSIS billings will be accurate.

9.12 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the HRMO resolve
the prior years’ balances on GSIS premium contributions and loan
amortizations before December 31, 2020 so that all LGU-Leyte employees can
fully avail of the GSIS benefits and avoid additional loan interests and
surcharges.

10.9 We recommended that the HRMO initiate the preparation of the DVs and
supporting documents for the immediate remittance to the HDMF the
premium contributions of the employees and Job Order workers and submit
the same to the Municipal Accountant for confirmation and processing.

10.10 We also recommended that the HRMO and Municipal Accountant resolve the
discrepancies in the LGU books of accounts on the negative balance of loan
amortization of employees to confirm that the amount paid for the employees’
loans were not taken from LGU funds and to reconcile monthly their records
to avoid accumulation of variances.

11.13 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE):
11.13.1 Limit the hiring of Job Order workers to indispensable functions only
and pay the prescribed daily minimum wage as prescribed by the
Regional Tripartite Wages and Productivity Boards for Region VIII;
and
11.13.2 Refrain from hiring Job Orders who have reached the compulsory
retirement age.

12.7 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
12.7.1 Direct the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board to expedite the
processing of documentary requirements and its submission to the
Environmental Management Bureau (EMB) for the grant of
Environmental Compliance Certificate (ECC) for the immediate
operation of the sanitary landfill of the LGU in CY 2020; and
12.7.2 Require all Barangays to comply with the requirements on waste
segregation and establishment of Materials Recovery Facility and
provide assistance for its compliance as coordinated efforts in
protecting the entire LGU residents from health hazards.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: (1) trace the exact date of acquisition of the infrastructure assets acquired in CY 2016 and CY 2017; (2) compute the depreciation of the infrastructure asset except for the road lot component of Road Network, and; (3) record the necessary adjustments in order to reflect in the books the correct carrying value of its Infrastructure Assets.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record subsequent road projects in the Road Network account and prepare Journal Entry Voucher to correct the projects erroneously classified in other accounts to Road Network account.
observations.managementAction:
Ongoing reclassification of assets to proper accounts to Road Network account.
Journal entries were made to reclassify the entries

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: (5) record purchase of fuel, oil and lubricant in the Inventory account not as outright expense; (6) require the submission of Fuel Consumption Reports and Driver’s Trip Tickets as basis for recording issuances/utilization of fuel; and (7) comply strictly with the provisions of COA Circular No. 75-6 dated November 7, 1975 on fuel consumption.

(26) Certify in the DV for each grant of cash advance that the previous cash advances of the claimant have been liquidated and accounted for; (27) grant cash advances at an amount sufficient for the purpose to avoid excess claims.
